Notes.—ED.]—BOSWELL: These four speeches were probably all designed for prose. What verse can be made out of line 114?

107.play: GLOUCESTER: Her husband, knave: wouldst thou betray me?
notes: would’st . . . me] 107. That is, would you make me say openly what might be considered ‘treason’? Perhaps Richard means to imply that he himself is also as guilty as the King.—ED.

108.play: BRAKENBURY: I beseech your grace to pardon me, and withal

109.play: Forbear your conference with the noble duke.

110.play: CLARENCE: We know thy charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.
notes: We . . . obey] 110. SEYMOUR: I cannot but suspect that Richard’s remark was suggested by words different from those uttered by Clarence, who, in the meekness of his loyalty, might naturally have said: ‘We’re the king’s subjects and we will obey.’

111.play: GLOUCESTER: We are the queen's abjects, and must obey.
notes: abjects] 111. JOHNSON: That is, not the queen’s subjects, whom she might protect, but her ‘abjects,’ whom she drives away.—MONCK MASON: I cannot approve of Johnson’s explanation. Gloucester forms a substantive from the adjective abject, and uses it to express a lower degree of submission than is implied by the word ‘subject,’ which otherwise he would naturally have made use of. The ‘Queen’s abjects’ means the most servile of her subjects, who must, of course, obey all her commands; which would not be the case of those whom she had driven away from her. In a preceding passage Gloucester says of Shore’s wife—‘I think, it is our way, If we will keep in favour with the king To be her men and wear her livery’; the idea is the same in both places, though the expression differs. In Jonson’s Every man out of his Humour Puntarvolo says to Swift: ‘I’ll make thee stoop, thou abject.’—STEEVENS: This substantive was not of Shakespeare’s formation. We meet with it in Psalm xxxv, 15: ‘—yea, the very abjects came together against me unawares.’ [MURRAY (N. E. D.) gives two examples of ‘abject,’ in the sense of an outcast, earlier than that quoted by Steevens, but does not note the present line as an example of the meaning, correctly interpreted, as I think, by Mason. For a somewhat similar use of ‘abject’ in opposition to subject compare: ‘I accompt all those abiects, that be not hir [Elizabeth’s] subjectes,’ Lyly, Euphues Glass for Europe.—p. 208, ed. Bond.—Ed.]

112.play: Brother, farewell: I will unto the king;
notes: I will vnto the King] 112. For examples of ‘ellipsis of verb of motion before a preposition or adverb,’ see ABBOTT, § 405.

113.play: And whatsoever you will employ me in,

114.play: Were it to call King Edward's widow sister,
notes: Widdow] 114. JOHNSON: This is a very covert and subtle manner of insinuating treason. By slipping, as it were casually, ‘widow’ into the place of wife, he tempts Clarence with an oblique proposal to kill the king. [Did Johnson forget that he himself had explained ‘widow,’ line 88 above, as referring to the

115.play: I will perform it to enfranchise you.
notes: enfranchise] 115. WRIGHT: That is, to set you at liberty. The word is still used in a technical sense in reference to copyhold land, or to persons who have no vote as citizens; but in its literal meaning it is not employed, though it frequently occurs in Shakespeare.

116.play: Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood

117.play: Touches me deeper than you can imagine.
notes: Touches me deeper] 117. TAWNEY: There is, no doubt, an intentional ambiguity in the phrase. Richard intends Clarence to take it in the sense, ‘afflicts me more profoundly,’ but it may also mean ‘concerns me more’; probably in the sense, ‘I had more hand in it than you imagine.’

118.play: CLARENCE: I know it pleaseth neither of us well.

119.play: GLOUCESTER: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long;
notes: lye for you] 119. I. REED: That is, be imprisoned in your stead. To ‘lie’ was anciently to reside, as appears by many examples.—[There is here doubtless a play upon the word, as in Sir Henry Wotton’s definition: ‘An ambassador is an honest man sent to lie abroad for the commonwealth.’—Ed.] [Note: The corresponding line is missing in the provided text, but contextually follows line 119.]
I must] 121. STEEVENS: Alluding to the proverb: ‘Patience perforce is medicine for a mad dog.’ [Ray’s Proverbs, p. 100, ed. 1815.—ED.]

123.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return.

124.play: Simple, plain Clarence! I do love thee so,

125.play: That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven,
notes: I will ... to Heaven] 125. BOAS (p. 151): This light-hearted mirth in a fratricide appalls us, but to Richard the moral aspect of the situation never presents itself: he is simply tickled by its irresistible humor.—[See note by COURTENAY, l. 37 supra.]

126.play: If heaven will take the present at our hands.

127.play: But who comes here? the new-deliver'd Hastings?

129.play: HASTINGS: Good time of day unto my gracious lord!
notes: Good time of day] 129. WRIGHT: A common mode of salutation, which appears in a fuller form in 2 Henry IV: I, ii, 107: ‘God give your lordship good time of day.’

130.play: GLOUCESTER: As much unto my good lord chamberlain!

131.play: Well are you welcome to the open air.

132.play: How hath your lordship brook'd imprisonment?

133.play: HASTINGS: With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must:

134.play: But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks

135.play: That were the cause of my imprisonment.

136.play: GLOUCESTER: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too;

137.play: For they that were your enemies are his,

138.play: And have prevail'd as much on him as you.
notes: prevail’d] 138. For other examples where ‘prevail’ means to overcome, see SCHMIDT (Lex.).

139.play: HASTINGS: More pity that the eagle should be mew'd,
notes: Eagles] 139. The Ff reading is, I am inclined to think, the correct one, since the other two nouns, ‘Kites’ and ‘Buzzards,’ are plural. If the Qq were composed from dictation, it would be almost impossible to distinguish ‘Eagle should,’ from ‘Eagles should.’ — Ed.
mew’d] 139. See note, l. 43 supra.

140.play: While kites and buzzards prey at liberty.
notes: play] 140. It would be, perhaps, difficult to find a single example wherein Rowe follows any text other than that of the Ff; it is not strange, therefore, that he here prints what is evidently a compositor’s error, either of eye or ear. With Pope the case is, however, different; many Qq readings which he adopts, without comment, are not as obviously correct as the present one. See Text. Notes. — Ed.

142.play: HASTINGS: No news so bad abroad as this at home;

143.play: The King is sickly, weak and melancholy,

144.play: And his physicians fear him mightily.
notes: feare] 144. For examples of the ‘omission of the preposition after certain verbs which may be regarded as transitive,’ see ABBOTT, § 200.

145.play: GLOUCESTER: Now, by Saint Paul, this news is bad indeed.
notes: S.Iohn] 145. That the Qq is the correct reading may be seen by comparing

146.play: O, he hath kept an evil diet long,
notes: an euill Diet] 146. ‘They thinke that he long time in king Edward’s life fore-thought to be king; in case that the king his brother (whose life he looked that euill diet should shorten) should happen to deceasse while his children were yoong.’ —Holinshed.—WRIGHT: ‘Diet’ here denotes generally mode of life.

147.play: And overmuch consumed his royal person:

148.play: 'Tis very grievous to be thought upon.

149.play: What, is he in his bed?

151.play: GLOUCESTER: Go you before, and I will follow you.

153.play: He cannot live, I hope; and must not die

154.play: Till George be pack'd with post-horse up to heaven.
notes: post-horse] 154. SINGER (Sh. Vind., p. 165): [Collier’s MS corrector’s change] Of ‘post-horse’ to posthaste, we may allow to be an admissible correction of a probable misprint, and therefore possibly right.—DYCE: I believe the old text is right; ‘with post-horse’—meaning with the speediest possible conveyance.

155.play: I'll in, to urge his hatred more to Clarence,

156.play: With lies well steel'd with weighty arguments;

157.play: And, if I fall not in my deep intent,

158.play: Clarence hath not another day to live:

159.play: Which done, God take King Edward to his mercy,

160.play: And leave the world for me to bustle in!
notes: bussle] 160. TAWNEY: The expression is an instance of rhetorical understatement. Richard intended to display a mischievous activity.

161.play: For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter.
notes: Warwickes yongest daughter] 161. MALONE: Lady Anne, the widow of Edward Prince of Wales. [See Dram. Person. s. v. Anne, l: 39.]—VAUGHAN (iii, 10): This line possesses an interest as contrasting the historical knowledge of Shakespeare writing Richard III., with that of the poet who composed 3 Henry VI. In that play the wife of Prince Edward, son of Henry VI., is twice erroneously

162.play: What though I kill'd her husband and her father?
notes: I kill’d her Husband] 162. ‘Sir Richard Crofts... brought forth his prisoner prince Edward, ... whom when king Edward had aduised, he demanded of him, how he durst so presumptuoulsie enter into his realme with banner displaied? Wherento the prince boldlie answered, saieng: “To recouer my fathers kingdome & heritage, from his father and grandfather to him, and from him after him to me, lineallie descended.” At which words king Edward said nothing, but with his hand thrust him from him, or (as some saie) stroke him with his gantlet; whom incontinentlie, George duke of Clarence, Richard duke of Glocester, Thomas Greie marqueese Dorcet, and William lord Hastings, that stood by, suddenlie murthered.’—Holinshed, Edward IV.—Walpole (p. 6): A cotemporary names the king’s servauts as perpetrators of this murder: Is not that more probable than that the king’s own brothers should have dipped their hands in so foul an assassination? Richard, in particular, is allowed on all hands to have been a brave and martial prince: he had great share in the victory at Tewkesbury.... Such men may be carried by ambition to command the execution of those who stand in their way, but are not likely to lend their hand, in cold blood, to a base and, to themselves, useless assassination. How did it import Richard in what manner the young prince was put to death? If he had so early planned the ambitious designs ascribed to him, he might have trusted his brother Edward, so much more immediately concerned, that the young prince would not be spared. If those views did not, as is probable, take root in his heart till long afterwards, what interest had Richard to murder an unhappy young prince? This crime therefore was so unnecessary, and is so far from being established by any authority, that he deserves to be entirely acquitted of it.—Gardiner (p. 16): If this was Richard’s first heinous crime, it was probably one in which he was only an accessory, or in which, if a principal actor, he received great encouragement from those about him. On the other hand, to suppose him altogether guiltless in this matter is a great violation of all reasonable probability. For however feeble may be the direct evidence of his complicity, it would be absurd to suppose that he either disapproved the act, or was greatly shocked at it.—Legge (i, 108): Carte, whom Horace Walpole describes as ‘one of the few modern historians who seem not to have swallowed implicitly all the vulgar tales propagated by the Lancastrians to blacken the house of York,’ distinctly says that the murder was perpetrated by Dorset and Hastings (History of England, ii, 190). If the fact of the murder could be established, they were certainly the most likely agents. What appears least likely is that Richard, the youngest of the four implicated, a lad of eighteen, described as good-natured and obsequious, would, in the presence of his elders, to whom he had always shown deference, execute the royal vengeance and in so doing claim precedence over them. And this is confirmed by Buck, who says [p. 81] he had seen ‘in a faithfull Manuscript Chronicle of those times, that the Duke of Glouester onely of all the great persons, stood still and drew not his sword.’ Such an attitude is precisely what we should expect from the young chivalrous knight, who, however willing to strike

her Father] 162. WRIGHT: In the battle of Barnet, where Warwick was slain, Richard commanded the vanguard of his brother’s army, but the death of Warwick is attributed to one of Edward’s soldiers. ‘He lept upon a horse to flie, and comming into a wood where was no passage, one of king Edwards men came to him, killed him, and spoiled him to the naked skin.’—Holinshed, p. 685.—TAWNEY: I believe ‘Father’ refers to Henry VI. The word is used in the sense of father-in-law in I, iii, 144, and in many other passages.

163.play: The readiest way to make the wench amends

164.play: Is to become her husband and her father:

165.play: The which will I; not all so much for love
notes: The which] 165. See ABBOTT, § 270.

not . . . for love] 165. JESSE (Memor. of Richard III., etc., p. 55): May it not have been at Middleham [the establishment of Warwick] in the days of their childhood, that Richard was first inspired by that memorable passion which was destined to triumph over all human opposition, even when Anne Neville had become the betrothed, if not the bride, of another, and which was eventually rewarded by her becoming his wife and queen? [Jesse, in a preceding passage, shows that Richard was sent, as a young boy, to Middleham to be under the supervision of Warwick; and for his military education.—Ed.]

166.play: As for another secret close intent,
notes: secret close intent] 166. HUDSON: This probably was to get into his hands the son and daughter of Clarence, who had been left in the care of their aunt, Lady Anne, and had succeeded to the large portion of the vast estates of their grandfather, the Earl of Warwick.—WRIGHT: It is not clear how Richard’s marriage with Anne could be supposed to favour his plans for obtaining the crown.—WARNER (p. 170): Richard’s object in the marriage was twofold: first, to get Anne’s enor

167.play: By marrying her which I must reach unto.

168.play: But yet I run before my horse to market:

169.play: Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns:

170.play: When they are gone, then must I count my gains.

1.play: [Enter the corpse of KING HENRY the Sixth, Gentlemen with halberds to guard it; LADY ANNE being the mourner]
notes: Scena Secunda.] 1. CAPELL (ii, 174): The action of this scene (that is, the entreatment) is in truth the play’s first, according to history; upon which the poet is trespasser six year at least, in making the commitment of Clarence a prior incident: but the step was necessary for the better knitting together this life’s enormities, and disposing them into a play; which is done with great artifice, difficulties being considered.—COURTENAY (ii, 66): It is remarkable that Shakespeare, while he introduces Gloucester courting the widowed Anne in the public streets, had not heard the circumstances of the marriage, as related by a contemporary. The Croyland Continuator tells us that Clarence concealed his sister-in-law from the pursuit of Gloucester, but that she was at last discovered in London, in the disguise of a cook-maid, and then placed in sanctuary. Although the marriages of the fifteenth century—perhaps the women of that time—are not to be judged by our present notions, I cannot but regard the marriage of Anne as a material point in the evidence which disproves Gloucester’s part in the death of Prince Edward and King Henry.—JESSE (Memor. of Rich. III., p. 101): At the time when the corpse of Henry VI. was on its way to Chertsey, Richard was marching with his brother, King Edward, against the Bastard Falconbridge.—SKOTTOWE (i, 192): Sober criticism will question whether dramatic effect be not lessened by this scene. The confidence of the wooer is inconsistent with his previously expressed opinion, that his assumption of the character of a lover would be preposterous. His surprise at his success pleads guilty, by anticipation, to the objection which might be justly urged against the probability of his preceding conquest.—RICHARDSON (p. 18): In considering this scene, it is necessary that we keep in view the character of Lady Anne. The outlines of this character are given us in her own conversation; but we see it more completely finished and filled up, indirectly indeed, but not less distinctly, in the conduct of Richard. She is represented by Shakespeare of a mind altogether frivolous; incapable of deep affection; guided by no steady principles of virtue, produced or strengthened by reason and reflection; the prey of vanity, which is her ruling passion; susceptible of every
Halberds] 1. WRIGHT: This means the same as, Gentlemen with halberds, as ‘ancient’ or ‘ensign’ is ensign bearer, and ‘trumpet’ signifies trumpeter.

2.play: LADY ANNE: Set down, set down your honourable load,
notes: Set downe . . . load] 2. DYCE (Few Notes, p. 103): Shakespeare had in his recollection a line at the commencement of a scene in the Sec. Part of the Troublesome Raigne of King John: ‘Set downe, set downe, the loade not worth your paine.’ (Sig. K, 4, ed. 1622.) [Is it not a little temerarious to assert positively what Shakespeare had, or had not, in his recollection?—Ed.]

3.play: If honour may be shrouded in a hearse,
notes: If Honor . . . Herse] 3. To this parenthesis there is one that is possibly similar in Holinshed’s account: ‘The dead corps on the Ascension euen was conueid with billes and glaue pompouslie (if you will call that a funerall pompe) from the Tower to the Church of Saint Paule,’ etc.—Ed.

4.play: Whilst I awhile obsequiously lament
notes: obsequiously] 4. See SCHMIDT (Lex.) for other examples of ‘obsequious’ used as applying to mourning.

5.play: The untimely fall of virtuous Lancaster.

6.play: Poor key-cold figure of a holy king!
notes: key-cold] 6. STEEVENS says: ‘A key, on account of the coldness of the metal of which it is composed, was anciently employed to stop any slight bleeding.’ He gives as an example, ‘It is best you hide your head, for feare your wise braines take key-colde.’—Satiro-mastix, Dekker, 1602. This quotation seems hardly to illustrate the explanation, or even to apply to the present passage. MALONE gives, I think, a more apposite and earlier use of the phrase, ‘And then in key-cold Lucrece’ bleeding stream He falls.’—Rape of Luc., line 1774. KNIGHT finds fault with Steevens’s note on the ground that it is ‘forced,’ and quotes: “‘But for Christ and obtaining an interest in him, O how key-cold are they.”—Gurnall’s Chris-

7.play: Pale ashes of the house of Lancaster!

8.play: Thou bloodless remnant of that royal blood!

9.play: Be it lawful that I invocate thy ghost,
notes: inuoacte] 9. For two other examples of 'invocate' used in this sense, see Schmidt (Lex.).

10.play: To hear the lamentations of Poor Anne,

11.play: Wife to thy Edward, to thy slaughter'd son,
notes: Wife to thy Edward] 11. See Anne Neville, Dram. Person.

12.play: Stabb'd by the selfsame hand that made these wounds!

13.play: Lo, in these windows that let forth thy life,
notes: windowes . . . life] 13. Tawney: The metaphor is perhaps based upon the superstitious custom of leaving open the window when a person is dying. Compare King John, V vii, 29: 'It would not out at windows nor at doors.'

14.play: I pour the helpless balm of my poor eyes.
notes: helplesse] 14. For examples of other adjectives used in active sense, see Walker, Crit., ii, 87.

15.play: Cursed be the hand that made these fatal holes!

16.play: Cursed be the heart that had the heart to do it!

17.play: Cursed the blood that let this blood from hence!
notes: the Blood] 17. For numerous examples wherein ‘blood’ may mean disposition, or temper, see SCHMIDT (Lex.).

18.play: More direful hap betide that hated wretch,

19.play: That makes us wretched by the death of thee,

20.play: Than I can wish to adders, spiders, toads,
notes: to Woluces . . . Toade] 20. R. G. WHITE: It has been remarked that the next line shows that the Qq give the true reading, because wolves are not ‘creeping things.’ If the Folio had merely ‘wolves’ for adders, this reasoning would be good, if not conclusive; but it has ‘to wolves, to spiders,’ etc., where the repetition of the preposition cuts off the connection which would otherwise exist between ‘wolves’ and ‘creeping venom’d thing,’ which refers only to spiders and toads. The change seems clearly to have been made, upon the revision of the play, for the purpose of giving the passage variety of thought and rhythm.—DELIUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 161): That Shakespeare first thought of Gloucester as like a ‘wolf,’ seems to be the simplest explanation of this reading of the Folio. Afterwards, on rearranging the sentence, he perhaps placed the amendment in the margin—to adders instead of ‘to wolves, to’—an emendation that the anonymous editor of the Quarto seems alone to have noticed.—SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 4) also thinks the Folio reading to be an unfinished correction by Shakespeare, and adds: ‘There seems to be nothing to induce another to alter anything. But Shakespeare himself, on reading the passage over, may, perhaps, have observed that, the particular act which calls forth the curse, being one of open violence and not of secret treachery, there is an incongruity in wishing to the actor the fate of “creeping venom’d things,” and he may have meant to alter it by the substitution of wolves, and noxious creatures of that kind. If it be thought extravagant to suppose that Shakespeare would not have completed the correction when he was about it, inquire of our poets what they do, when they cannot quite make up their minds, on the instant how to finish a passage which they have begun.’—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 32): I regard the reading ‘wolves’ as confirmatory of the theory, that the Folio (minus the corrector’s emendations) gives us the play as it was originally composed. I can see no difficulty in supposing that Shakespeare, in the outpour of his invention, wrote down the lines as we have them in the Folio. In fact, I think we want ‘wolves’ here, as well as ‘spiders’ and ‘toads’—the former, to express the blood-thirsty ferocity of Gloucester, and the latter, to indicate the loathing with which Anne regards him. I consider the correction of the Quarto a deliberate correction, but I am very doubtful whether it is Shakespeare’s.

21.play: Or any creeping venom'd thing that lives!
notes: venom’d] 21. WRIGHT: Compare ‘The gilded newt and eyeless venom’d worm,’ Timon, IV, iii, 182. Similarly poisoned is used for poisonous in Lyly’s Euphues (ed. Arber), p. 196: ‘Nylus breedeth the precious stone and the poysoned serpent.’ [For another familiar example see Macbeth, IV, i, 4: ‘In the poisoned entrails throw.’]

22.play: If ever he have child, abortive be it,

23.play: Prodigious, and untimely brought to light,
notes: Prodigious] 23. That is, portentous, monstrous.

24.play: Whose ugly and unnatural aspect
notes: Aspect] 24. ABBOTT (§ 490) gives numerous examples of the shifting of accent in certain words.

25.play: May fright the hopeful mother at the view;

26.play: And that be heir to his unhappiness!
notes: unhappiness] 26. STEEVENS: That is, disposition to mischief. So, in Much Ado, I, i, 361: ‘Dreamed of unhappiness and waked herself with laughing.’ WRIGHT notes that these are the only passages in Shakespeare wherein the word occurs.

27.play: If ever he have wife, let her he made

28.play: A miserable by the death of him
notes: death] 28. SIR WILLIAM BLACKSTONE (Sh. Soc. Papers, i, 98) conjectures that we should here read life, since, when Anne repeats the curse, IV, i, 86, she says, ‘As miserable by the life of thee.’ Blackstone was, however, anticipated by Cibber, whose arrangement was first published in 1700, nine years before Rowe’s.—ED.
of him] 28. For other examples of a pronoun used for a pronominal adjective see, if needful, ABBOTT, § 225.

29.play: As I am made by my poor lord and thee!

30.play: Come, now towards Chertsey with your holy load,
notes: Chertsey] 30. ORDISII (p. 59): Richard, we may imagine, after watching his brother Clarence pass on towards the fateful Tower, had lingered in Candlewick Street (now Cannon Street), in expectation of the funeral cortege; the destination, Chertsey, suggests that he may have intercepted the procession on its way from the Cathedral to Paul’s wharf.—MARSHALL: Chertsey is in Surrey near the Thames, not far below Staines. There was a very ancient Abbey there, having a mitred Abbot with a seat in the House of Lords. The convent buildings have long since been demolished, and only a very few fragments are now remaining.

31.play: Taken from Paul's to be interred there;

32.play: And still, as you are weary of the weight,

33.play: Rest you, whiles I lament King Henry's corse.

34.play: [Enter GLOUCESTER]

35.play: GLOUCESTER: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.

36.play: LADY ANNE: What black magician conjures up this fiend,

37.play: To stop devoted charitable deeds?

38.play: GLOUCESTER: Villains, set down the corse; or, by Saint Paul,
notes: Villaines] 38. WRIGHT thinks the Quarto reading may perhaps be the true one, since Richard’s speech is ‘addressed to the Gentleman in command of the party of halberdiers.’ But is not this first speech of Richard’s addressed directly to the bearers? Possibly the Quarto reading originated in the similarity in sound between ‘Villain set’ and ‘Villains set.’ —See also I, i, 143. Do not these examples prove that the compositors followed the voice of him who read aloud the copy to them?—ED.

39.play: I'll make a corse of him that disobeys.
notes: He . . . disobeyes] 39. JOHNSON: Compare Hamlet, I, iv, 85: ‘I’ll make a ghost of him that lets me.’

40.play: Gentleman: My lord, stand back, and let the coffin pass.

41.play: GLOUCESTER: Unmanner'd dog! stand thou, when I command:
notes: Stand’st thou] 41. PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-’76; p. 82): As I cannot see that the printer was likely to fall into error here, I am disposed to attribute the reading of the Folio to the corrector. He frequently displays an inability to appreciate the force of a recurrent word, and ‘stand’st thou when I command?’ furnishes a sufficient sense, if we give to ‘stand’ the meaning of withstand, oppose.

42.play: Advance thy halbert higher than my breast,
notes: Halbert] 42. WRIGHT: That is, a kind of pole-axe, or long-handled axe, with a pike attached. In Icelandic barda signifies an axe, and Professor Skeat connects the first part of the word with helm, a helve or handle, the form in Middle High German being helm barde, or helbarte. It may be that the Icelandic barda is derived from the Middle High German barda, a broad axe, and as this in Old High German is barta, it is also possible that ‘halberd’ and partisan, as they denote similar weapons, may be etymologically connected.

43.play: Or, by Saint Paul, I'll strike thee to my foot,

44.play: And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.
notes: spurne vpon] 44. WRIGHT says: ‘Elsewhere in Shakespeare “spurn” is fol-

45.play: LADY ANNE: What, do you tremble? are you all afraid?

46.play: Alas, I blame you not; for you are mortal,
notes: you are Mortall] 46. Are we to infer from this that Anne considers herself divine! she is not only able to bear the sight of Richard, but even to command him to depart.—ED.

47.play: And mortal eyes cannot endure the devil.
notes: Mortall eyes] 47. BARNARD: Shakespeare probably means us to understand that Richard possessed the power of fascination through the evil eye, a faculty conferred upon him by a compact with the devil. [Previous to this note in his edition of this play, Barnard had written an article (N. & Q., 23d May, 1896, p. 402) on Richard III. and the Evil Eye; therein he presents as proofs of Shakespeare’s intention a number of passages from the play wherein allusions to ‘sight,’ ‘eyes,’ ‘devil,’ occur. Barnard’s remarks will be found under the passages to which they directly refer.—ED.]

48.play: Avaunt, thou dreadful minister of hell!

49.play: Thou hadst but power over his mortal body,

50.play: His soul thou canst not have; therefore be gone.

51.play: GLOUCESTER: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.
notes: curst] 51. That is, shrewish, as in Shakespeare passim.

52.play: LADY ANNE: Foul devil, for God's sake, hence, and trouble us not;
notes: Diuell] 52. For instances of monosyllabic pronunciation of certain words composed of two short syllables, see WALKER, Vers., p. 64, or Guest, p. 52.

53.play: For thou hast made the happy earth thy hell,

54.play: Fill'd it with cursing cries and deep exclaims.
notes: exclaimes] 54. Compare IV, iii, 139; Rich. II.: I, ii, 2; Tro. & Cress., V, iii, 91; and Tit. And., IV, i, 86. For other examples of like forms, see ABBOTT, § 451.

55.play: If thou delight to view thy heinous deeds,

56.play: Behold this pattern of thy butcheries.
notes: paterne] 56. WRIGHT: Compare ‘Thou cunning’st pattern of excelling nature,’ Othello, V, ii, 11. And, ‘That peerlesse paterne of Dame Natures pride,’ Faerie Queene, IV, vi, 24.

57.play: O, gentlemen, see, see! dead Henry's wounds

58.play: Open their congeal'd mouths and bleed afresh!
notes: Open . . . bleed afresh] 58. Referring to the belief that a murdered body will bleed afresh in the presence of the murderer. CHURCHILL (p. 223) says this 'reference to the reopening of Henry's wounds rests upon Holinshed's account of Henry's burial (derived from Warkworth); there is no mention of the fact in Hall.' The widespread belief in this superstition is attested by the numerous examples of its mention by earlier authors. STEEVENS quotes a remark by TOLLET that 'this opinion seems to be derived from the ancient Swedes, or Northern nations from whom we descend; for they practised this method of trial in dubious cases, as appears from Pitt's Atlas, in Sweden, p. 20.' See GREY, ii, 53, or KNIGHT (Historical Notes, at end of play) for an account of a trial wherein this belief was used to testify against a murderer. REED cites King James's Demonologie, ed. 1608, p. 422, as having a reference to this superstition. WRIGHT refers to Scott's Fair Maid of Perth where the body of Proudftte is exposed in the church of St. John for the purpose of applying this test for the discovery of his slayer. He cites also Brand's Popular Antiquities (Bohn's Antiq. Library), iii, 229.—ED.

59.play: Blush, Blush, thou lump of foul deformity;

60.play: For 'tis thy presence that exhales this blood
notes: exhales] 60. WRIGHT: That is, draws forth. Shakespeare always uses the word in this sense, as when he speaks of 'some meteor that the sun exhales' (Rom. & Jul., III, v, 13), connecting the latter part of the word with the English hale, and not with the Latin halare, to breathe. In the inflated language of Pistol, Hen. V: II, i, 66, exhale is simply draw [sc. a sword]: 'The grave doth gape and doting death is near, Therefore exhale.'

61.play: From cold and empty veins, where no blood dwells;

64.play: O God, which this blood madest, revenge his death!

65.play: O earth, which this blood drink'st revenge his death!
notes: Earth . . . drink’st] 65. TAWNEY: Compare Genesis, iv, 2, 'the ground which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand'; and 1 Hen. IV: I, i, 5, 6: 'No more the thirsty entrance of this soil Shall daub her lips with her own children's blood.' There is a still bolder figure in 3 Henry VI: II, iii, 23: 'Then let the earth be drunken with our blood.' [See also IV, iii, 32, 33, of this play.—ED.]

66.play: Either heaven with lightning strike the
notes: Either] 66. For examples of monosyllabic pronunciation of certain disyllables ending in -ther, see WALKER, Vers., p. 103.

68.play: Or earth, gape open wide and eat him quick,
notes: Earth . . . quicke] 68. WRIGHT: There is probably a reference here to Number, xvi, 30: ‘But if the Lord make a new thing, and the earth open her mouth, and swallow them up, with all that appertain unto them, and they go down quick into the pit; then ye shall understand that those men have provoked the Lord.’ [Compare True Tragedie of Richard Duke of Yorke: ‘Maie that ground gape and swallow me alive.’ Sh. Soc. Reprint, p. 122.—ED.]

69.play: As thou dost swallow up this good king's blood

70.play: Which his hell-govern'd arm hath butchered!
notes: butchered] 70. WRIGHT: It is worth while to draw attention, once for all, to the unusual number of instances in this play in which the participial termination -ed is accentuated. [A noteworthy reminder. No one who has pondered over every syllable of this play, for the purposes of collation, can fail to be struck with this peculiarity.—ED.]

71.play: GLOUCESTER: Lady, you know no rules of charity,

72.play: Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

73.play: LADY ANNE: Villain, thou know'st no law of God nor man:

74.play: No beast so fierce but knows some touch of pity.
notes: but knowes] 74. For examples of omission of subject in relative clauses, following ‘but,’ see ABBOTT, § 123.
touch] 74. That is, sensation, feeling. For other examples, see SCHMIDT (Lex. 4).

75.play: GLOUCESTER: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.
notes: But . . . no Beast] 75. Richard’s reasoning seems to be that if to know some trace of pity be the mark of a beast, inasmuch as he does not show that mark he cannot be a beast. Anne’s next remark applies to the first part of his speech; she hardly hears its ending.—ED.

76.play: LADY ANNE: O wonderful, when devils tell the truth!

77.play: GLOUCESTER: More wonderful, when angels are so angry.

78.play: Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,
notes: Vouchsafe (diuine perfection of a Woman), etc.] 78. RICHARDSON (p. 21): Here also we may observe the application of those flatteries and apparent obsequiousness, which, if they cannot take effect at present, otherwise than to give higher provocation, yet when her wrath subsides, will operate in a different direction, and tend to excite that vanity which is the predominant disposition of her mind, and by means of which he will accomplish his purpose. It was not alone sufficient to provoke her anger and her resentment to the utmost, in order that they might immediately subside; but, by alleging apparent reasons for change of sentiment, to assist them in their decline. Though Lady Anne possesses no decided, determined virtue, yet her moral nature, uncultivated as it appears, would

79.play: Of these supposed-evils, to give me leave,
notes: supposed Crimes] 79. R. G. WHITE: If evils were the original word, the change was evidently made with intention, and is a great improvement; for it opposes known evils to supposed crimes; and the evils which Anne actually suffered, and for which she claims the right to curse, were the direct consequence of crimes which Richard calls ‘supposed.’ By the change, too, Shakespeare freed the line of a superfluous and harmful syllable in a part of the verse in which he solicitously avoided irregularity.

80.play: By circumstance, but to acquit myself.

81.play: LADY ANNE: Vouchsafe, defused infection of a man,
notes: defus’d] 81. JOHNSON: I believe ‘diffus’d’ in this place signifies irregular, uncouth; such is its meaning in other passages of Shakespeare.—STEEVENS: It may mean, ‘thou that art as dangerous as a pestilence, that infects the air by its diffusion.’ It may, however, mean irregular. So, in Merry Wives: ‘rush at once With some diffused song,’ IV, iv, 54. Again in Greene’s Farewell to Folie, 1617: ‘I haue scene an English gentleman so defused in his sutes; his doublet being for the weare of Castile, his hose for Venice.’ [p. 253; ed. Grosart. See also Hen. V: V, ii, 61: ‘defused attire and everything that seems unnatural.’] —R. G. WHITE: I suspect that ‘defus’d’ is a misprint for an epithet antithetical to ‘divine’ in Richard’s speech. If ‘deprav’d infection of a man’ had been found in any old copy, I think that it would have been adopted without question.—WRIGHT: ‘Defus’d infection’ is a phrase coined to match ‘divine perfection,’ and the play upon words was more aimed at by the writer than their appropriateness. ‘Defused’ is properly disordered and must here mean shapeless.—WEBB: The word ‘defus’d’ would have more point if taken, not from di-, in various directions, and fusus, poured, but in the sense of decocted, giving the prefix de- its usual sense of

82.play: For these known evils, but to give me leave,
notes: evillS] 82. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-’76; p. 6): I suppose it was for the sake of the metre that ‘evils’ was not changed to crimes in this line [see Text. Notes, l. 82.] It could not have been done without some further alteration; and as the necessary alteration could have involved no difficulty for the author, it must be admitted to be more like the work of a corrector who was not the author; although the corrector of this play, whoever he was, has shown himself quite capable of dealing with such a difficulty.

83.play: By circumstance, to curse thy cursed self.

84.play: GLOUCESTER: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have

85.play: Some patient leisure to excuse myself.
notes: patient leysure] 85. Compare, for a somewhat similar construction, ‘At your kind’st leisure,’ Macbeth, II, i, 24; and: ‘At your best leisure,’ Jul. Cas., III, i, 5.—Ed.

86.play: LADY ANNE: Fouler than heart can think thee, thou canst make

87.play: No excuse current, but to hang thyself.
notes: currant] 87. That is, genuine, in the same sense as the word is now applied to money.

88.play: GLOUCESTER: By such despair, I should accuse myself.

89.play: LADY ANNE: And, by despairing, shouldst thou stand excused;
notes: dispairing . . . excused] 89. SEYMOUR: This argument is urged to Cromwell in the Introduction to the famous pamphlet, Killing No Murder: ‘Let this consideration arm and fortify your highness’s mind against the fears of death and the terrors of an evil conscience, that the good you shall do by your death will, in some sort, atone for the evils of your life.’

90.play: For doing worthy vengeance on thyself,

91.play: Which didst unworthy slaughter upon others.

92.play: GLOUCESTER: Say that I slew them not?

93.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then they are not dead:

94.play: But dead they are, and devilish slave, by thee.

95.play: GLOUCESTER: I did not kill your husband.
notes: I . . . your Husband] 95. See note on I, i, 166; or Jesse (Mem. of King Rich.), p. 85 et seq.

96.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then he is alive.

97.play: GLOUCESTER: Nay, he is dead; and slain by Edward's hand.
notes: by Edwards hand] 97. Wright: In 3 Hen. VI: V, v, Edward, Clarence, and Richard all stab the young prince.

98.play: LADY ANNE: In thy foul throat thou liest: Queen Margaret saw

99.play: Thy murderous falchion smoking in his blood;
notes: Faulchion] 99. Skeat (Dict.): This word may have been really taken from the French fauchon, and afterwards altered to falchion by the influence of the Italian or Low Latin falci, crude form of falx, a sickle.

100.play: The which thou once didst bend against her breast,
notes: bend] 100. That is, direct, aim. For numerous examples of this use of ‘bend,’ see Schmidt (Lex.). Wright quotes Stow’s Annales (ed. 1580, p. 1082) ‘. . . the next morning, he bent seauen great peeces of Ordinance Culuerings, and Demi Canons, against the foote of the Bridge.’—Tawney calls attention to a similar use of Lat. tendo.

101.play: But that thy brothers beat aside the point.

102.play: GLOUCESTER: I was provoked by her slanderous tongue,

103.play: which laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

104.play: LADY ANNE: Thou wast provoked by thy bloody mind.

105.play: Which never dreamt on aught but butcheries:

106.play: Didst thou not kill this king?

notes: I graunt ye] 107. ‘He slewe with his owne handes king Henry the sixt, being prisoner in the tower, as menne constantly saye, and that without commaundemente or knowledge of the king.’—More, p. 9.—‘The occasion of the murder of Henry VI. hath no other proof but the malicious affirmation of one man; for many other men more truly did suppose that he died of mere grief and melancholy, when he heard the overthrow of his cause and friends, with the slaughter of the prince his son: and Johannes Majerus saith it was reported, King Henry VI. died of grief and thought.’—Buch, bk, iii, 81.—Wessels (p. 13): It has been said that the murder of Henry is imputed to Richard more upon vague reports than any sufficient ground, and, moreover, it seems difficult to explain why Richard should have committed so foul a deed from which he could not derive any advantage for himself. Edward and even Clarence were much more interested in it. According to Shakespeare, Richard is concerned in the death because Henry stands between him and the crown, but in reality there is no proof whatever, not even any probability, that Richard should have begun to covet the crown as early as King Henry's death.

108.play: LADY ANNE: Dost grant me, hedgehog? then, God grant me too
notes: Hedge-hogge] 108. Tawney gives as a reason for Anne’s use of this epithet that ‘the hedge-hog was an ill-omened beast,’ but was it not also the hump-backed appearance of that animal which suggested its application to ‘crook-backed Richard.’ And may there not be also a subaudition of Richard’s crest? See I, iii, 237.—Ed.

109.play: Thou mayst be damned for that wicked deed!

110.play: O, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous!

111.play: GLOUCESTER: The fitter for the King of heaven, that hath him.
notes: The better . . . him] 111. Steevens: Compare Pericles, IV, i, 10: ‘I’ll do ’t: but yet she is a goodly creature. The fitter then the gods should have her.’—Delius (Jahrbuch, vii, 158): ‘So much the better is it for the King of Heaven who has him,’ replies Gloucester in frivolous mockery of the praise which Anne has given to the dead King. But the Anonymous Corrector, by changing this to the fitter, weakens and anticipates, by this tautology, a thought amplified by Gloucester in his next speech: ‘he was fitter for that place than earth.’ [Might not ‘the better’ refer to King Henry?—Ed.]

112.play: LADY ANNE: He is in heaven, where thou shalt never come.
notes: heaven . . . come] 112. There is a curious resemblance between this line and some lines in a Scottish ballad, The Daemon Lover: ‘“O yon are the hills of Heaven” he said “Where you will never win.”’—Child, pt. viii, p. 360, where the editor says that this ballad was first published in Scott’s Minstrelsy, 5th Edition, 1812.—Ed.

113.play: GLOUCESTER: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither;
notes: holpe] 113. WRIGHT: This form, both of the preterite and the participle, is the more common in Shakespeare.

114.play: For he was fitter for that place than earth.

115.play: LADY ANNE: And thou unfit for any place but hell.

116.play: GLOUCESTER: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.

119.play: LADY ANNE: Ill rest betide the chamber where thou liest!

120.play: GLOUCESTER: So will it, madam till I lie with you.

122.play: GLOUCESTER: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,
notes: But . . . Anne] 122. RICHARDSON (p. 26): Richard was well aware that a declaration of love from him would, of course, renew her indignation. He accordingly manages her mind in such a manner as to soften its violence, by suggesting the idea of his passion, in the part of the dialogue containing, in his language, the ‘keen encounter of their wits,’ as a matter not altogether serious; and afterwards when he announces it more seriously, by mentioning it, as it were, by chance and indirectly. Still, however, with those precautions to introduce the thought with an easy and familiar appearance, it must excite violent indignation. Here, therefore, as in the former part of the scene, he must have recourse to the same command of temper, and of the same means of artfully irritating her emotion, till it entirely subsides. Accordingly, he adheres without deviation to his plan; he persists in his adulation; provokes her anger to its utmost excess; and, finally, by varying the attitudes of his flatteries, by assuming an humble and suppliant address, he subdues and restores her soul to the ruling passion. In the close of the dialogue the decline of her emotion appears distinctly traced. It follows the same course as the passion she expresses in the beginning of the scene. She is at first violent; becomes more violent; her passion subsides; yet, some ideas of propriety wandering across her mind, she makes an effort to recall her resentment. The effort is feeble; it only enables her to express contempt in her aspect; and at last she becomes the prey of her vanity. In the concluding part of the dialogue she does not, indeed, directly comply with the suit of Richard, but indicates plainly that total change in her disposition which it was his purpose to produce.

123.play: To leave this keen encounter of our wits,
notes: To leave . . . writes] 123. THE COWDEN-CLARKES: The dialogue here in its
our] 123. The reading of Q1 your might possibly have occurred through the resemblance of the written in ‘of,’ to y. In general, Q1 follows Q2.

124.play: And fall somewhat into a slower method,
notes: something] 124. For other examples of ‘something,’ see WALKER, Crit. i, 222.
slower] 124. STEEVENS: As quick was put for spritely, so ‘slower’ was put for serious. In the next scene Lord Grey desires the Queen to ‘cheer his grace with quick and merry words.’

125.play: Is not the causer of the timeless deaths
notes: timelessse] 125. WRIGHT: This appears to have been a favourite word with Shakespeare in his early days, for it occurs in Lucrece, [l. 44] and in those plays which must be referred to this first period of his career.

126.play: Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,

128.play: LADY ANNE: Thou art the cause, and most accursed effect.
notes: cause . . . effect] 128. WARBURTON: ‘Effect’ for executioner. He asks was not the causer as ill as the executioner? She answers, thou wast both. But, for causer, using the word ‘cause,’ this led her to the word ‘effect,’ for execution, ‘executioner.’ But Hanmer will make a fine oratorical period of it. [See Text. Notes.]—JOHNSON: I cannot but be rather of Hanmer’s opinion than Warburton’s,

129.play: GLOUCESTER: Your beauty was the cause of that effect;

130.play: Your beauty: which did haunt me in my sleep

131.play: To undertake the death of all the world,

132.play: So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

133.play: LADY ANNE: If I thought that, I tell thee, homicide,
notes: Homicide] 133. According to Murray (N. E. D.), this word is applied both to the act and to the agent, among early writers, in about equal proportion; Shakespeare uses it, however, in reference to the agent only. Compare Macbeth, III, i, 32, where ‘parricide’ is used for the act.—Ed.

134.play: These nails should rend that beauty from my cheeks.
notes: rent] 134. Wright: This is the frequent form of the word [rend] in the Authorised Version of 1611, although in modern editions it is only found in Jeremiah, iv, 30: ‘Though thou rentest thy face with painting.’ It occurs several times in Shakespeare.

135.play: GLOUCESTER: These eyes could never endure sweet beauty's wreck;

136.play: You should not blemish it, if I stood by:

137.play: As all the world is cheered by the sun,

138.play: So I by that; it is my day, my life.

139.play: LADY ANNE: Black night o'ershade thy day, and death thy life!

140.play: GLOUCESTER: Curse not thyself, fair creature thou art both.

141.play: LADY ANNE: I would I were, to be revenged on thee.

142.play: GLOUCESTER: It is a quarrel most unnatural,

143.play: To be revenged on him that loveth you.

144.play: LADY ANNE: It is a quarrel just and reasonable,

145.play: To be revenged on him that slew my husband.

146.play: GLOUCESTER: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband,

147.play: Did it to help thee to a better husband.

148.play: LADY ANNE: His better doth not breathe upon the earth.

149.play: GLOUCESTER: He lives that loves thee better than he could.

152.play: LADY ANNE: Why, that was he.

153.play: GLOUCESTER: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.

notes: Spits at him] 156. SEYMOUR: This indelicate action Rosalind was not ashamed either to commit or tell us of. [As You Like It, III, ii, 438.]—BARNARD (See note l. 50 supra): Anne spits to avert the ‘infection’ of the evil eye. Spitting was believed to be a preventive against fascination, as against other forms of danger or ill luck. [This is not the usual form of stage-direction, in a prompter’s copy. It is not mandatory, but descriptive.—ED.]

157.play: Why dost thou spit at me?

158.play: LADY ANNE: Would it were mortal poison, for thy sake!
notes: mortal poyson] 158. GREY (ii, 56): An allusion probably to the Sultan of Cambaya, who did eat poison from his cradle, and was of that poisonous nature that when he was determined to put any nobleman to death, he had him stript naked, spit upon him, and he instantly died.

159.play: GLOUCESTER: Never came poison from so sweet a place.

160.play: LADY ANNE: Never hung poison on a fouler toad.
notes: poyson . . . Toads] 160. ‘All manner of toads, both of the earth and water are venomous, althoug it be held that the toads of the earth are more poysonful than the toads of the water . . . But the toads of the land, which do descend into the marishes, and so live in both elements, are most venomous, and the hotter the country is the more full are they of poyson.’—Topsel, p. 730.

161.play: Out of my sight! thou dost infect my eyes.

162.play: GLOUCESTER: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

163.play: LADY ANNE: Would they were basilisks, to strike thee dead!
notes: Basliskes] 163. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): A fabulous reptile, also called a cockatrice, alleged to be hatched by a serpent from a cock’s egg; ancient authors stated that its hissing drove away all other serpents, and that its breath, and even its look, was fatal. So called, says Pliny, from a spot resembling a crown on its head; medieval authors furnished it with ‘a certain combe or coronet.’ [‘There is some question amongst writers about the generation of this serpent [the Cockatrice, or Basilisk]: for some affirm him to be brought forth of a cock’s egg . . . which egg set upon by a snake or a toad bringeth forth the Cockatrice, being half a foot in length, the hinder-part like a snake, the former part like a cock, because of a treble combe on his forehead. . . . Among all living creatures, there is none that perisheth sooner than doth a man by the poyson of a cockatrice, for with his sight he killeth him, because the beams of the Cockatrices eyes do corrupt the visible spirit of a man, which visible spirit corrupted all the other spirits coming from the brain and life of the heart are thereby corrupted, and so the man dyeth.’—Topsel, Hist. of Serpents, p. 677.—For an interesting ‘sense-history’ of the word Cockatrice, see MURRAY, s. v.]

164.play: GLOUCESTER: I would they were, that I might die at once;
notes: I would they were] 164. HAZLITT (Sh. Char., 149): Kean’s courtship scene with Lady Anne is an admirable exhibition of smooth and smiling villainy. The progress of wily adulation, of encroaching humility, is finely marked by his action, voice, and eye. He seems, like the first Tempter, to approach his prey secure of the event, and as if success had smoothed his way before him. Cooke’s manner of representing this scene was more vehement, hurried, and full of anxious uncertainty. This, though more natural in general, was less in character in this particular instance. Richard should woo not as a lover but as an actor—to show his

165.play: For now they kill me with a living death.
notes: a liuing death] 165. STEEVENS: The same conceit occurs in the Trimming of Thomas Nash, 1597: ‘How happy the rat, caught in a trap, and there dies a living death.’ Again in Watson’s Sonnets, 1580: ‘Loue is a sowr delight; a sugred greefe, a liuing death’ [Hekatompathia, Sonnet xviii.—ed. Arber]—MALONE: Compare also ‘For I have heard it is a life in death That laughs and weeps, and all but with a breath.’ Ven. & Ad., ll. 412–414.

166.play: Those eyes of thine from mine have drawn salt tears,
notes: Those eyes of thine] 166. ABBOTT (§ 239): This of yours, is now, as in early English, generally applied to one out of a class, whether the class exist or be imaginary . . . It is, however, commonly used by Shakespeare where even the conception of a class is impossible. [Compare:] ‘Will not a calf skin stop that mouth of thine?’ King John, III, i, 299.

167.play: Shamed their aspect with store of childish drops:

168.play: These eyes that never shed remorseful tear,
notes: These eyes] 168. WRIGHT: Compare 3 Hen. VI: II, i, 79, where Richard,

169.play: No, when my father York and Edward wept,
notes: No] 169. MALONE: The same phraseology occurs in a subsequent passage: 'I hope he is much grown since last I saw him. But I hear no' (II, iv, 8). [DYCE (ed. ii) declines to accept any similarity between these two passages, as does also the present ED.]

170.play: To hear the piteous moan that Rutland made
notes: Rutland] 170. BARNARD: This allusion to the scene of Rutland's death is unhistorical, for at that time York was either dead or a prisoner at the point of execution, while Edward was in Herefordshire, and Gloucester in London. [Polydore Vergil, Hall, and Holinshed have undoubtedly neglected to mention the fact that Edward and York heard, at the same moment, of the murder of Rutland; but then these same chroniclers have also omitted any account of this wooing of Anne. Since, then, it is a fictitious Richard who is here speaking, why should he be restricted to historic accuracy?—ED.]

171.play: When black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;

172.play: Nor when thy warlike father, like a child,

173.play: Told the sad story of my father's death,

174.play: And twenty times made pause to sob and weep,

175.play: That all the standers-by had wet their cheeks
notes: That] 175. For omission of so before 'that' see, if necessary, ABBOTT, §283.

176.play: Like trees bedash'd with rain: in that sad time
notes: bedash'd] 176. ROLFE calls attention to this as the only appearance of this word in Shakespeare.

177.play: My manly eyes did scorn an humble tear;

178.play: And what these sorrows could not thence exhale,
notes: exhale] 178. See l. 64 supra.

179.play: Thy beauty hath, and made them blind with weeping.

180.play: I never sued to friend nor enemy;

181.play: My tongue could never learn sweet smoothing word;
notes: smoothing] 181. WRIGHT: That is, flattering; 'soothing' as in the Qq is used in the same sense. Compare I, iii, 311, and Coriol., II, ii, 77: 'You soothed not, therefore hurt not.' In Bunyan's Holy War Mrs. Soothe-up is the wife of

182.play: But now thy beauty is proposed my fee,
notes: But now] 182. For other examples of the omission of that after ‘now,’ see Abbott, § 284 (p. 194).

183.play: My proud heart sues, and prompts my tongue to speak.

185.play: Teach not thy lips such scorn, for they were made
notes: thy lip such Scorn] 185. In speaking would it not be difficult—almost impossible—to distinguish between ‘thy lip such scorn’ of the Folios and thy ‘lips such scorn’ of the Qq? The pronoun following would, of course, decide by its number, whether its antecedent were singular or plural. But is the plural to be here preferred? Is not the expression of scorn or contempt restricted to the lower lip? Compare Wint. Tale, I, ii, 431; where Polixenes tells Camillo that Leontes ‘falling a lip of much contempt, speeds’ from him.—Ed.

186.play: For kissing, lady, not for such contempt.

187.play: If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive,

188.play: Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword;

189.play: Which if thou please to hide in this true bosom.
notes: hide] 189. Tawney: The word means sheathe in As You Like It, II, vii, 119. Abdo is used by Virgil in the same sense—Aeneid, ii, 553. [Is not ‘sheathe,’ ex vi termini, a peaceful action? It is so certainly in Orlando’s case, where ‘hide’ may well be equivalent to it. But is it so equivalent here? or is it so in Virgil, when Pyrrhus plunged his sword up to the hilt in the side of Polites? Sheathed would be there, I think, a weak translation of abdidit; and would be, I speak in all modesty, equally so here, as a paraphrase of ‘hide.’—Ed.]

190.play: And let the soul forth that adoreth thee,

191.play: I lay it naked to the deadly stroke,

192.play: And humbly beg the death upon my knee.
notes: the death] 192. Wright: Used technically for a judicial punishment, as in Henry V: IV, i, 81: ‘When they have feared the death, they have borne life away.’ [For other examples of this technical use, see Schmidt (Lex.), or Abbott, § 92.]

193.play: [He lays his breast open: she offers at it with his sword]

194.play: Nay, do not pause; for I did kill King Henry,

195.play: But 'twas thy beauty that provoked me.

196.play: Nay, now dispatch; 'twas I that stabb'd young Edward,

197.play: But 'twas thy heavenly face that set me on.

199.play: Take up the sword again, or take up me.

200.play: LADY ANNE: Arise, dissembler: though I wish thy death,

201.play: I will not be the executioner.

202.play: GLOUCESTER: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.

204.play: GLOUCESTER: Tush, that was in thy rage:

205.play: Speak it again, and, even with the word,

206.play: That hand, which, for thy love, did kill thy love,

207.play: Shall, for thy love, kill a far truer love;

208.play: To both their deaths thou shalt be accessary.

209.play: LADY ANNE: I would I knew thy heart.

211.play: LADY ANNE: I fear me both are false.

212.play: GLOUCESTER: Then never man was true.

213.play: LADY ANNE: Well, well, put up your sword.

214.play: GLOUCESTER: Say, then, my peace is made.

215.play: LADY ANNE: That shall you know hereafter.

216.play: GLOUCESTER: But shall I live in hope?

217.play: LADY ANNE: All men, I hope, live so.

218.play: GLOUCESTER: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.

219.play: LADY ANNE: To take is not to give.

220.play: GLOUCESTER: Look, how this ring encompasseth finger.

221.play: Even so thy breast encloseth my poor heart;

222.play: Wear both of them, for both of them are thine.

223.play: And if thy poor devoted suppliant may

224.play: But beg one favour at thy gracious hand,

225.play: Thou dost confirm his happiness for ever.

227.play: GLOUCESTER: That it would please thee leave these sad designs

228.play: To him that hath more cause to be a mourner,

229.play: And presently repair to Crosby Place;

230.play: Where, after I have solemnly interr'd

231.play: At Chertsey monastery this noble king,

232.play: And wet his grave with my repentant tears,

233.play: I will with all expedient duty see you:

234.play: For divers unknown reasons. I beseech you,

236.play: LADY ANNE: With all my heart; and much it joys me too,

237.play: To see you are become so penitent.

238.play: Tressel and Berkeley, go along with me.

240.play: LADY ANNE: 'Tis more than you deserve;

241.play: But since you teach me how to flatter you,

242.play: Imagine I have said farewell already.

244.play: GLOUCESTER: Sirs, take up the corse.

246.play: GLOUCESTER: No, to White-Friars; there attend my coining.

248.play: Was ever woman in this humour woo'd?

249.play: Was ever woman in this humour won?

250.play: I'll have her; but I will not keep her long.

251.play: What! I, that kill'd her husband and his father,

252.play: To take her in her heart's extremest hate,

253.play: With curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes,

254.play: The bleeding witness of her hatred by;

255.play: Having God, her conscience, and these bars

257.play: And I nothing to back my suit at all,

258.play: But the plain devil and dissembling looks,

259.play: And yet to win her, all the world to nothing!

261.play: Hath she forgot already that brave prince,

262.play: Edward, her lord, whom I, some three months since,

263.play: Stabb'd in my angry mood at Tewksbury?

264.play: A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman,

265.play: Framed in the prodigality of nature,

266.play: Young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, right royal,

267.play: The spacious world cannot again afford

268.play: And will she yet debase her eyes on me,

269.play: That cropp'd the golden prime of this sweet prince,

270.play: And made her widow to a woful bed?

271.play: On me, whose all not equals Edward's moiety?

272.play: On me, that halt and am unshapen thus?

273.play: My dukedom to a beggarly denier,

274.play: I do mistake my person all this while:

275.play: Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot,

276.play: Myself to be a marvellous proper man.

277.play: I'll be at charges for a looking-glass,

278.play: And entertain some score or two of tailors,

279.play: To study fashions to adorn my body:

280.play: Since I am crept in favour with myself,

281.play: Will maintain it with some little cost.

282.play: But first I'll turn yon fellow in his grave;

283.play: And then return lamenting to my love.

284.play: Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass,

285.play: That I may see my shadow as I pass.

1.play: [Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH, RIVERS, and GREY]
notes: Scene Tertia] 1. WRIGHT: The scene is laid in the palace at Westminster, which formerly stood on the south side of Westminster Hall. 'This hath been,' says Stow, 'the principal seat and palace of all the kings of England since the Conquest; for here have they in the great hall kept their feasts of coronation especially, and other solemn feasts, as at Christmas and such like, most commonly' (Survey, etc., pp. 172-174). The date of this scene is April, 1483.
Lord Gray] 3. See FRENCH, Dram. Person., l. 15.

2.play: RIVERS: Have patience, madam: there's no doubt his majesty
notes: Majesty] 4. For examples of softening unaccented syllables of a polysyllable, see ABBOTT, § 468.

3.play: Will soon recover his accustom'd health.

4.play: GREY: In that you brook it in, it makes him worse:
notes: In . . . worse] 6. TAWNEY: There appears to be a confusion of two constructions, 'the fact that you take it ill makes him worse' and 'in that you take it ill, you make him worse.' The line may be literally paraphrased, 'Because you take it ill, the fact that you do so makes him worse.'

5.play: Therefore, for God's sake, entertain good comfort,

6.play: And cheer his grace with quick and merry words.
notes: eyes] 8. R. G. WHITE: It would seem that if words [of the Qq] were the original reading, the change was made in the revision. It was because the Queen appeared despondingly, 'brook'd it ill,' that the king was worse, as Gray would make it appear; and therefore he begs her to look upon her husband with lively, cheerful eyes.

7.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: If he were dead, what would betide of me?
notes: [I . . . me] 9. This is the last line at the foot of the column, in the Folio, and though the catchword is properly 'Gray,' the compositor has repeated the line at the top of the next page. Possibly the stints given to the compositors were incorrectly marked.—Ed.
betide] 9. WRIGHT: 'Betide' is here used loosely for become. It properly means to happen, come to pass, and is sometimes followed by to and sometimes used without any preposition.

8.play: RIVERS: No other harm but loss of such a lord.

9.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: The loss of such a lord includes all harm.
notes: The losse . . . harmes] 12. Compare 'Where the greater malady is fixed, The lesser is scarce felt,' Lear, III, iv, 8.—Ed.

10.play: GREY: The heavens have bless'd you with a goodly son,

11.play: To be your comforter when he is gone.

12.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Oh, he is young and his minority

13.play: Is put unto the trust of Richard Gloucester,
notes: vnto] 16. For a similar use of 'unto' meaning into, compare 'He hath turned a heaven unto a hell,' Mid. N. Dream, I, i, 207, wherein Q2 and the Ff read into; and 'our wars Will turn unto a peaceful comic sport,' 1 Henry VI: II, ii, 45.—Ed.

14.play: A man that loves not me, nor none of you.

15.play: RIVERS: Is it concluded that he shall be protector?

16.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: It is determined, not concluded yet:
notes: determin'd . . . concluded] 19. WRIGHT: It is resolved upon, though no formal record of the fact has been made. At Trinity College, Cambridge, the book, in which official entries are made of the decisions of the Master and Seniors, is called the Conclusion Book.

17.play: But so it must be, if the king miscarry.

18.play: [Enter BUCKINGHAM and DERBY]

19.play: GREY: Here come the lords of Buckingham and Derby.
notes: Derby] 21. See Dram. Person., Sir William Stanley, l. 24.

20.play: BUCKINGHAM: Good time of day unto your royal grace!

21.play: DERBY: God make your majesty joyful as you have been!

22.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: The Countess Richmond, good my Lord of Derby.
notes: Countesse Richmond] 25. MALONE: Margaret, daughter to John Beaufort, first Duke of Somerset. After the death of her first husband, Edmund Tudor, Earl of Richmond, half brother to Henry VI., by whom she had only one son, afterwards King Henry VII., she first married Sir Henry Stafford, uncle to Humphrey, Duke of Buckingham.
good my L.] 25. For transposition of possessive adjectives, see ABBOTT, § 13.

23.play: To your good prayers will scarcely say amen.

24.play: Yet, Derby, notwithstanding she's your wife,

25.play: And loves not me, be you, good lord, assured

26.play: I hate not you for her proud arrogance.

27.play: DERBY: I do beseech you, either not believe

28.play: The envious slanders of her false accusers;

29.play: Or, if she be accused in true report,

30.play: Bear with her weakness, which, I think proceeds

31.play: From wayward sickness, and no grounded malice.

32.play: RIVERS: Saw you the king to-day, my Lord of Derby?

33.play: DERBY: But now the Duke of Buckingham and I
notes: But now] 36. That is, even now at this instant.

34.play: Are come from visiting his majesty.

35.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What likelihood of his amendment, lords?
notes: Qu.] 35. CAPELL (ii, 175): If what the Queen asks at l. 38 be consider'd by the judicious, the Quarto assignation of this speech will seem the gracefuller: Rivers, a wise and grave person, judges from the countenance of the lords he addresses that they had no bad news, and asks it to give his sister some comfort.

36.play: BUCKINGHAM: Madam, good hope; his grace speaks cheerfully.

37.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: God grant him health! Did you confer with him?

38.play: BUCKINGHAM: Madam, we did: he desires to make atonement
notes: I Madam] 41. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-76; p. 39): The Quarto line is distinctly irregular; which the Corrector alters, with the obvious intention of removing the irregularity.

attonement] 41. More twice makes use of this word in speaking of the reconciliation between the Queen's kindred and that of the King: 'the late made attonemente, in whyche the kinges pleasure hadde more place then the parties willes' (p. 20), and 'hauyng more regarde to their olde variance, then their new attonement' (p. 23).—ED.

39.play: Betwixt the Duke of Gloucester and your brothers,
notes: Duke of Glouster] 42. WRIGHT: Richard is charged by More with fomenting the quarrels with the queen's family: 'And for as muche as hee well wiste and holpe to mayntayn, a long continued grudge and hearte brennynge betweene the Quenes kindred and the kinges blood eyther partyc envyinge others authorite, he nowe thought that their deuision shoulde bee (as it was in dede) a fortherlye begynynge to the pursuite of his intente' (pp. 11, 12).

Brothers] 42. CAPELL (ii, 175): In the opening of Act Second, where that atonement takes place which Buckingham speaks of, performance differs from his account; for there we find some sons of this Queen and but one brother: that there was design of atoning at least a second, his expressions are evidence; and who the intended second might be, may be gather'd with some certainty from expressions in Act Third and elsewhere: In that Act the Prince is made to lament, with great feeling, the absence of 'more uncles,' his speeches pointing plainly to a recent comitment just imparted.—WRIGHT: The only 'brother' of the Queen mentioned in this play is Earl Rivers. But the first Earl Rivers, Elizabeth's father, had seven sons and six daughters.—DANIEL (Introd., p. xvi; foot-note): I suspect that the original author of the play was not very clear as to the relationship of his dramatis personae, and that he supposed Grey, who is nowhere referred to as one of the Queen's sons, to be one of her brothers. In two places in the Quarto, I, iii, 73; IV, iii, 402, 'brothers,' of the Folio, is corrected to brother, though in the four other places this correction has been overlooked.

40.play: And betwixt them and my lord chamberlain;

41.play: And sent to warn them to his royal presence.
notes: warne] 44. That is, summon.

42.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Would all were well! but that will never be

43.play: I fear our happiness is at the highest.

notes: Enter Richard] 47. COLLIER [Emendations, etc., p. 325): We meet with a very characteristic stage-direction [by the MS Corrector] here: it is, Enter Richard, stamping angerly, which, no doubt, shows the manner of some early actor of the part, perhaps of Burbage himself, the original Richard; for, supposing the corrector of the Folio, 1632, never to have seen him (he died in March, 1619), his peculiarities in the performance would, most probably, be traditionally handed down to his successors.

45.play: GLOUCESTER: They do me wrong, and I will not endure it:

46.play: Who are they that complain unto the king,

47.play: That I, forsooth, am stern, and love them not?

48.play: By holy Paul, they love his grace but lightly

49.play: That fill his ears with such dissentious rumours.

50.play: Because I cannot flatter and speak fair,

51.play: Smile in men's faces, smooth, deceive and cog,
notes: smooth] 54. See note on 'smoothing,' I, ii, 188.

cogge] 54. WRIGHT (Note on Coriol., III, ii, 133): That is, cheat, cozen. Cotgrave gives, 'Ioncher. To strew. . . also, to gull; cog, or foist with; lie vnto, deceiue, giue gudgeons, beare in hand with vntruthes; also to dallie, ieast or toy with.'

52.play: Duck with French nods and apish courtesy,
notes: French node] 55. STEEVENS: An importation of artificial manners seems to have afforded our ancient poets a never failing topic of invective. So, in a

53.play: I must be held a rancorous enemy.

54.play: Cannot a plain man live and think no harm,

55.play: But thus his simple truth must be abused

notes: Tacke] 59. SKEAT (Dict.): The phrase ‘thou Sire John’ is in Chaucer, Cant. Tales, 14816; on which Tyrwhitt remarks: ‘I know not how it has happened that in the principal modern languages, John, or its equivalent, is a name of contempt, or at least of slight. So the Italians use Gianni, from whence Zani; the Spaniards Juan, as bobo Juan, a foolish John; the French Jean, with various additions; and in English when we call a man a John, we do not mean it as a title of honour. Chaucer, in l. 3708, uses Jacke fool, as the Spaniards do bobo Juan, and I suppose Jack-ass has the same etymology.’ ‘Go fro the window, Jacke fool, she said.’ This Middle English Jacke is obviously borrowed from the French Jaques, but it is very remarkable that this common French name is considered as an equivalent to the English common name John, since it really answers to Jacob.

57.play: RIVERS: To whom in all this presence speaks your grace?

58.play: GLOUCESTER: To thee, that hast nor honesty nor grace.

59.play: When have I injured thee? when done thee wrong?

60.play: Or thee? or thee? or any of your faction?

61.play: A plague upon you all! His royal person,--

62.play: Whom God preserve better than you would wish!--

63.play: Cannot be quiet scarce a breathing-while,
notes: Cannot . . . scarse] 66. WRIGHT: The double negative is, perhaps, due to the adverb being separated from the verb. [See l. 95, post.]
breathing while] 66. Compare Ven. & Ad., ‘It shall be fickle false and

64.play: But you must trouble him with lewd complaints.
notes: lewd] 67. BRADLEY (N. E. D. s. v.): 5. Of persons, their actions, etc.: Bad, vile, evil, wicked, base; unprincipled, ill-conditioned; good-for-nothing, worthless, ‘naughty.’

65.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Brother of Gloucester, you mistake the matter.
notes: Brother] 73. See I. 42, supra.

66.play: The king, of his own royal disposition,

67.play: And not provoked by any suitor else;
notes: else] 70. WRIGHT: Of course ‘else’ is superfluous, as in Macbeth V, viii, 4: ‘Of all men else I have avoided thee.’ The construction may be compared with that in the well-known lines of Milton, Paradise Lost, iv, 323, 324: ‘Adam the goodliest of men since born His sons; the fairest of her daughters, Eve.’ Professor Masson, in his note on that passage, refers to Paradise Lost, ii, 678, 679, for a similar instance: ‘God and his Son except, Created thing naught valued he nor shunned.’

68.play: Aiming, belike, at your interior hatred,

69.play: Which in your outward actions shows itself
notes: in] 69. For examples of ‘on’ meaning in consequence of see, if needful, ABBOTT, § 180. For ‘of’ as in Qq, see § 168.

70.play: Against my kindred, brothers, and myself,

71.play: Makes him to send; that thereby he may gather
notes: Makes ... ground] 74. R. G. WHITE: Whatever may be the case with regard to the missing line [as in the Quarto], nothing can be clearer than that in the Folio we have, not an accidental corruption, but a part, at least, of the result of a revision of the passage, in which Shakespeare rid this of the superfluous thereby, and, happily, both for sense and rhythm, substituted ‘learn the ground’ for ‘gather

Notes.] 75. WRIGHT: The Quarto reading is an instance of the construction, by which, when two infinitives depend upon an auxiliary verb, the second is often preceded by to. Compare Cymb., III, ii, 64-66: ‘How we may steal from hence, and from the gap That we shall make in time, from our hencegoing And our return, to excuse.’ Again, in the Prayer Book Version of Psalm lxix, 26: ‘Let their habitation be void: and no man to dwell in their tents,’ and Psalm lxxxviii, 8: ‘That they might put their trust in God: and not to forget the works of God.’

72.play: The ground of your ill-will, and so remove it.

73.play: GLOUCESTER: I cannot tell: the world is grown so bad,

74.play: That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch:
notes: Wren] 76. BARNARD sees (Notes & Queries, 14 March, 1896) in this use of the name ‘wren’ a fourfold allusion to the Woodvilles thus: a scornful comparison with the size of the wren; the size of the Woodville family and the proverbially numerous progeny of the wren; the fact that the wren was regarded as unholy; finally, that, as the wren had been made king of the birds by a trick, so by sorcery Edward IV. obtained his wife Elizabeth Grey, to whom the Woodvilles owed their rise.

make prey] 76. WRIGHT: ‘Make’ is frequently joined with a substantive so as to be equivalent to the cognate verb, so make pursuit = pursue, III, ii, 34; make abode = abide, Two Gent., IV, iii, 23. See III, v, 90.

75.play: Since every Jack became a gentleman

76.play: There's many a gentle person made a Jack.

77.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Come, come, we know your meaning, brother
notes: meane] 95. The COWDEN-CLARKES: It is noteworthy that not only does Shakespeare use ‘mean’ where now means is the word employed, but that on three occa-

79.play: You envy my advancement and my friends':

80.play: God grant we never may have need of you!

81.play: GLOUCESTER: Meantime, God grants that we have need of you:

82.play: Your brother is imprison'd by your means,

84.play: Held in contempt; whilst many fair promotions

85.play: Are daily given to ennoble those

86.play: That scarce, some two days since, were worth a noble.
notes: Noble] 87. WRIGHT: A very obvious pun, to which a worthy parallel may be found in 1 Henry VI: V, iv, 23: ‘You have suborn’d this man Of purpose to obscure my noble birth. ’Tis true I gave a noble to the priest The morn that I was wedded to her mother.'—BARNARD: The ‘noble’ was a gold coin worth 6s. 8d., or half a mark. It was first struck by Edward III., and took its name from the excellence of the metal. The sum survives in the solicitor’s fee.

87.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: By Him that raised me to this careful height
notes: carefully] 88. WRIGHT: Compare ‘O full of careful business are his looks!’ Rich. II: II, ii, 75.

88.play: From that contented hap which I enjoy'd,
notes: hap] 89. Compare I, ii, 20.

89.play: I never did incense his majesty

90.play: Against the Duke of Clarence, but have been

91.play: An earnest advocate to plead for him.

92.play: My lord, you do me shameful injury,

93.play: Falsely to draw me in these vile suspects.
notes: in] 94. See I, ii, 287.
suspects] 94. Compare III, v, 37.

94.play: GLOUCESTER: You may deny that you were not the cause
notes: deny . . . not] 95. For examples of a double negative, see WALKER, Crit., i, 66; and line 66, above for a triple negative, see Twelfth Night, III, i, 163: As You Like It, I, ii, 27, of this ed.—R. G. WHITE (ed. ii): As often happened, Shakespeare's heedlessness led him to write the reverse of what he intended to write. [Inasmuch as this ‘often happens’ in Shakespeare, it is to be regretted that R. G. WHITE has not furnished us with at least a few examples.—ED.]

95.play: Of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.

96.play: RIVERS: She may, my lord, for--

97.play: GLOUCESTER: She may, Lord Rivers! why, who knows not so?

98.play: She may do more, sir, than denying that:

99.play: She may help you to many fair preferments,

100.play: And then deny her aiding hand therein,

101.play: And lay those honours on your high deserts.

102.play: What may she not? She may, yea, marry, may she--

104.play: GLOUCESTER: What, marry, may she! marry with a king,
notes: King] 105. This, of course, refers to Edward IV.

105.play: A bachelor, a handsome stripling too:
notes: stripling] 106. Skeat (Dict.): The sense is, one as thin as a strip, a growing lad not yet filled out. Compare ‘you tailor’s yard, you sheathe, you bow-case,’ 1 Henry IV: II, iv, 273.

106.play: I wis your grandam had a worser match.
notes: I wis] 107. Skeat (Dict.): It is to be particularly noted that the Middle English prefix i (= Anglo Saxon ge) is often written apart from the rest of the word, and with a capital letter. Hence, by the mistake of editors, it is sometimes printed ‘I wis,’ and explained to mean I know. Hence, further, the imaginary verb wis, to know, has found its way into our dictionaries. But it is pure fiction, the verb being wit. ‘I wis’ means certainly.

Grandam . . . worser match] 107. Vaughan (iii, p. 20): It is remarkable that Shakespeare, at first sight, seems to have here just missed a taunt which would have suited the language of Richard more precisely; for Lord Rivers’s mother did marry much beneath her, and his father was, in consequence, fined by Henry VI. for his presumption in achieving this alliance. His grandmother, on the contrary, did not make a derogatory match, although she married a man of still lower rank, in the person of his grandfather, John de Wydevill, who was a simple esquire of the county of Northampton. In the general sense of the terms, she did not make
worser] 107. For examples of double comparative, see ABBOTT, § 11.

107.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My Lord of Gloucester, I have too long borne

108.play: Your blunt upbraidings and your bitter scoffs:
