Notes], it will appear that Hastings has most pretensions to that which is most active, and not the tamer character Stanley.—MARSHALL: Perhaps the dramatist was right in allowing one of the Queen’s party to consent to the arrangement proposed by Gloucester, as, of course, they were not supposed to have any suspicion that an attack was going to be made on the prince.

138.play: Yet, since it is but green, it should be put

139.play: To no apparent likelihood of breach,

140.play: Which haply by much company might be urged:

141.play: Therefore I say with noble Buckingham,

142.play: That it is meet so few should fetch the prince.

144.play: GLOUCESTER: Then be it so; and go we to determine

145.play: Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.

146.play: Madam, and you, my mother, will you go
notes: Madam, and you my Sister] 151. Dyce here prefers the Quarto reading (see Text. Notes) since, ‘throughout the present scene at least, Gloucester evidently keeps up towards the Queen an appearance of due respect,—which would be not a little violated if here he addressed his mother first.’—Marshall: There is no disrespect to the Queen (who was not, be it remembered, queen regnant) in Gloucester addressing his mother, who was much the older lady, first; and the use of the term ‘sister’ seems to be intentional, Richard’s object being to inspire Elizabeth with confidence by seeming to treat her with a brother’s affection, and not ceremoniously as a subject. [See note on ‘sister,’ l. 108, above.—Ed.]

147.play: To give your censures in this weighty business?
notes: censures] 152. That is, opinions, counsels.

businesses] 152. Here to be pronounced, for the sake of the metre, as a trisyllable.—Spedding (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 18): [The omission, in the Folio, of the Quarto half line, ‘With all our hearts,’ is] by oversight, I presume. It cannot have been meant for correction.—Pickersgill (op. cit., p. 89): One instance out of many of the Corrector’s abhorrence of half lines. As the completion of them would involve too large a draft upon his inventive genius, he cuts the Gordian knot by leaving them out altogether.—Marshall: ‘Ans.’ [in the Quarto] might have been a misprint for Ambo; but, dramatically speaking, it is much better that the Queen and Duchess should make their exit without saying anything.

148.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: With all our harts.

150.play: BUCKINGHAM: My lord, whoever journeys to the Prince,
notes: the Prince] 158. Delius (Jahrbuch, vii, p. 151): Here again the anonymous corrector of the Quarto has seen fit to change the words of the poet. [See Text. Notes.] But Buckingham would have been the last to apply the royal title to the son of Edward who has heretofore been designated as ‘the Prince’ only, inasmuch as he had not yet been proclaimed King, or crowned.

151.play: For God's sake, let not us two be behind;

152.play: For, by the way, I'll sort occasion,

153.play: As index to the story we late talk'd of,
notes: Index] 157. That is, introduction, prelude. Compare IV, iii, 88; and for other examples, see Schmidt (Lex.).

154.play: To part the queen's proud kindred from the king.

155.play: GLOUCESTER: My other self, my counsel's consistory,
notes: Consistory] 159. Murray (N. E. D.): † 1. A place where councillors meet, a council-chamber. (Almost always as a translation of the corresponding French or Latin word, and never applied to anything English.) [The present line quoted.] —Delius: In the poem The Ghost of Richard III. Richard says: ‘My working head, my counsel’s consistory Debates how I might reign, the princes living.’ [Shakspere Soc. Reprint, p. 33.]

156.play: My oracle, my prophet! My dear cousin,

157.play: I, like a child, will go by thy direction.
notes: 1, as a childe, will go by thy direction] 161. The Cowden-Clarkes: This from that arch-schemer, Richard, shows his subtle mode of making men’s weaknesses subservient to his own views, since he affects to be guided by Buckingham’s superior ability in craft and strategy, of which he knew him to be proud. See Buckingham’s first speech in the dialogue between himself and Richard, Act III, sc. v.

158.play: Towards Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

1.play: [Enter two Citizens meeting]
notes: Scena Tertia.] 1. Cowden-Clarke (Sh. Char., p. 469): Some of Shakespeare’s most insignificant scenes abound with notable axioms and aphoristic wisdom. For example, lines 39–50. In this short and apparently unimportant scene the serene piety and resignation coming in contrast with the treasons and cruelty with which the whole argument of the drama is fraught, is conceived in the

2.play: First Citizen: Neighbour, well met: whither away so fast?

3.play: Second Citizen: I promise you, I scarcely know myself:

4.play: Hear you the news abroad?

5.play: First Citizen: Ay, that the king is dead.

6.play: Second Citizen: Bad news, by'r lady; seldom comes the better:
notes: seldome comes the better] 9. REED: A proverbial saying, taken notice of in the English Courtier and Country Gentleman, 1586, sign, B: ‘—as the proverbe sayth, seldome come the better. Val. That proverb indeed is auncient, and for the most part true.’—MALONE: This passage, quoted by Reed, proves that there

7.play: I fear, I fear 'twill prove a troublous world.

8.play: [Enter another Citizen]

9.play: Third Citizen: Neighbours, God speed!

10.play: First Citizen: Give you good morrow, sir.

11.play: Third Citizen: Doth this news hold of good King Edward's death?

12.play: Second Citizen: Ay, sir, it is too true; God help the while!
notes: God helpe the while] 15. For other examples of this use of ‘while,’ see ABBOTT, § 137.

13.play: Third Citizen: Then, masters, look to see a troublous world.

14.play: First Citizen: No, no; by God's good grace his son shall reign.

15.play: Third Citizen: Woe to the land that's govern'd by a child!
notes: Woe . . . Childe] 18. STEEVENS: ‘Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child.’—Ecclesiastes, x, 16.—WORDSWORTH (Sh.’s Knowledge, etc.): That it is an unhappy thing for a country when its king is under age is a thought which might occur to many minds; but that the thought should be expressed in words so precisely parallel could not have happened without actual contact of the mind of the one writer with the mind of the other. Among the countless marvels of Shakespeare’e mind it is not the least remarkable that he appears equally at home in regard to matters that must have been alien from his own experience and to those that came within it.—WRIGHT: This text is quoted again by Buckingham in his speech at the Guildhall, ‘Wo to that realme whose kyng is a child’ (Hall, p. 371), and in his conversation with Morton, Bishop of Ely, ‘I remembred an olde prouerbe worthy of memorye, that often ruithe the realme, where chyldren rule, and women gouerne’ (ibid., p. 386). [Is it not possible that the use of this phrase by the Chronicler prompted Shakespeare to its present application quite as much as the verse in Ecclesiastes!—Ed.]

16.play: Second Citizen: In him there is a hope of government,

17.play: That in his nonage council under him,
notes: nonage] 20. WRIGHT: That is, minority. Cotgrave has: ‘Pupillarité: f. Nonage.’

18.play: And in his full and ripen'd years himself,

19.play: No doubt, shall then and till then govern well.

20.play: First Citizen: So stood the state when Henry the Sixth
notes: Henry] 23. WRIGHT: ‘Henry’ must here be a trisyllable.

21.play: Was crown'd in Paris but at nine months old.

22.play: Third Citizen: Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot;

23.play: For then this land was famously enrich'd

24.play: With politic grave counsel; then the king

25.play: Had virtuous uncles to protect his grace.

26.play: First Citizen: Why, so hath this, both by the father and mother.

27.play: Third Citizen: Better it were they all came by the father,

28.play: Or by the father there were none at all;

29.play: For emulation now, who shall be nearest,

30.play: Will touch us all too near, if God prevent not.

31.play: O, full of danger is the Duke of Gloucester!

32.play: And the queen's sons and brothers haught and proud:
notes: haught] 35. WRIGHT: That is, haughty. Compare ‘Clifford and the haught Northumberland,’ 3 Hen. VI: II, i, 169. [Also Rich. II: IV, i, 254: ‘No Lord of thine, thou haught, insulting man.’]

33.play: And were they to be ruled, and not to rule,
notes: And were they to be rul’d] 36. ABBOTT (§ 105): Here, at first sight, but seems required instead of ‘and.’ But ‘and were they’ means if indeed they were. (Compare ‘And if they live I hope I need not fear,’ III, ii, 165.)

34.play: This sickly land might solace as before.
notes: solace] 37. That is, be happy, take delight; compare ‘One thing to rejoice, and solace in,’ Rom. & Jul., IV, v, 47; also Cymb., I, vi, 86.

35.play: First Citizen: Come, come, we fear the worst; all shall be well.

36.play: Third Citizen: When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks;

37.play: When great leaves fall, the winter is at hand;

38.play: When the sun sets, who doth not look for night?

39.play: Untimely storms make men expect a dearth.
notes: makes] 42. For other examples of the third person plural in s, see Shakespeare passim.

40.play: All may be well; but, if God sort it so,

41.play: 'Tis more than we deserve, or I expect.

42.play: Second Citizen: Truly, the souls of men are full of dread:

43.play: Ye cannot reason almost with a man
notes: You cannot reason (almost)] 46. Compare ‘Would you imagine or almost believe,’ III, v, 40.
reason] 46. See I, iv, 157.

44.play: That looks not heavily and full of fear.

45.play: Third Citizen: Before the times of change, still is it so:

46.play: By a divine instinct men's minds mistrust
notes: instinct] 49. To be pronounced, through Latin influence, with the accent on the second syllable; compare ‘Háth, by instinct, knówledge from óther’s eyes.’ — 2 Hen. IV: I, i, 86, or see ABBOTT, § 490.

47.play: Ensuing dangers; as by proof, we see
notes: Pursuing] 50. WRIGHT: The Folios read 'pursuing danger,' although in F, the catchword on the previous page is 'ensuing.'

48.play: The waters swell before a boisterous storm.

49.play: But leave it all to God. whither away?
notes: But leaue it all to God] 52. MRS GRIFFITHS (p. 315): Nothing can demonstrate the investigating faculties of Shakespeare more than [the foregoing scene]. He never lived in any times of commotion himself, therefore the particular knowledge he there shows, in the general nature of such a crisis, must be owing more to philosophy than experience; rather to his own reflection, than any knowledge of history. I speak with regard to the English writers only on such subjects; who were all, before his time, most barren of observation and maxim. And as to the Greek and Roman historiographers, who were rich in both, the invidious Commentators of our Poet have denied him any manner of acquaintance with such outlandish literati; and I ... have joined issue with them in this particular. For learning gives no talents, but only supplies the faculty of showing them; and this he could do, without any foreign assistance.

50.play: Second Citizen: Marry, we were sent for to the justices.

51.play: Third Citizen: And so was I: I'll bear you company.

1.play: [Enter the ARCHBISHOP OF YORK, young YORK, QUEEN ELIZABETH, and the DUCHESS OF YORK]
notes: Steevens] 1. Shakespeare does not always attend to the propriety of his own alterations. As historical truth must be violated whichever reading be chosen, I am content with such an arrangement as renders the versification smoothest. Where sense cannot claim a preference, a casting vote may be safely given in favour of sound.

2.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Last night, I hear, they lay at Northampton;

3.play: At Stony-Stratford will they be to-night:

4.play: To-morrow, or next day, they will be here.

5.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I long with all my heart to see the prince:

6.play: I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

7.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But I hear, no; they say my son of York

8.play: Hath almost overta'en him in his growth.

9.play: YORK: Ay, mother; but I would not have it so.

10.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Why, my young cousin, it is good to grow.
notes: good Cosin . . . good to grow] 10. This rather awkward repetition of 'good,'

11.play: YORK: Grandam, one night, as we did sit at supper,

12.play: My uncle Rivers talk'd how I did grow

13.play: More than my brother: 'Ay,' quoth my uncle

14.play: Gloucester,

15.play: 'Small herbs have grace, great weeds do grow apace:'
notes: Small Herbes . . . grow apace] 15. WRIGHT: Compare Heywood’s Proverbs: ‘Ill weede growth fast, that is showyng In the show of thy fast growyng.’—Spen. Soc. reprint, p. 164. [Also Ray’s Proverbs: ‘Ill weeds grow apace,’ p. 428.]

16.play: And since, methinks, I would not grow so fast,
notes: since] 16. ABBOTT (§ 62): ‘Since’ is here used for since then, like our ever since. Compare a transitional use of ‘since’ between an adverb and conjunction in ‘Waverly,’ or, ’tis Sixty Years since.’ Omit ‘’tis,’ and ‘since’ becomes an adverb.

17.play: Because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

18.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold

19.play: In him that did object the same to thee;

20.play: He was the wretched'st thing when he was young,
notes: the wretched’st thing] 20. JOHNSON: ‘Wretched’ is here used in a sense yet retained in familiar language, for paltry, pitiful, being below expectation.—RITSON: Rather, the weakest, most puny, least thriving.

21.play: So long a-growing and so leisurely,

22.play: That, if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

23.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Why, madam, so, no doubt, he is.

24.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I hope he is; but yet let mothers doubt.

25.play: YORK: Now, by my troth, if I had been remember'd,
notes: if I had beene remembred] 25. For other examples of this construction, see ABBOTT, § 295, or SCHMIDT, Lex., s. v. Remembered.

26.play: I could have given my uncle's grace a flout,

27.play: To touch his growth nearer than he touch'd mine.

28.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: How, my pretty York? I pray thee, let me hear it.

29.play: YORK: Marry, they say my uncle grew so fast

30.play: That he could gnaw a crust at two hours old
notes: he could gnaw a crust at two houres old] 30. ‘It is for trowth reported . . . that hee [Richard] came into the world with the feete forwarde . . . and (as the same runneth) also not vntoothed.’—More, p. 8.

31.play: 'Twas full two years ere I could get a tooth.

32.play: Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

33.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I pray thee, pretty York, who told thee this?

34.play: YORK: Grandam, his nurse.

35.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: His nurse! why, she was dead ere thou wert born.

36.play: YORK: If 'twere not she, I cannot tell who told me.
notes: I cannot tell who told me] 36. Cowden-Clarke (Sh. Char., p. 462): He had heard his mother say it. She rebukes him for being ‘too shrewd.’ Here is a ‘touch of caution in the character of the young York, the natural birth of surrounding plots and machinations. [See note by Hunter, l. 40.]—Barnard: Apparently he had overheard it in conversation not meant for his ears, but archly puts his Grandam off by replying ‘His nurse.’

37.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: A parlous boy: go to, you are too shrewd.
notes: parlous] 37. Murray (N. E. D.): A syncopated form of perilous, . . .found from fourteenth century alongside of the fuller forms, but since seventeenth century more or less archaic in literary use; common dialectally from Durham to Hampshire. 2. Dangerously cunning, clever, eager, etc.; keen, shrewd. [The present line quoted.]
shrew’d] 37. Skeat (Did.): The older sense is malicious, mischievous, scolding or shrew-like, as in: [‘O, when she’s angry, she is keen and shrewd.’]—Mid. N. Dream, III, ii, 323.

38.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Good madam, be not angry with the child.
notes: gracious Madam] 24. WALKER (Crit., i, 289) considers this repetition in the Folio to be due to the eye of the transcriber having caught the word ‘gracious’ in the preceding line. He furnishes many examples of lines wherein a like substitution may have occurred.

Dut. Good Madam, be not angry] 38. Hunter (Illust., ii, 87): This passage affords another illustration of the superiority of the text of the Folios over that of the Quartos in this play. [In the Ff this line] is given to the Duchess of York, with whom it is much more in character. The poet seems to have intended that we should understand that the boy had on some occasion overheard his mother say what he repeats concerning his uncle, and she is disturbed to find that what

39.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Pitchers have ears.
notes: Pitchers have ears] 39. MALONE: Shakespeare has not quoted this proverbial saying correctly. It appears from A Dialogue both Pleasant and Pietyfull, by William Bulleyne, 1564, that the old proverb is this: ‘Small pitchers have great ears.’—RITSON: Compare ‘Pitchers have ears, and I have many servants.’—Tam. of Shr., IV, iv, 52. [BREWER (s. v. Pitcher) explains this proverbial saying as referring to the fact that ‘the handles or ears of small jugs are out of proportion to their size.’]

40.play: [Enter a Messenger]
notes: Enter a Messenger] 40. CAMBRIDGE EDD. (Note x.): We have followed the Folios in reading ‘a Messenger,’ and in assigning the speeches that follow to him rather than to the Marques Dorset as in the Quartos. The change must have been deliberate, and as the Queen does not greet the person who brings the intelligence, and expresses no anxiety for his safety when she herself is going to sanctuary, it seems more proper that the messenger should be one of inferior rank than one so nearly connected with the Queen. His ignorance of the cause of the arrest of the nobles and the terms in which he speaks of them are in keeping with the character of a messenger. In IV, i, the Queen apparently meets Dorset for the first time since Richard’s designs were disclosed, and passionately urges his escape.—DANIEL (p. xvii): For the service of the stage and to economize a ‘Messenger,’ Dorset is made [in the Quartos] to deliver this part, and is thereby placed in a most incongruous position. It is impossible to read the part given to him in the Quarto without at once perceiving that it was never originally intended for him: the ‘Messenger’ of the Folio was clearly its first exponent.

41.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Here comes a messenger. What news?

42.play: Messenger: Such news, my lord, as grieves me to unfold.

43.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: How fares the prince?

44.play: Messenger: Well, madam, and in health.

45.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What is thy news then?

46.play: Messenger: Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret,

47.play: With them Sir Thomas Vaughan, prisoners.

48.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Who hath committed them?

notes: Glouster and Buckingham] 50. WRIGHT: At the time of the king’s death Buckingham was on the Welsh Marches and Richard at York. They met at Northampton, each with a strong body of horse, on the day that the young king left for Stony Stratford.

Arch. For what offence?] 51. JOHNSON: This question is given to the Archbishop, but the Messenger plainly speaks to the Queen or the Duchess.—MALONE: The editor of the Folio altered lady [as in the Quarto] to ‘lord’ in l. 56; but it is more probable that the compositor prefixed Car. (the designation of the Archbishop in the Quarto) to the words: ‘For what offence?’ instead of Qu. than that lady should have been printed in the subsequent speech instead of ‘lord.’ Compositors always keep the names of the interlocutors in each scene ready-composed for use; and hence mistakes sometimes arise.—DYCE (ed. i): The messenger in replying above to the Archbishop, calls him ‘my Lord’; later the Archbishop addresses the Queen as ‘my gracious Lady,’ l. 68.—MARSHALL: Setting aside the fact that both Quartos and Folios have ‘my gracious lady’ in l. 56, the epithet ‘gracious’ has been applied to the Queen, l. 24, and therefore the supposition that lady was a misprint in the Folio for ‘lord’ can hardly be entertained.

52.play: Messenger: The sum of all I can, I have disclosed;

53.play: Why or for what these nobles were committed
notes: Why, or for what] 53. ABBOTT (§ 75): A distinction seems here to be drawn between ‘why’ and ‘for what.’ ‘Why’ refers, perhaps, to the past cause, ‘for what,’ to the future object.

54.play: Is all unknown to me, my gracious lady.

55.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Ay me, I see the downfall of our house!
notes: the ruine of my House] 55. DELIUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 160): Here, partly through carelessness, partly through misunderstanding, the anonymous corrector of the Quarto changes ‘my house’ to our house.

56.play: The tiger now hath seized the gentle hind;

notes: Insulting tyranny] 57. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. Insult, v): 'Insultare may be viewed either as frequentative of insultre, to leap upon, ... or as compound of in + saltare, frequentative of saltire. Cotgrave has: "Insulter, to insult, crow, vaunt, or triumph over; to wrong, reproach, affront; contemne; also to rebound, rejoyce at, leape for joy."'—The phrase 'insulting tyranny' occurs also in 1 Hen. VI: IV, vii, 19, where it is applied to death.

Jutt] 57. WRIGHT: To jet is commonly to strut, to walk proudly, to throw the body about in an affected manner; and hence it has been supposed to have in this passage the secondary sense of to be ostentatious. But jet and 'jut'... are the same in origin, and signify to stick out, project, and so to encroach upon. So Cotgrave interprets the French lettée, 'a lettie or iuttie, a bearing out or leaning ouer in buildings.' Compare 'Think you not how dangerous It is to jet upon a prince's right?' Tit. And., II, i, 64. And again in the old play, Sir Thomas More: 'It is hard when Englishmens pacience must be thus jetted on by straungers.'—Sh. Soc. reprint, p. 2.

58.play: Upon the innocent and aweless throne:

60.play: I see, as in a map, the end of all.

61.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Accursed and unquiet wrangling days,

62.play: How many of you have mine eyes beheld!

63.play: My husband lost his life to get the crown;

64.play: And often up and down my sons were toss'd,

65.play: For me to joy and weep their gain and loss:

66.play: And being seated, and domestic broils

68.play: Make war upon themselves; blood against blood,

notes: O preposterous!] 69. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-'76; p. 42): I cannot help suspecting that the 'O' was meant to be struck out. I should indeed have thought Pope's conjecture of 'O most preposterous' the more probable; nor should I have objected to the alexandrine in this place, though such alexandrines belong

70.play: And frantic outrage, end thy damned spleen;

71.play: Or let me die, to look on death no more!
notes: looke on earth no more] 71. THEOBALD (ed. ii): By the reading of the Quartos the thought is finely and properly improved. The old Dutchess had no antipathy to the world, or looking upon the earth in general: Her complaints are restrain’d to the calamitous Days she had seen, the Miseries and Slaughters of civil Wars at home: during the Process of which she had been witness to so many Murthers, such Havock and Destruction; that she very reasonably wishes that such Outrage may cease, or that she may not live to see any more Friends massacred.—CAPELL (ii, 180): The thought of this line, which wants no explaining, recalls another of great singularity that makes the close of a Sonnet in this Poet’s collection . . .: ‘So shalt thou feed on death, that feeds on men And Death once dead, there’s no more dying then.’ [Sonnet cxlvi.]—DELIUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 154): If this passage be taken in connection with the rest of the speech of the Duchess, it will be seen that the reading of the Folio, ‘earth,’ though apparently commonplace, is nevertheless the more correct. The many horrors of war, which, for so many years, have passed before her eyes, have so wearied her that she prays for death to be free from the sight of her own country—the earth.—SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 20) classifies the Folio change, ‘earth,’ among the ‘alterations not intended by Shakespeare’; and adds: ‘An injudicious correction, I should say, but for the origin of which it is not necessary to seek beyond the printing-office.’

72.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Come, come, my boy; we will to sanctuary.
notes: we will to Sanctuary] 72. ROLFE: That is, the sanctuary at Westminster. This old building stood where Westminster Hospital now stands (then within the precincts of the Abbey), and retained its privileges as a refuge for criminals until the dissolution of the monastery, and for debtors until 1602. This was the second time that Elizabeth had fled thither; the first having been in 1470, when with her mother and her three daughters she was the guest of Abbot Milling until the birth of her son Edward, Nov. 1 of that year.—WHEATLEY (iii, 208): The privileges survived the Reformation, and the bulk of the houses which composed the precinct were not taken down till 1750.

74.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I'll go along with you.

75.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: You have no cause.

76.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: My gracious lady, go;

77.play: And thither bear your treasure and your goods.

78.play: For my part, I'll resign unto your grace

79.play: The seal I keep: and so betide to me
notes: and so betide to me] 79. ABBOTT (§ 397): ‘Betide’ may here be used impersonally. But perhaps ‘so’ is loosely used as a demonstrative for such fortune, in the same way in which as assumes the force of a relative [in ‘I have not from your eyes that gentleness As I was wont to have.’—Jul. Cas., I, ii, 33].

80.play: As well I tender you and all of yours!

81.play: Come, I'll conduct you to the sanctuary.
notes: Go, Ile conduct you, etc.] 81. WEBB: This powerful scene is a fitting close to the Second Act. It should be observed that its strength lies, not in the interest of the characters, which are in themselves relatively insignificant, nor in any special elevation of diction or thought, but merely in the action, the surprising ‘reversal of fortune,’ the change in the situation. The scene opens with a company of Lords and Ladies engaged in elegant trifling, in witty comments on the utterances of a precocious child. Suddenly a messenger comes in with his news and all is changed. In the imprisonment of Rivers and Gray the company are not slow to perceive a deadly blow struck at themselves. The gloomy vaticinations of Queen Elizabeth, together with the agonized exclamations of the Duchess, are a fitting prelude to the disasters which are soon to fall on them and their party. Moreover, it will be noticed that though Richard is not himself present, his influence is paramount. The scene opens with caustic allusions to his deformities, only to close with a remarkable instance of his power. Perhaps no scene more clearly shows how Richard III. is a one-man play.

1.play: [The trumpets sound. Enter the young PRINCE EDWARD, GLOUCESTER, BUCKINGHAM, CARDINAL, CATESBY, and others]

2.play: BUCKINGHAM: Welcome, sweet prince, to London, to your chamber.

3.play: GLOUCESTER: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign

4.play: The weary way hath made you melancholy.

5.play: PRINCE EDWARD: No, uncle; but our crosses on the way

6.play: Have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy

7.play: I want more uncles here to welcome me.

8.play: GLOUCESTER: Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years

9.play: Hath not yet dived into the world's deceit

10.play: Nor more can you distinguish of a man

11.play: Than of his outward show; which, God he knows,

12.play: Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart.
notes: iumpeth] 12. For examples of ‘jump,’ meaning to agree, see SCHMIDT (Lex. b.).

13.play: Those uncles which you want were dangerous;

14.play: Your grace attended to their sugar'd words,

15.play: But look'd not on the poison of their hearts :

16.play: God keep you from them, and from such false friends!

17.play: PRINCE EDWARD: God keep me from false friends! but they were none.

18.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, the mayor of London comes to greet you.

19.play: [Enter the Lord Mayor and his train]

20.play: Lord Mayor: God bless your grace with health and happy days!

21.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I thank you, good my lord; and thank you all.

22.play: I thought my mother, and my brother York,

23.play: Would long ere this have met us on the way

24.play: Fie, what a slug is Hastings, that he comes not
notes: Slug] 24. WALKER (Crit., ii, 346): To slug, active and neuter, connected with sluggard. Slug for sluggard; also for anything that obstructs motion or progress. [The present line quoted.]

25.play: To tell us whether they will come or no!

26.play: [Enter HASTINGS]

27.play: BUCKINGHAM: And, in good time, here comes the sweating lord.
notes: sweating] 27. WEBB suggests that there is here perhaps a pun intended on ‘Hastings.’

28.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Welcome, my lord: what, will our mother come?

29.play: HASTINGS: On what occasion, God he knows, not I,

30.play: The queen your mother, and your brother York,

31.play: Have taken sanctuary: the tender prince

32.play: Would fain have come with me to meet your grace,

33.play: But by his mother was perforce withheld.

34.play: BUCKINGHAM: Fie, what an indirect and peevish course
notes: peevish] 34. See I, iii, 204.

35.play: Is this of hers! Lord cardinal, will your grace
notes: Lord Cardinal!] 35. WRIGHT: In Holinshed (p. 717, col. i) it is Richard and not Buckingham who suggests that the Cardinal should take upon him the office of persuading the Queen to give up her son. ... In More’s narrative, which Holinshed copies, it is left uncertain who the ‘Lord Cardinal’ is, and in the sequel we find that the Archbishop of York undertakes the mission. [See note by CHURCHILL, l. 4, supra; also Appendix: Source of Plot.]—DANIEL (Time Analysis, p. 328, foot-note): The prelate of II, iv, in the Folio is an Archbishop; in the Quarto

36.play: Persuade the queen to send the Duke of York

37.play: Unto his princely brother presently?

38.play: If she deny, Lord Hastings, go with him,

39.play: And from her jealous arms pluck him perforce.

40.play: CARDINAL: My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory
notes: Buckingham] 40. For examples where polysyllabic names in the middle of a line receive but one accent, see Abbott, § 469 (p. 354); compare III, vii, 236.

41.play: Can from his mother win the Duke of York,

42.play: Anon expect him here; but if she be obdurate

43.play: To mild entreaties, God in heaven forbid

44.play: We should infringe the holy privilege

45.play: Of blessed sanctuary! not for all this land
notes: Sanctuarie] 45. Walker (Vers. 163) gives several examples, besides the present line, wherein ‘sanctuary’ is, for the sake of the metre, to be pronounced as a disyllable. He adds: ‘In old times the words, like the thing itself, was in frequent use, and, like other familiar terms, became shortened for convenience sake. In Sir Henry Ellis’s Letters Illustrative of English History, as quoted, Athenaeum, No 973, p. 625, col. 3, letter from Lawrence Stubbs to Cardinal Wolsey, the sanctuary men are called in three places Sentuary [sic.] men. Santry still exists as a family name; so, by the way, does “Sanctuary.”’

46.play: Would I be guilty of so deep a sin.

47.play: BUCKINGHAM: You are too senseless--obstinate, my lord,
notes: sencelesse obstinate] 47. Collier (ed. ii): The old reading makes the

ceremonious, and traditional] 48. WARBURTON: ‘Ceremonious’ for superstitious; ‘traditional’ for adherent to old customs. [Is not the scansion of this line difficult, or almost impossible, unless Walker’s rule (Crit., iii, 73), of the elision of the middle e in ‘ceremonious,’ be adopted? It would then read: Too cer | moni | ous and | tradi | tional. And yet even this is harsh. In making a quadrisyllable of ‘ceremonious,’ a contraction of the last two syllables -ious occurs almost instinctively.—Ed.]

49.play: Weigh it but with the grossness of this age,
notes: the grossenesse of this Age] 49. WARBURTON: But the more ‘gross,’ that is, the more superstitious, the age was, the stronger would be the imputation of violated sanctuary. The speaker resolves this question in the negative, because it could be claimed by those only whose actions necessitated them to fly thither; or by those who had an understanding to demand it, neither of which could be an infant’s case: the first line, then, should be read: ‘the greatness of his age,’ that is, the Duke of York’s. The corrupted reading of the old Quarto is something nearer the true: ‘the greatness of his age.’—JOHNSON: Warburton’s emendation is very plausible, yet the common reading may stand: That is, compare the act of seizing him with the ‘gross’ and licentious practices of these times, it will not be considered as a violation of sanctuary, for you may give such reasons as men are now used to admit.—HEATH (p. 294): A mistake in the pointing of the former editions misled Warburton to misunderstand the construction, and in consequence the meaning, of this passage, and occasioned his having recourse to his emendation. This is evident from his own reasoning in support of it. Put but a semicolon at the end of the preceding line, and a full stop at the end of this, and then we may fairly reinstate the common reading clear of all objection. That is, you consider it only according to that gross undistinguishing superstition which prevails in the present age.—CAPELL (ii, 181): The address’d person is charg’d in the speech’s entry with being senseless-obstinate in his attachment to church forms and matters of meer traditional establishment, such as (it is insinuated) are rights of sanctuary generally: the extension of that right in the present case is the next topic; and he is told, in this line and the next, that it does not extend to children, persons of green years; and the reasons why it extends not are set forth in the lines that follow: This is the speech’s chain; and so natural and consequential that the editor cannot persuade himself—there will be a person in future who shall look on what has obtain’d in many copies other than as itself a corruption likewise transmitted down from the first.—MALONE: Warburton is not accurate. The original Quarto, 1597 and the two subsequent Quartos, as well as the Folio, all read ‘grossnesse.’ Greatness is the corrupt reading of a late Quarto of no authority, printed in 1623

] 49. HUNTER (Illust., ii, 88): If for ‘this age’ we read his age, we should have all the effect of Warburton’s admirable suggestion, with less disturbance of the text, ‘grossness’ being taken as equivalent to greatness in its comparative sense, when used as equivalent to size or extent.—R. G. WHITE: That is (and very clearly to my apprehension) weigh it but with the gross judgement, the blunted perception of this age.—The COWDEN-CLARKES: This may mean, if the lawlessness of those who usually claim sanctuary be compared with the young prince’s innocence, which needs no such protection, then there can be no breaking of sanctuary in taking him thence. In the account given in Hall’s Chronicle, whence Shakespeare probably derived the groundwork for the present scene, Buckingham enlarges upon the vices of those who require the safety of sanctuary in contradistinction to the royal child’s immaculacy, so as to give colour to the above interpretation.—LETTSON (ap. Dyce ii): The context seems to require a word like cunning or knowledge; for the meaning is apparently, examine the matter well, the superior knowledge and cleverness of the present age, and you will find you can seize the child without breaking sanctuary.—WRIGHT: Johnson in reality gives two interpretations, which turn upon the different meanings of which ‘weigh with’ is capable: weigh this act against the violent practices of these times, and so, compare it with them; or, weigh it as such actions are weighed in this gross age, and so, estimate it by that standard.—VAUGHAN (iii, 69): [In III, vi, the] Scrivener says, in pointing out the inconsistency between the time apparently given to the preparation of the indictment against Hastings, and the time actually required for it: ‘Who so gross That seeth not this palpable device?’ Where Holinshed says: ‘Every child might well perceive that it was prepared before.’ It is clear, therefore, that in Shakespeare’s terminology ‘grossness’ is characteristic of childhood, and an equivalent of childishness. ‘Grossness,’ therefore, applies here to the child’s age, and not to the world’s age. [SCHMIDT (Lex.) gives numerous passages wherein ‘grossness’ does not necessarily mean childishness, but rather dullness, stupidity. Because Shakespeare elsewhere paraphrases a certain word in Holinshed, in a certain way, is it quite justifiable to apply that paraphrase to the word when used by him in a wholly different connection?—ED.]—WEBB: The obvious meaning of this seems to be, the times are grown so bad that strong measures must be taken; the privilege of sanctuary has been abused and must be suspended with a high hand, if wrongdoers are to be brought to justice.

50.play: You break not sanctuary in seizing him.

51.play: The benefit thereof is always granted

52.play: To those whose dealings have deserved the place,

53.play: And those who have the wit to claim the place:

54.play: This prince hath neither claim'd it nor deserved it;

55.play: And therefore, in mine opinion, cannot have it:
notes: And therefore] 55. Dyce (ed. ii) upholds the omission of this ‘and’ in the Second Folio, saying that the ‘eye of the transcriber, or of the original compositor, had caught the word from the second line above.’ Was this roving eye also responsible for other repetitions in the lines preceding and following this? Lines 61, 62, both end with ‘the place’; lines 63, 64, with ‘it’; lines 65, 66, with ‘there.’ Is it not possible that the editor of the Second Folio omitted ‘and’ for the sake of the metre?—Ed.

56.play: Then, taking him from thence that is not there,
notes: taking him from thence, that is not there] 56. The Cowden-Clarkes: The effect of this paradoxical sentence is most characteristic; it is in keeping with the sophistry used by Buckingham, and with the crooked policy of his casuistry. [Shakespeare is here, I think, paraphrasing the speech of the Duke of Buckingham as given by More and copied by Hall and Holinshed: ‘[reason] is not fullye so farrefoorth, as may serue to lette vs of the fetcheynge foorth of this noble manne to hys honoure and wealthe, oute of that place in whiche he neither is, nor canne be a Sancutary manne.’—p. 46.—Ed.]

57.play: You break no privilege nor charter there.

58.play: Oft have I heard of sanctuary men;

59.play: But sanctuary children ne'er till now.
notes: Sancutuarie children] 59. See Appendix: Source of Plot, for origin of this speech.—Besant (p. 155): The following note by Machyn, [the City Chronicler of the Tudor period], presents one of the last appearances of the old Sanctuary customs: ‘The vi day of December the abbot of Westminster went a procession with his convent; before him went all the sanctuary men with crosse keys upon their garments, and after whent iij for murder: one was the Lord Dacre’s sone of the Northe . . . and a boy that kyld a byge boye that sold papers and pryntyd bokes, . . . the boy was one of the chylderyn that was at the scholI ther in the abbey.’—Diary, p. 121. [According to the foregoing, ‘sanctuary children’—though spoken of by More as almost unknown—were perhaps not so unusual a sight to the Londoner of Shakespeare’s time.—Ed.]

60.play: CARDINAL: My lord, you shall o'er-rule my mind for once.

61.play: Come on, Lord Hastings, will you go with me?

63.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Good lords, make all the speedy haste you may.

65.play: Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother come,

66.play: Where shall we sojourn till our coronation?

67.play: GLOUCESTER: Where it seems best unto your royal self.
notes: it think’st] 67. Wright (note on think’st thee, Hamlet, V, ii, 63): The word

68.play: If I may counsel you, some day or two
notes: some day or two] 68. For other examples of ‘some’ used with a numeral adjective, qualifying a singular noun of time, see ABBOTT, § 21.

69.play: Your highness shall repose you at the Tower:
notes: Your Highnesse . . . at the Tower] 69. LEGGE (i, 210): To modern ears the very name of the Tower is ominous; but it was otherwise in the fifteenth century, when it was a royal palace, the state apartments of which continued to be occupied by the sovereign until the days of Elizabeth. Edward IV. had been lodged there before his coronation, and had frequently kept his Court there with great magnificence. The Tower had been selected because the Prince would there be less accessible to the influences from which it was deemed important to separate him. So far from its being intended to make it a place of captivity, the Croyland Chronicler informs us that ‘a discussion took place in the Council’ (‘in Senatu,’ by which, as elsewhere, he doubtless means in the Council, and not, as has been invariably represented, in Parliament), ‘about removing the King to some place where fewer restrictions would be imposed upon him.’ It had long been the custom for the sovereigns to take up their residence at the Tower for a short time previous to their coronation, ‘and thence they generally proceeded in state through the city to be crowned at Westminster.’ All authorities are agreed in representing Richard as acting with the tenderness which his nephew’s temperament and circumstances demanded. He is even said to have besought him on his knees to banish fear, to confide in his affection, and rely on the necessity of those summary measures which occasioned him such painful forebodings. The removal to the Tower seems to have taken place about the 19th of May, and the preparations for the coronation were hurried forward.

70.play: Then where you please, and shall be thought most fit

71.play: For your best health and recreation.

72.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I do not like the Tower, of any place.
notes: of any place] 72. ABBOTT (§ 409): This (which is a thoroughly Greek idiom, though independent in English) is illustrated by Milton’s famous line: ‘The fairest of her daughters Eve.’ The line is a confusion of two constructions: ‘Eve fairer than all her daughters,’ and ‘Eve fairest of all women.’ So ‘I dislike the tower more than any place’ and ‘most of all places’ becomes, ‘of any place.’—WRIGHT: Compare, for this construction, ‘Of all men else I have avoided thee.’—Macbeth, V, viii, 4; and ‘That York is most unmeet of any man.’—3 Hen. VI: I, iii, 167.

73.play: Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?
notes: Did Iulius Caesar build that place] 73. WHEATLEY (iii, 392): There is no authority to confirm tradition in the remote antiquity assigned to the Tower. No part of the existing structure is of a date anterior to the keep, or the great square

74.play: BUCKINGHAM: He did, my gracious lord, begin that place;
notes: my gracious Lord] 74. MARSHALL objects to the omission of the epithet ‘gracious,’ proposed by Steevens [see Text. Notes], since ‘Buckingham does not elsewhere address Edward, the titular King, simply as "my Lord." Gloucester so does, but he, as prince of the blood, was entitled so to do.’

75.play: Which, since, succeeding ages have re-edified.
notes: re-edify’d] 75. WRIGHT: This word only occurs again in Tit. And., I, i, 351: ‘This monument five hundred years hath stood, Which I have sumptuously re-edified.’ Compare: ‘The ruin’d wals he did reedifye,’ Spenser, Faerie Queene, II, x, 46. ‘Edify’ was formerly not infrequently used in its literal sense as equivalent to build. So in Faerie Queene: ‘There was an holy chappell edifyde,’ I, i, 34. [CRAIGIE (N. E. D.) gives numerous examples of ‘re-edify,’ in the sense to rebuild, extending over a period from 1420 to 1680.]

76.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Is it upon record, or else reported

77.play: Successively from age to age, he built it?

78.play: BUCKINGHAM: Upon record, my gracious lord.

79.play: PRINCE EDWARD: But say, my lord, it were not register'd,

80.play: Methinks the truth should live from age to age,

notes: retayl’d to all posteritie] 81. WARBURTON: And so it is; and, by that means, like so many ‘retailed’ things, becomes adulterated. We should read ‘instail’d to all posterity’; which is finely and sensibly expressed, as if the truth was the natural inheritance of our children; which it is impiety to deprive them of.—JOHNSON: ‘Retail’d’ may signify diffused, dispersed.—M. MASON: ‘Retailed’ means handed down from one to another. Richard uses the word ‘retailed’ in the same sense in IV, iii, 352, where, speaking to the Queen of her daughter, he says: ‘To whom I will retail my conquest won.’

82.play: Even to the general all-ending day.
notes: generall ending] 82. MARSHALL: The omission of all [in the Folios, and all the Quartos but the First] very likely arose from the transcriber mistaking it for the final syllable of ‘general.’
So wise . . . neuer liue long] 83. STEEVENS: A proverbial line: ‘Is cadit

85.play: PRINCE EDWARD: What say you, uncle?

86.play: GLOUCESTER: I say, without characters, fame lives long.
notes: Characters] 86. WRIGHT: The noun has here the accent on the second syllable only in this passage in Shakespeare. In Hamlet, I, iii, 59, the verb has the same accent: 'And these few precepts in thy memory See thou character.'

88.play: Thus, like the formal vice, Iniquity,
notes: like the formall Vice, . . . two meanings in one word] 88. —HEATH (p. 297): Richard doth not, in this passage, seriously moralize at all, or even dream of so doing.... The term moralize is only introduced in allusion to the title of our old dramatic pieces, which were commonly called moralities, in which the Vice was always one of the shining characters.—STEEVENS: From the following stage direction, in Histriomastix, or The Player Whipt, 1610, it appears that ‘the Vice’ and ‘Iniquity’ were sometimes distinct personages: ‘Enter a roaring devil, with the Vice on his back, Iniquity in one hand, and Juventas in the other. Devil. Ho, ho, ho! these babes mine are all, The Vice, Iniquite, and Child Prodigal.’ [I. 281, et seq., ed. Simpson.] The following part of this note was obligingly communicated by the Rev. Mr Bowle, of Idmestone near Salisbury. I know no writer who gives so complete an account of this obsolete character as Archbishop Harsnet, in his Declaration of Popish Impostures, p. 114, Lond., 1608: ‘It was a pretty part (he tells us) in the old church-playes, when the nimble Vice would skip up nimbly like a jackanapes into the devil’s necke, and ride the devil a course, and belabour him with his wooden dagger, till he made him roare, whereat the people would laugh to see the devil so vice-haunted.’—UPTON (Var. ’21, Appendix, 248): The allusion here is to the Vice, a droll character in our old plays, accoutred with a long coat [see COLLIER, post], a cap with a pair of ass’s ears, and a dagger of lath. Shakespeare alludes to his buffoon appearance in Twelfth Night: ‘In a trice, like to the old Vice;—Who with dagger of lath, in his rage and his wrath, Cries, ah, ah! to the Devil,’ IV, ii, 134. In 2 Henry IV: III, ii, 343. Falstaff compares Shallow to a Vice’s dagger of Lath. In Hamlet, III, iv, 98, Hamlet calls his uncle ‘A vice of kings,’ i. e., a ridiculous representation of majesty. The iniquity was often the Vice in our moralities; and is introduced in Ben Jonson’s The Devil’s an Ass, and likewise mentioned in his Epigr. cxv: ‘Being no vitious person, but the Vice About the town, . . . Acts old Iniquity, and in the fit Of miming, gets th’ opinion of a wit.’ But a passage cited from his play [p. 10, ed. Gifford] will make the following observations more plain: Pug asks the devil ‘to lend him a Vice.’ ‘Satan. What Vice? . . . Pug. Why any: Fraud, Or Covetousness, or lady Vanity, Or old Iniquity. Sat. I’ll call him hither. Enter Iniquity. Ini. What is he calls upon me, and would seem to lack a Vice? Ere his words be half spoken, I am with him in a trice.’ Again in 1 Henry IV: II, iv, 499, where Hal, humourously characterizing Falstaff, calls him, ‘That reverend Vice, that great Iniquity, that father Ruffian, that Vanity of years,’ in allusion to this buffoon character.—MALONE: ‘Moralize’ as a verb active occurs in Rape of Luc.: ‘Nor could she moralize his wanton sight, More than his eyes were open to the light,’ ll. 104, 105. Where it means, to interpret, or investigate the latent meaning of his wanton looks, as in the present passage, it signifies either, to extract the double and latent meaning of one word or sentence, or to couch two meanings under one word or sentence. So ‘moral’ is used in Much Ado for a secret meaning: ‘You have some moral in this Benedictus,’ III, iv, 77. The word which Richard uses in a double sense is ‘live’, which in his former speech he had used literally, and in the present is used metaphorically. [Mason’s argument, that ‘word’ may here signify a whole sentence], is defective. Thorough in tournaments the motto on a

89.play: I moralize two meanings in one word.

90.play: PRINCE EDWARD: That Julius Caesar was a famous man;

91.play: With what his valour did enrich his wit,
notes: With what] 91. ABBOTT (§ 252) calls attention to this curious use of ‘what,’ meaning here, that with which.

92.play: His wit set down to make his valour live

93.play: Death makes no conquest of this conqueror;
notes: his Conqueror] 93. DELIUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 149): Death has not conquered Julius Cæsar, who may, much rather, be said to have conquered death, inasmuch as he still lives, at least, in fame. This fine thought the anonymous corrector of the Quarto so little understood that he altered ‘his’ to this. [That the Folio reading, although having so few advocates, is nevertheless to be preferred for the reasons given by DELIUS, is the opinion of the present ED.]

94.play: For now he lives in fame, though not in life.
notes: For now . . . not in Life] 94. COWDEN-CLARKE (Sh. Char., p. 463): Shakespeare not infrequently makes his children talk beyond their years, and at times almost greatly; nevertheless they still wear the childlike air and manner—so completely did he invest himself for the time in the spiritual garment of each character as he summoned it to the scene. The questions the little king puts to his uncle respecting Julius Caesar, and his precocious reflection upon the character of that great Roman, strictly harmonise with his forced maturity of mind, and yet the manner of his speech is youthful. Although here is sound philosophy, and that it is somewhat precocious in reflection, yet how perfectly is the language in which it is expressed that of a superior-minded child!

95.play: I'll tell you what, my cousin Buckingham,--

97.play: PRINCE EDWARD: An if I live until I be a man,
notes: And if] 97. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. And. C. conj. conditional = If.): This was a common use of Middle High German unde; ... It may have originated from ellipsis, as in the analogous use of so, e. g., ‘I’ll cross the sea, so it please my lord’ (Shakespeare); cf. ‘and it please’; or it may be connected with the introductory and in ‘And you are going?’ A direct development from the original prepositional sense, though a priori plausible, is on historical grounds improbable. Modern writers, chiefly since Horne Tooke, have treated this as a distinct word, writing it an, a spelling occasionally found circa 1600, especially in an’t, equivalent to and if.

98.play: I'll win our ancient right in France again,

99.play: Or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

notes: lightly] 100. BRADLEY (N. E. D. s. v.): † 6. b. As is apt to happen; commonly, often. [SCHMIDT (Lex.) quotes the present line as the only example of Shakespeare’s use of the word ‘lightly’ in the foregoing sense. Between the several subdivisions of meaning given by Bradley, e. g., ‘4. Easily, readily; 5. Quickly, swiftly; 6. As may easily happen. † 6. b. As is apt to happen’; besides the use

101.play: [Enter young YORK, HASTINGS, and the CARDINAL]
notes: Enter young York] 101. WRIGHT: In the history, the young Duke was brought to his uncle in the Star Chamber and afterwards taken to join his brother at the Bishop of London’s palace at St. Paul’s.

102.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, in good time, here comes the Duke of York.

103.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Richard of York! how fares our loving brother?

104.play: YORK: Well, my dread lord; so must I call you now.
notes: dearre Lord] 104. KNIGHT: The epithet dread [of Q1Q2] requires to be retained, for ‘dear Lord’ would not mark the new title by which York addresses his brother—‘Lord’ being the title by which York is himself subsequently named.—R. G. WHITE: [Dread Lord was a royal title, and] it is selected with noticeable tact, as the one which marks most strongly the change of relation between the little playfellows. ‘Great and manifold were the blessings, Most Dread Sovereign’—are the words in which the translators of our Bible began their Dedication to King James.—DELlUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 155): The formality in the speech of the prince to his younger brother is intended to be slightly jocose, and with the same spirit of jocosity the younger prince replies, addressing his brother indeed as his Lord, but yet qualifying his address with the familiar ‘dear.’ The anonymous corrector of the Quarto has completely destroyed the delicate formality of the first line, as well as the familiarity of the second, since he changes ‘noble’ to loving and ‘dear’ to dread. [That the Quarto reading dread is the more fitting is shown, I think, by the reply of the young king: ‘Too late he died that might have kept that title Which by his death hath lost much majesty.’ The title which has lost majesty in its new representative is presumably the royal one, and or not that of ‘dear Lord.’ The use of ‘our’ in the preceding line seems to indicate also the remembrance by the young king of his royal position.—Ed.]

105.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Ay, brother, to our grief, as it is yours:

106.play: Too late he died that might have kept that title,
notes: Too late he dy’d] 106. WARBURTON: That is, too lately, the loss is too fresh in our memory. [SCHMIDT (Lex. s. v. adv. 3) furnishes many examples of late used in this sense. KEIGHTLEY suggests that the words should be too soon [see Text. Notes], or too early, and adds: ‘This may perhaps be regarded as an instance of the substitution of a contrary term.’ See Expositor, p. 62, § 10, where a remarkable number of similar typographical errors is given.—Ed.]

107.play: Which by his death hath lost much majesty.

108.play: GLOUCESTER: How fares our cousin, noble Lord of York?

109.play: YORK: I thank you, gentle uncle. O, my lord,

110.play: You said that idle weeds are fast in growth
notes: You said, that idle Weeds, etc.] 110. COURTENAY (Comment., ii, 82) remarks that he can ‘find no warrant in the Chronicles’ for this ‘pert conversation of the Duke of York.’ But then the Chronicles are also silent in regard to the scene between Clarence and the Murderers; and also to the main part of the remorse of Edward for the death of his brother.—Ed.

111.play: The prince my brother hath outgrown me far.

113.play: YORK: And therefore is he idle?

114.play: GLOUCESTER: O, my fair cousin, I must not say so.

115.play: YORK: Then is he more beholding to you than I.
notes: beholding] 115. See II, i, 139, and note.

116.play: GLOUCESTER: He may command me as my sovereign;

117.play: But you have power in me as in a kinsman.

118.play: YORK: I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.
notes: give me this Dagger] 118. ABBOTT (p. 449) cites this line as an example of dramatic irony.—MARSHALL: The emendation which we have ventured to print is a very simple one. [Thus: ‘I pray you, uncle, give me this—(playing with Gloucester’s sword-belt—then touching the dagger) this dagger.’] It is probable that, if our conjecture is right, the transcriber might have overlooked the repetition of this. It is pretty certain, whether we insert the word this or not, that the speaker was intended to pause before naming his request; and it would seem, from the context, that Gloucester had no idea of what the little prince was going to ask for, and that he was rather relieved when he found that his request was a comparatively trifling one.—BARNARD (Notes & Queries, 23d May, 1896, p. 402): This may simply be meant to represent a natural request on the part of a pert, forward boy, or it may also involve an intimation that it were well that Gloucester should be rendered harmless by being disarmed. May it, however, further imply the disarming of Gloucester in another sense as well? For one of the most familiar amulets against the evil eye was a model of a sword or dagger; and there would be a touch of Shakespearean irony in thus proposing that Richard should provide his intended victim with a counter-charm against his own malignity. [See note by BARNARD, I, ii, 50.]

119.play: GLOUCESTER: My dagger, little cousin? with all my heart.

121.play: YORK: Of my kind uncle, that I know will give;

122.play: And being but a toy, which is no grief to give.
notes: being] 122. For other examples of a ‘contraction in words, wherein a light vowel is preceded by a heavy vowel or diphthong,’ see ABBOTT, § 470. Such a contraction, Abbott remarks, will explain this apparent Alexandrine.

123.play: GLOUCESTER: A greater gift than that I'll give my cousin.

124.play: YORK: A greater gift! O, that's the sword to it.

125.play: GLOUCESTER: A gentle cousin, were it light enough.

126.play: YORK: O, then, I see, you will part but with light gifts;

127.play: In weightier things you'll say a beggar nay.

128.play: GLOUCESTER: It is too heavy for your grace to wear.

129.play: YORK: I weigh it lightly, were it heavier.
notes: I weigh it lightly] 129. WARBURTON: That is, I should still esteem it but a trifling gift, were it heavier.

130.play: GLOUCESTER: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?
notes: my Weapon] 130. MARSHALL: Note the emphasis [on ‘my’]; Gloucester asks contemptuously: ‘Would you, child as you are, have my weapon, the sword with which I have done such mighty deeds?’ [This is Marshall’s own emphasis, and probably correct, but it is not the rhythmical emphasis. Is it possible to read the line with its due iambics, and make the ictus fall on ‘my’?—Ed.]

131.play: YORK: I would, that I might thank you as you call me.

134.play: PRINCE EDWARD: My Lord of York will still be cross in talk:

135.play: Uncle, your grace knows how to bear with him.

136.play: YORK: You mean, to bear me, not to bear with me:

137.play: Uncle, my brother mocks both you and me;

138.play: Because that I am little, like an ape,

139.play: He thinks that you should bear me on your shoulders.

140.play: BUCKINGHAM: With what a sharp-provided wit he reasons!
notes: sharpe provided wit] 140. COLLIER (Notes, etc., p. 330): The MS Corrector assures us that, although the intention of the dramatist is evident, a decided misprint has crept into the line: he reads, ‘sharply pointed wit.’—SINGER (Sh. Vind., 171): ‘Sharp provided’ could never be a misprint for sharply pointed, nor do they resemble in sound. The old reading, according to Baret, may well be continued: ‘provided of those things which he should saie.’ Interference, and especially such a violent change as the correctors propose, is entirely unnecessary.—COWDEN-CLARKE (Sh. Char., p. 468): The little York’s pert and sly taunts to his uncle Gloucester, in the presence of the young king, are most naturally managed, and as skilfully contrasted with the sweet, gentle sketch of his brother, whose premature thought has taken the character of pensive care and unnaturally early wisdom.

141.play: To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle,

142.play: He prettily and aptly taunts himself:

143.play: So cunning and so young is wonderful.

144.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, will't please you pass along?

145.play: Myself and my good cousin Buckingham

146.play: Will to your mother, to entreat of her
notes: Will to your Mother] 146. Compare: ‘I’ll to the king and signify to him,’ I, iv, 101.—Oechelhäuser (Essay, etc., p. 100): The opinion advanced by Rötscher, and later adopted by other aesthetic critics, for example, Meziéres, that Richard’s design of murdering his nephews first came to maturity during this conversation with the prince, cannot, in my opinion, be maintained consistently with the whole tenour of the play, and especially with Richard’s character. Had he h.‘ fall those grim asides—so portentous of an approaching end—on the occasion of the v.‘ocking gibes of the precocious little Duke of York, it would be a more plausible g.‘und for the foregoing opinion that he here first contemplated the murder. But Richard uttered both those asides in conversation with the elder of the two princes. Is it in accordance with the present events and characters to conceive that the innocent remarks on the founding of the tower by Caesar, and the sensible remark: ‘Me-thinks the truth should live from age to age,’ etc., first suggested to Richard the thought of murdering both nephews? The removal itself, no matter whether Prince Edward appeared witty or not, was a natural consequence of the first step. Imprisonment in that wild time of party-discord was so insufficient that, in order to remain in undisturbed possession of the throne, this bloody deed must have been of necessity included in Richard’s original idea.

147.play: To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.

148.play: YORK: What, will you go unto the Tower, my lord?

149.play: PRINCE EDWARD: My lord protector needs will have it so.
notes: will haue it so] 149. The reading ‘e’em will have it so,’ which is that of Collier’s MS, was not adopted by him either in his edition ii, or in his Monovolume.—Ed.

150.play: YORK: I shall not sleep in quiet at the Tower.

151.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, what should you fear?
notes: Why, what should you fear] 151. Steevens: Without Hanmer’s addition of sir, this half line is harsh and quite unmetrical.—Malone: Certainly unmetrical, and why not? Here Steevens again falls into the error which I have so often had occasion to mention—that every word, and every short address of three or four words, are to be considered as parts of metrical verses, a notion which has again and again been confuted. But if any were to be made to this line, a more improper

152.play: YORK: Marry, my uncle Clarence' angry ghost:
notes: Clarence angry Ghost] 152. Compare: 'The bitter sentence of poor Clarence death,' I, iv, 184.

153.play: My grandam told me he was murdered there.

154.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I fear no uncles dead.

155.play: GLOUCESTER: Nor none that live, I hope.

156.play: PRINCE EDWARD: An if they live, I hope I need not fear.
notes: And if] 156. See I. 103 supra.

And if they liue. . . feare] 156. VAUGHAN (iii, 75): If 'they' be referred to the uncles that live of Gloucester's answer [there is an absurdity]; but there is perfect propriety and sense if 'they' be referred to the 'uncles' of the prince's observation. In this case we must take the two speeches of the prince together as meaning this: 'I fear not my uncles if they are dead, and I hope I need not fear at all if my uncles are alive.' The prince is desirous to avoid any statement that he is not afraid of his living uncle Richard, and he frames his language accordingly, so as to answer Gloucester's question in form, without giving him the assurance for which the question was asked. . . . That the words, 'And if they live,' do refer to the 'uncles dead' of the prince's speech, is evinced by what follows: 'And with a heavy heart Thinking on them go I unto the Tower'; where 'them' cannot possibly relate to any but uncles, who, as he supposed, might be dead.

157.play: But come, my lord; and with a heavy heart,

158.play: Thinking on them, go I unto the Tower.

159.play: [A Sennet. Exeunt all but GLOUCESTER, BUCKINGHAM and CATESBY]

160.play: BUCKINGHAM: Think you, my lord, this little prating York

161.play: Was not incensed by his subtle mother
notes: incensed] 161. MURRAY (N. E. D.): v.² † 4. To incite to some action; to urge, instigate, stir up, 'set on.' Coverdale: Erasmus Par. i, Pet. 7: 'The Profession . . . wherunto they ought rather to be encensed and allured by your honest behaviour.' —NARES: To put sense into, to instruct, inform. A provincial expression still quite current in Staffordshire, and probably Warwickshire, whence we may sup-

162.play: To taunt and scorn you thus opprobriously?

163.play: GLOUCESTER: No doubt, no doubt; O, 'tis a parlous boy;
notes: perilious] 163. See note on ‘parlous,’ II, iv, 39.
capable] 164. That is, quick of apprehension, intelligent.

165.play: He is all the mother's, from the top to toe.

166.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, let them rest. Come hither, Catesby.
notes: Catesby] 166. For other examples of e mute, pronounced, see ABBOTT, § 487; compare III, vii, 61; 69; 87; 89, where this name, although occurring in the middle of the line, must, for the sake of the metre, be pronounced ‘Catésby.’

167.play: Thou art sworn as deeply to effect what we intend
notes: Thou art sworne] 167. WRIGHT: These words should perhaps be placed by themselves, or, omitting ‘hither,’ be read with the previous line.—TAWNEY: Possibly ‘deeply’ may be taken with ‘effect,’ in the sense of cunningly. [Would not a comma after ‘deeply’ render the sense of these two lines somewhat clearer? Thus: ‘Thou art sworn as deeply, to effect what we intend, As closely to conceal what we impart.’ That is, You are as deeply sworn to carry out our wishes, as you are to secrecy in regard to them.—BARNARD offers as an alternative interpretation to the above: Thou art sworn both cunningly to effect what we intend and closely to conceal what we impart.—ED.]

168.play: As closely to conceal what we impart:

169.play: Thou know'st our reasons urged upon the way;

170.play: What think'st thou? is it not an easy matter

171.play: To make William Lord Hastings of our mind,

172.play: For the instalment of this noble duke

173.play: In the seat royal of this famous isle?
notes: Seat Royall] 173. WRIGHT: Compare: ‘and the morow after, he was proclaymed kyng and with solemnpite rode to Westminster, and there sate in the eate roial.’—Hall, Chronicle, p. 375.

174.play: CATESBY: He for his father's sake so loves the prince,

175.play: That he will not be won to aught against him.

176.play: BUCKINGHAM: What think'st thou, then, of Stanley? what will he?

177.play: CATESBY: He will do all in all as Hastings doth.

178.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, then, no more but this: go, gentle Catesby,

179.play: And, as it were far off sound thou Lord Hastings,

180.play: How doth he stand affected to our purpose;

181.play: And summon him to-morrow to the Tower,

183.play: If thou dost find him tractable to us,

184.play: Encourage him, and show him all our reasons:

185.play: If he be leaden, icy-cold, unwilling,

186.play: Be thou so too; and so break off your talk,
notes: the talke] 186. WRIGHT, in referring to the Quarto reading your, remarks that the ‘MS had probably “y” or “y.”’

187.play: And give us notice of his inclination:

188.play: For we to-morrow hold divided councils,
notes: diuided Councell] 188. ‘But the protector and the duke, after that, they had set the lord Cardinal], the Archebishopp of Yorke than lorde Chauncellour, the Bishopp of Ely, the lord Stanley and the lord Hastings than lord Chamberleine, with many other noble men to commune and deuise about the coronacion in one place: as fast were they in an other place contreuyng the contrary, and to make the protector kyng.’—More, p. 66.

189.play: Wherein thyself shalt highly be employ'd.

190.play: GLOUCESTER: Commend me to Lord William: tell him, Catesby,

191.play: His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries
notes: Knot] 191. Compare: ‘A knot you are of damned blood-suckers,’ III, iii, 8.

192.play: To-morrow are let blood at Pomfret-castle;

193.play: And bid my friend, for joy of this good news,

194.play: Give mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.

195.play: BUCKINGHAM: Good Catesby, go, effect this business soundly.

196.play: CATESBY: My good lords both, with all the heed I may.

197.play: GLOUCESTER: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?

199.play: GLOUCESTER: At Crosby Place, there shall you find us both.
notes: Crosby House] 199. See I, ii, 236, and note.

201.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, my lord, what shall we do, if we perceive

202.play: Lord Hastings will not yield to our complots?
notes: Complot] 202. WRIGHT: Cotgrave has ‘Complot: m. A complot, conspiracie, couin, confederacie, packe, or compacting together.’

203.play: GLOUCESTER: Chop off his head, man; somewhat we will do:
notes: Something . . determine] 203. KNIGHT: It is difficult not to have a leaning to the Quarto reading, with which we have so long been familiar; but, on the other hand, it is impossible to believe that the correction came from any hand but that of the author.—COLLIER (Notes, etc., p. 330): [Since] Richard’s answer to Buckingham is made, by the MS corrector, to conform precisely to the words of the Quarto, we may perhaps conclude that the actor so recited the line in the time of the old corrector, and not as it stands more tamely in the Folios.—R. G. WHITE: Aside from the authority of the Folio, ‘determine,’ in its fine sense, bring to an end, is much preferable to the more vague and commonplace do [of the Quarto] (Richard means to have done with vacillation and vacillators in the surest and quickest way), and the change avoids the bald repetition of Buckingham’s query, ‘what shall we do?’ and also the cacophony, ‘somewhat we will.’—MARSHALL: It is not necessary to take ‘determine’ here as equivalent to to put an end to. It seems to us that the Qto reading is more commonplace than that of the Ff. Gloucester answers with characteristic promptitude, ‘Chop off his head,’ so getting rid of Hastings; but the next sentence, ‘something we will determine,’ is spoken in a more serious manner, the meaning being, having got rid of him, we will determine on some plan of action.

204.play: And, look, when I am king, claim thou of me

205.play: The earldom of Hereford, and the moveables
notes: Earledome of Hereford] 205. WRIGHT: Buckingham claimed the earldom of Hereford in virtue of his descent from Thomas of Woodstock, who married Eleanor, daughter and coheiress of Humphrey de Bohun.

206.play: Whereof the king my brother stood possess'd.

207.play: BUCKINGHAM: I'll claim that promise at your grace's hands.
notes: Buck. He clayme that promise] 207. Richardson (p. 30): [Buckingham] possesses some talents, and considerable discernment of human nature: his passions are ardent; he has little zeal for the public welfare, or the interests of virtue or religion; yet to a certain degree, he possesses humanity and a sense of duty. He is moved with the love of power and of wealth. He is susceptible, perhaps, of envy against those who arise to such pre-eminence as he thinks might have suited his own talents and condition. Possessing some political abilities, or at least possessing that cunning, that power of subtile contrivance, and that habit of activity, which sometimes pass for political abilities, and which, imposing upon those who possess them, make them fancy themselves endowed with the powers of distinguished statesmen; he values himself for his talents, and is desirous of displaying them. Indeed, this seems to be the most striking feature in his character; and the desire of exhibiting his skill and dexterity appears to be the foremost of his active principles. Such a person is Buckingham; and the conduct of Richard is perfectly consonant. Having too much penetration, or too little regard to the public weal, to be blindfolded or imposed upon, like the Mayor Richard treats him with apparent confidence. Moved, perhaps, with envy against the kindred of the Queen, or the hope of preeminence in consequence of their ruin, he concurs in the accomplishment of their destruction, and in assisting the usurper to attain his unlawful preferment. But, above all, excessively vain of his talents, Richard borrows aid from his counsels, and not only uses him as the tool of his designs, but seems to share with him in the glory of their success. Knowing, too, that his sense of virtue is faint, or of little power, and that the secret exultation and triumph for over-reaching their adversaries will afford him pleasure sufficient to counterbalance the pain that may arise in his breast from the perpetration of guilt, he makes him, in a certain degree, the confident of his crimes. It is also to be remarked that Buckingham, elated with the hope of reward, and elated still more with vanity in the display of his talents, appears more active than the usurper himself; more inventive in the contrivance of expedients, and more alert in their execution. There are many such persons, the instruments of designing men: persons of some ability, of less virtue, who derive consequence to themselves by fancying they are privy to the vices or designs of men whom they respect, and who are lifted with triumph in the fulfilment of crafty projects. Richard, however, sees the slightness of Buckingham's mind, and reveals no more of his projects and vices than he reckons expedient for the accomplishment of his purpose: for, as some men, when at variance, so restrain their resentments as to leave room for future reconciliation and friendship, so Richard manages his seeming friendships as to leave room, without the hazard of material injury to himself, for future hatred and animosity.

208.play: GLOUCESTER: And look to have it yielded with all willingness.

209.play: Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards

210.play: We may digest our complots in some form.

1.play: [Enter a Messenger]

2.play: Messenger: What, ho! my lord!

3.play: HASTINGS: [Within]  Who knocks at the door?

4.play: Messenger: A messenger from the Lord Stanley.

5.play: [Enter HASTINGS]

6.play: HASTINGS: What is't o'clock?

7.play: Messenger: Upon the stroke of four.

8.play: HASTINGS: Cannot thy master sleep these tedious nights?
notes: Cannot my Lord, etc.] 8. CAPELL (ii, 182): The superior ease of this speech and the three that follow it will recommend them to the comparer; the speaker's sense in this first is—are the nights so tedious thy master can't sleep in them? and in his second he cuts the messenger short, whose division made him fear he would sermonise; the levity of the proceeding is consonant to all that comes from him afterwards.—SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–'76; p. 21): The Folio reading is a metrical irregularity, such as we find frequently in the Quarto, and generally corrected in the Folio. But if the corrector, whoever he was, forgot to strike out thy, in the Quarto line, it would easily be turned into 'my' by the printer's intelligence.—MARSHALL: If we adopt the reading of F, we must elide 'Cannot' into Can't. It looks very much as if the passage were intended to be prose.

9.play: Messenger: So it should seem by that I have to say.

10.play: First, he commends him to your noble lordship.

11.play: HASTINGS: And then?

12.play: Messenger: And then he sends you word

13.play: He dreamt to-night the boar had razed his helm:
notes: Bore . . . rased off his Helme] 13. STEEVENS says this term “‘rased” or rashed is always given to describe the violence inflicted by a boar,’ and quotes: ‘In his anointed flesh rash boarish fangs.’—Lear (Quarto), III, vii, 58; also ‘ha, cur avaunt, the bore so rase thy hide.’—Warner, Albion’s England, 1602, bk. vii, ch. xxvii. CRAIGIE (N. E. D. s. v. Rase. v.3 to pull or pluck) gives the present line as an example, but among the many meanings which he also assigns to Rase, v.1, that mentioned by Steevens does not appear. Under Rash v.1, 3 (to dash things together, or one thing through another), the line from the Quarto Lear is quoted by Craigie; but a technical meaning as applied to the injury by a boar is not assigned to it. By the boar is, of course, meant Gloucester. See I, iii, 237.—ED.

14.play: Besides, he says there are two councils held;
notes: two Counsels] 14. See III, i, 200.

15.play: And that may be determined at the one

16.play: which may make you and him to rue at the other.

17.play: Therefore he sends to know your lordship's pleasure,

18.play: If presently you will take horse with him,

19.play: And with all speed post with him toward the north,

20.play: To shun the danger that his soul divines.

21.play: HASTINGS: Go, fellow, go, return unto thy lord;

22.play: Bid him not fear the separated councils

23.play: His honour and myself are at the one,
notes: His Honor] 23. MALONE: This was the usual address to noblemen in Shakespeare’s time. Compare ‘I leave it to your honourable survey, and your honour to your heart’s content.’—Ven. & Ad., Dedication.

24.play: And at the other is my servant Catesby
notes: at the other, is . . . Catesby] 24. MALONE: Compare ‘I fear’d the end; my Catesbies being there Discharg’d all doubts: Him held I most entire.’—Mirror for Magistrates; The Lord Hastings, 1575 [p. 420, ed. 1610].

my good friend Catesby] 24. DELIUS (Jahrbuch, vii, 155): Shakespeare is but following his authority—Holinshed—in thus making Hastings refer to Catesby as his ‘good friend’; but the anonymous corrector of the Quarto, who does not appear to have read the chronicles, considers ‘my good friend’ in the mouth of a nobleman as too undignified, and therefore substitutes the meaningless phrase, my servant.

25.play: Where nothing can proceed that toucheth us

26.play: Whereof I shall not have intelligence.

27.play: Tell him his fears are shallow, wanting instance:
notes: without instance] 27. JOHNSON: That is, wanting some example, or act of malevolence, by which they may be justified: or which, perhaps, is nearer to the true reading, wanting any immediate ground or reason.—M. MASON: ‘Instance’ seems to mean symptom or prognostic. Compare: ‘Before the always wind-obeying deep Gave any tragic instance of our harm.’—Com. of Err., I, i, 65.—DYCE (Gloss. s. v. instance): A word used by Shakespeare with various shades of meaning which it is not always easy to distinguish—motive, inducement, cause, ground; symptom, prognostic; information, assurance; proof, example, indication. [The majority of editors have accepted Johnson’s interpretation of ‘instance’ in preference to that of Mason.—Ed.]

28.play: And for his dreams, I wonder he is so fond

29.play: To trust the mockery of unquiet slumbers

30.play: To fly the boar before the boar pursues,

31.play: Were to incense the boar to follow us

32.play: And make pursuit where he did mean no chase.

33.play: Go, bid thy master rise and come to me

34.play: And we will both together to the Tower,

35.play: Where, he shall see, the boar will use us kindly.

36.play: Messenger: My gracious lord, I'll tell him what you say.
notes: He goe, my Lord, etc.] 36. This line, as it appears in the Quartos, is repeated in III, vii, 74, with but slight variation, thus: ‘He tell him what you say, my Lord’; possibly the reason for the changes in the Folio text in both lines is that given by DANIEL in explanation of a somewhat similar repetition in III, i, 209, 210, and IV, ii, 93.

37.play: [Exit]

38.play: [Enter CATESBY]

39.play: CATESBY: Many good morrows to my noble lord!

40.play: HASTINGS: Good morrow, Catesby; you are early stirring

41.play: What news, what news, in this our tottering state?

42.play: CATESBY: It is a reeling world, indeed, my lord;
