The two villains had been fleshed, and were like dogs that had been allowed the taste of human blood; yet they wept, like two children, while narrating the particulars of the murder of the princes.—SINGER (Sh. Vind., p. 173): If Collier can adduce any example of ‘blooded’ in the sense here intended to it, it will surprise me very much. All authorities I am acquainted with are against it; and I hold it to be an invention of the ingenious correctors, who do not hesitate at coining a word.

Dogged] 7. TAWNEY quotes two or three passages wherein dogs are taken by Shakespeare as symbols of inhumanity and creatures lacking pity. He adds: ‘Shakespeare appears to have had a low opinion of dogs.’ Tawney might have easily increased his examples; but, on the other hand, Shakespeare is one of the very few dramatists who has given a prominent part to a dog in one of his plays (the only other instance I can, at present, recall is Puntarvolo’s dog in Jonson’s Every Man out of his Humour); whatever may have been Shakespeare’s private opinion, Launce, although speaking of Crab as ‘the sourest-natured dog that lives,’ made of that dog a companion and confidant.

8.play: Melting with tenderness and kind compassion

9.play: Wept like two children in their deaths' sad stories.
notes: in their deaths sad Story] 9. That is, in relating, or hearing, or remembering. This would fill out the ellipsis, and then ‘in’ becomes the common in before a verbal meaning during or while.—Ed.

10.play: 'Lo, thus' quoth Dighton, 'lay those tender babes:'

11.play: 'Thus, thus,' quoth Forrest, 'girdling one another

12.play: Within their innocent alabaster arms:
notes: Alablaster] 12. MURRAY (N. E. D.): From Greek ἀλάβαστρος, properly ἀλάβαστος, said to be from name of a town in Egypt. The spelling in sixteenth and seventeenth centuries is almost always ‘alablaster’; apparently due to a confusion with arblaster, a cross-bowman, also written alablaster.

13.play: Their lips were four red roses on a stalk,

14.play: Which in their summer beauty kiss'd each other.

15.play: A book of prayers on their pillow lay;

16.play: Which once,' quoth Forrest, 'almost changed my mind;

17.play: But O! the devil'--there the villain stopp'd

18.play: Whilst Dighton thus told on: 'We smothered

19.play: The most replenished sweet work of nature,

20.play: That from the prime creation e'er she framed.'

21.play: Thus both are gone with conscience and remorse;
notes: both are gone with Conscience and Remorse] 21. SCHMIDT (Note in Trans.): ‘Gone with,’ in the sense of being completely overcome, occurs in another passage in Shakespeare. ‘O Richard! York is too far gone with grief.’—Rich. II: II, i, 184. The words, ‘and so I left them both,’ show that this is the only meaning here applicable, provided we read with the Qq thus, and not with the Ff, ‘hence.’ [See, for this use of ‘with,’ ABBOTT, § 193.]

22.play: They could not speak; and so I left them both,

23.play: To bring this tidings to the bloody king.
notes: this tydings] 23. WRIGHT: Compare: ‘The tidings comes that they are all arrived.’—King John: IV, ii, 115; and: ‘But let ill tidings tell themselves.’—Ant. & Cleo., II, v, 87.

24.play: And here he comes.

25.play: [Enter KING RICHARD III]

26.play: All hail, my sovereign liege!

27.play: KING RICHARD III: Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in thy news?
notes: Tirrell ... thy Newes] 27. MACREADY, in his account of his first appearance as Richard III., says (p. 141): ‘At the close of the compunctious soliloquy that Cibber has introduced Tyrrel enters: with all the eagerness of fevered impatience I rushed to him, inquiring of him, in short broken sentences, the children’s fate; with rapid decision on the mode of disposing of them, hastily gave him his orders, and hurrying him away exclaimed with triumphant exultation: “Why then my loudest fears are hushed!” The pit rose to a man, and continued waving hats and handkerchiefs in a perfect tempest of applause for some minutes. The battle was won. The excitement of the audience was maintained at fever-heat through the remainder of the tragedy.’

28.play: TYRREL: If to have done the thing you gave in charge
notes: to haue done] 28. BARNARD: That is, to have the thing done (performed). ‘Have’ here is not auxiliary, but is equivalent to, to possess.

29.play: Beget your happiness, be happy then,

30.play: For it is done, my lord.

31.play: KING RICHARD III: But didst thou see them dead?

32.play: TYRREL: I did, my lord.

33.play: KING RICHARD III: And buried, gentle Tyrrel?

34.play: TYRREL: The chaplain of the Tower hath buried them;

35.play: But how or in what place I do not know.
notes: where . . . I do not know] 35. SKOTTOWE (i, 198): This assertion by Tirrell displays the poet's remembrance of one passage and forgetfulness of another, in Sir Thomas More; who first narrates that Tirrell himself directed the burial of the children 'at the stayre foote, metely depe in the grounde vnder a great heape of stones.' But, it is added, they were afterwards taken up by 'a prieste of Sir Robert Brackenbury, and secretly entered in such place, as by the occasion of his deathe, whiche onely knew it, could neuer sync come to light. . . . Bothe Dighton and Tirrell confessed the murther . . . but whither the bodies were remoued thei could nothing tel.'—[pp. 131, 132.]—DIXON (i, 34): As the priest would be likely to inter the princes in consecrated ground, search was made, not only in the open graveyard near St Peter's, but within the church. To find these relics would have been to render a signal service to King Henry. No effort was spared; but fate was against the search; and as the bodies could not be found, the most cunning princes of Europe affected to believe that Perkin Warbeck, the Pretender, was King Edward's son. Two hundred years after the deed was done the mystery was cleared. In the reign of Charles the Second, when the Keep (no longer used as a royal palace) was being filled with state-papers, some workmen, in making a new staircase into the royal chapel, found under the old stone-steps, hidden close away, and covered with earth, the bones of two boys, which answered in every way to those which had been sought so long. Deep public wonder was excited; full inquiry into all the facts was made; and a report being sent to Charles that these bones were those of the murdered princes, the King gave orders for their removal to a royal sepulchre in Westminster Abbey. The bones thus found now lie in the great chapel built by Henry the Seventh, side by side with some of the most eminent of English kings.—BAYLEY (i, 63): Those who have been most forward to blacken the memory of Richard have advanced, as an additional argument against him, the discovery of some bones, resembling those of two children, at the foot of the staircase leading to the chapel in the White Tower: but, in fairness, little or no reliance should be placed on this circumstance; for so many and so contradictory are the statements respecting the burial of these young princes, that bones found at one time in an uninhabited turret were regarded as the remains of one of them; although it afterwards turned out that they were the limbs of an old ape that had clambered up there and died! It is also a very general opinion that the building called the Bloody-Tower received its appellation from the circumstance of the children having been stifled in it; and it is commonly and confidently asserted that the bones were found under a staircase there; yet both of these stories seem wholly without foundation.

36.play: KING RICHARD III: Come to me, Tyrrel, soon at after supper,
notes: soone] 36. ARROWSMITH (Sh.'s Editors, p. 7): Although 'soon' in the west of England to this day signifies evening (Halliwell, Dict. of Arch. and Prov. Words), yet elsewhere, or to persons unversed in the nomenclature of the Tudor-Stewart era, such a signification is unknown, and would be sought to as little pur-

and after Supper] 36. The majority of editors, as will be seen in the Text. Notes, here follow the Qq, reading: soon as after supper, whereon Wright thus notes: ‘It is not quite certain how this phrase is to be understood. We may either regard “after supper” as a compound word, equivalent to rear-supper, and denoting the banquet which followed the more substantial meal, or we may take “at after” as equivalent to after, as in: “At after souper goth this noble king.”—Chaucer, Cant. Tales, 10616 (ed. Tyrwhitt), and again in: “They were both left benle tyll the next daye at after dyner.”—Ascham, Toxophilus, I, 128 (ed. Arber). But although “at after” in this sense is not infrequently found, it does not elsewhere occur in Shakespeare, while “soon” followed by “at” is very common.’ [Bartlett, Concord., gives nine examples.]—Spedding (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 25) considers the Folio change a mere misprint.—PICKERSGILL (Ibid., p. 94) says: ‘It is obvious that the change from at into “and” cannot be Shakespeare’s; for the phrase is a common one with him. There are two clear indications that the alteration was deliberately made: (1) the insertion of a comma after “soon,” and (2) the substitution of “when” for and in the following line.’

37.play: And thou shalt tell the process of their death.
notes: thou shalt tell the process of their death] 37. Kemble (Remarks, p. 88): The whole passage—Is it done?—are they dead?—are they buried?—forcibly expresses the tyrant’s fear lest the bloody work may have been left incomplete: He is far from finding himself at ease on Tyrrel’s first relation of the event; and his solicitude to hear the process of the murder retold only discovers the anxiety with which he labours, till he shall have put the irrevocable deed past doubt. By the immutable law of nature, this painful state of mind inevitably waits on the entrusting of dangerous crimes to the execution of mercenary agents; and in the case of Macbeth also, similar guilt produces similar torture; compare—‘Get thee gone: Tomorrow We’ll hear ourselves again.’—Macbeth, III, iv.
processe] 37. For other examples of ‘process’ in the sense of narrative, account, see Schmidt, Lex. s. v. 3.

38.play: Meantime, but think how I may do thee good,

39.play: And be inheritor of thy desire.

40.play: Farewell till soon.
notes: Farewell till then] 40. If soon be taken with the meaning evening, as given by Arrowsmith in a note on l. 172 above, the Quarto reading: Farewell till soon, is, perhaps, to be here preferred. Richard has just told Tirrell to meet him after

41.play: [Exit TYRREL]
notes: Farewell] 41. WINTER (Shadows, etc., i, 313): Chief among the beauties in Mansfield’s impersonation of Richard was imagination. The attitude of the monarch toward his throne—the infernal triumph, and yet the remorseful agony and withering fear—in the moment of ghastly loneliness when he knows that the innocent princes have been murdered and that his imperial pathway is clear, made up one of the finest spectacles of dramatic illumination that the stage has afforded. You saw the murderer’s hideous exultation, and then, in an instant, as the single ray of red light from the setting sun streamed through the Gothic window and fell upon his evil head, you saw him shrink in abject fear, cowering in the shadow of his throne; and the dusky room was seemingly peopled with gliding spectres. That treatment was theatrical, but in no derogatory sense theatrical—for it comports with the great speech on conscience; not the fustian of Cibber, about mutton and short-lived pleasure, but the speech that Shakespeare has put into Richard’s mouth; the speech that inspired Mansfield’s impersonation—the brilliant embodiment of an intellectual man, predisposed to evil, who yields to that inherent impulse, and thereafter, although intermittently convulsed with remorse, fights with tremendous energy against the goodness that he scorns and defies, till at last he dashes himself to pieces against the adamant of eternal law.

42.play: The son of Clarence have I pent up close;
notes: The Sonne of Clarence] 42. See I. 64 above.

43.play: His daughter meanly have I match'd in marriage;
notes: His daughter meanly . . . matcht] 43. RITSON: To Sir Richard Pole, Knt. This Lady, at seventy years of age, without any legal process, and for no crime but her relation to the crown, was beheaded in the Tower by Henry VIII.—HUNTER (Illust., ii, 9): It should be observed that the marriage was not so mean as the words would lead us to suppose, and as is, I believe, generally supposed by many persons who do not take their ideas of history from Shakespeare only. The mother of Sir Richard Pole was half-sister to Margaret, Countess of Richmond. . . . I do not know whether Shakespeare is right in representing the marriage of the daughter of Clarence with Sir Richard Pole as having taken place during the reign of Richard; but it was in fact a union of the houses of York and Lancaster, similar to that in the marriage of Richmond and the lady Elizabeth; . . . and, if brought about in the reign of Henry VII., probably a stroke of the same policy which gave to the new Lancastrian sovereign the heiress of the rival house. . . . Shakespeare’s line has, I have no doubt, contributed much to keep out of sight the real quality and condition of the father of Cardinal Pole, whose reputation was European, and who was one of the most illustrious characters of the age.—BOSWELL-STONE (p. 396): Margaret Plantagenet was about twelve years of age at the time of Richard's death. . . . The dramatist has, apparently, confounded her with her first cousin. In 1485 the rumour that Richard would marry his niece was accompanied by a

44.play: The sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom,
notes: Abraham bosome] 44. See Luke xvi, 22.—WEBB: The profanity of such a phrase in Richard’s mouth heightens the horror of his catalogue of crimes. [Was it not part of Richard’s scheme to ‘clothe his naked villainy with odd old ends stolen forth of holy writ’?—ED.]

45.play: And Anne my wife hath bid the world good night.
notes: Anne . . . hath bid this world good night] 45. Anne, Richard’s Queen, died 16 March, 1485.—LEGGE (ii, 174): The spite of Richard’s enemies was such, that reports, incredible and contradictory, were circulated in anticipation of the Queen’s death, with the view of bringing suspicion to bear upon the King. It was said that he had caused a rumour to be circulated, ‘but he would not have the author known,’ that the Queen was dead, the object imputed to him being so to alarm the Queen that she, ‘taking some conceit of this strange fame, should fall into some sudden sickness.’ She was represented as passionately demanding from the King why she should die? Richard, of course, was the consummate hypocrite. He ‘answered her with fair words, and with smiling and flattering leasings comforted her, bidding her to be of good cheer, for to his knowledge she should have none other cause.’—Grafton, p. 837. Defamation is commonly successful. It was so in the case of Richard III. . . . The Queen was known to be in delicate health. She had never recovered from the shock occasioned by the sudden death of her son, and was now the victim of consumption. But it was with a shock of surprise, not wholly free from suspicion, that the public heard of her death. There were not wanting those who openly attributed it to poison, and the tears which the King shed at her funeral to hypocrisy . . . . There is not the shadow of a doubt that she died a natural death, not the less deeply lamented by the King, even if for political reasons he had contemplated a marriage with the Princess Elizabeth when left a widower, as, in the previous year, the Queen’s physicians had assured him that he soon must be. [Is it necessary here to call attention to Shakespeare’s ‘conjuring with the time,’ thus affecting the duration of the action? It is scarcely more than a half hour by actual count since Catesby was bid to rumour it abroad that Anne was sick, and also to inquire out some ‘mean poor gentleman’ for Margaret Plantagenet, yet now, without any outside intelligence, Richard announces the death of Anne and the accomplished alliance. See Note by WHITE, l. 192.—ED.]

46.play: Now, for I know the Breton Richmond aims
notes: for I know] 46. Compare II, ii, 100: ‘For it requires the royal debt it lent you’; and see ABBOTT, § 151.

the Britaine Richmond] 46. MALONE: He thus denominates Richmond, because after the battle of Tewksbury he had taken refuge in the court of Francis II., Duke of Bretagne, where, by the procurement of Edward IV., he was kept a long time in a kind of honourable custody. [Britaïne is here unquestionably an adjec-

47.play: At young Elizabeth, my brother's daughter,

48.play: And, by that knot, looks proudly o'er the crown,
notes: lookes . . . on the Crowne] 48. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-’76; p. 64): The Folio substitutes ‘on’ for the Quarto o’er. I suppose, because the meaning was that Richmond looked up to the crown; and to look o’er did not mean to look up to.—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-’76; p. 105): Surely, the Folio reading here is a weak dilution: the Quarto, on the other hand, appears to me very forcible, perfectly intelligible, and quite in accordance with Shakespeare’s modes of elliptical expression. Richmond no doubt looks up to the crown at present, but he looks up to it with the aspiration to place himself over it, to become master of it.—TAWNEY follows the Quarto reading and thus explains it: ‘That is, despises me the wearer of the crown.’

49.play: To her I go, a jolly thriving wooer.

52.play: KING RICHARD III: Good news or bad, that thou comest in so bluntly?

53.play: CATESBY: Bad news, my lord: Ely is fled to Richmond;

54.play: And Buckingham, back'd with the hardy Welshmen,

55.play: Is in the field, and still his power increaseth.
notes: Is in the field] 55. R. G. White (ed. ii): Events are hurried, or rather huddled, upon each other: it was only at the end of the last scene that Buckingham saw that he must leave Richard for his own safety. The purpose of the dramatist was only to get a recital of the events to the ears of the audience.

56.play: KING RICHARD III: Ely with Richmond troubles me more near
notes: more neere] 56. Craigie (N. E. D. s. v. 12. b): To come or go near to, to touch closely. 1600 Holland, Livy, xlii, xiv, 1123: ‘In this last speech he came neere unto the LL of the Senat, and touched them to the quick.’

57.play: Than Buckingham and his rash-levied army.

58.play: Come, I have heard that fearful commenting

59.play: Is leaden servitor to dull delay;

60.play: Delay leads impotent and snail-paced beggary

61.play: Then fiery expedition be my wing,

62.play: Jove's Mercury, and herald for a king!

63.play: Come, muster men: my counsel is my shield;

64.play: We must be brief when traitors brave the field.

1.play: [Enter QUEEN MARGARET]
notes: old Queene Margaret] 1. HUNTER (Illust., ii, 98): There is, I believe, no historical evidence, and the taste might be questioned which led to the introduction of Queen Margaret in this play. [See Dram. Person., Margaret.] Yet there is something striking in a scene in which three illustrious ladies meet together, each of whom has such a tale to tell of husbands, children, brothers, killed in York and Lancaster’s long jars. In the hands of very skilful actors the scene might be made effective. We have no directions for ‘old Queen Margaret’ . . . speaking aside, but it is plain that her three first speeches are uttered aside, nor does she join the others till they sit upon the ground. Shakespeare was aware of the effect of this. Constance assumes this attitude; and in Rich. II. we have: ‘For God’s sake let us sit upon the ground,’ III, ii, 155.—DOWNEN (Sh. his Mind, etc., p. 181): There is a Blake-like terror and beauty in the scene in which the three women seat themselves upon the ground in their desolation and despair, and cry aloud in utter anguish of spirit. . . Misery has made them indifferent to all ceremony of queenship, and for a time to their private differences; they are seated, a rigid yet tumultuously passionate group, in the majesty of mere womanhood and supreme calamity. Readers acquainted with Blake’s illustrations to the book of Job will remember what effects, sublime and appalling, the artist produces by animating a group of figures with one common passion, which spontaneously produces in each individual the same extravagant movement of head and limbs.—WORDSWORTH (Hist. Plays, iii, p. 334): This appalling scene, and especially the speeches of Queen Margaret, may be regarded as introduced to afford a kind of relief to the reader’s mind in contemplating the crimes of Richard. To read such language creates a diversion from the unparalleled horror which those crimes excite, being itself scarcely less horrible.—CHURCHILL (p. 335): At the moment of success, when all seems sure, trouble comes. Richard must take the field to defend what he has won. There follows the comment of Fate, from

2.play: QUEEN MARGARET: So, now prosperity begins to mellow

3.play: And drop into the rotten mouth of death.

4.play: Here in these confines slily have I lurk'd,

5.play: To watch the waning of mine adversaries.

6.play: A dire induction am I witness to,
notes: induction] 6. Compare I, i, 37.

7.play: And will to France, hoping the consequence

8.play: Will prove as bitter, black, and tragical.

9.play: Withdraw thee, wretched Margaret: who comes here?

10.play: [Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH and the DUCHESS OF YORK]
notes: Enter Duchesse] 10. MOULTON (p. 112): Probabilities are forced to bring in Queen Margaret, the head and sole rallying-point of the ruined Lancastrians: when the two aged women are confronted, the whole civil war is epitomised. It is hardly necessary to point out that this enveloping action is itself a Nemesis action.

11.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Ah, my young princes! ah, my tender babes!

12.play: My unblown flowers, new-appearing sweets!

13.play: If yet your gentle souls fly in the air

14.play: And be not fix'd in doom perpetual,

15.play: Hover about me with your airy wings

16.play: And hear your mother's lamentation!

17.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Hover about her; say, that right for right
notes: right for right] 17. JOHNSON: This is one of those conceits which our author may be suspected of loving better than propriety. ‘Right for right’ is justice answering to the claims of justice. Compare: ‘Where’t should be branded if that right were right.’—l. 146 below.—MALONE: In 1, iii, Margaret was reproached with the murder of young Rutland, and the death of her husband and son were imputed to the divine vengeance roused by that wicked act: ‘So just is God to right the innocent.’ Margaret now perhaps means to say, The right of me an injured mother, whose son was slain at Tewksbury, has now operated as powerfully as that right which the death of Rutland gave you to divine justice, and has destroyed your children in their turn.

18.play: Hath dimm'd your infant morn to aged night.
notes: Infant morne . . . Aged night] 18. TAWNEY: Perhaps the meaning is: ‘Death which comes to most human beings in old age, has fallen upon them in childhood.’

19.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: So many miseries have crazed my voice,
notes: cras’d] 19. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): 4. fig. To destroy the soundness of, impair, ruin; to ruin financially, render bankrupt. [Compare, perhaps, ‘care-cras’d,’ I11, vii, 193.]

20.play: That my woe-wearied tongue is mute and dumb,
notes: still and mute] 20. WRIGHT: If Shakespeare’s pen produced either [this or the Quarto reading], it might be worth while to enquire why one was substituted for the other. [Is not the reading of the Folio to be preferred? the tongue was quiet, and the voice was dumb.—ED.]

21.play: Edward Plantagenet, why art thou dead?

22.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Plantagenet doth quit Plantagenet.
notes: quit] 22. CRAIGIE (N. E. D. s. v. II, ii, c): To be return or equivalent for, to balance; especially in phrase, to quit (the) cost.

23.play: Edward for Edward pays a dying debt.

24.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Wilt thou, O God, fly from such gentle lambs,

25.play: And throw them in the entrails of the wolf?

26.play: When didst thou sleep when such a deed was done?
notes: When didst thou sleepe] 26. MALONE: The editor of F, changed ‘When’ to Why, though Margaret’s answer evidently refers to the word found in the original copy.—STEEVENS: I am not quite certain of the authenticity of this reading. The reply of Margaret might have been designed as an interrogatory echo to the last words of the Queen.—M. MASON: This appears to be the true reading, as Margaret’s next speech is an answer to that question that was not addressed to her.—The COWDEN-CLARKES: This near repetition of the word ‘when’ gives an effect of impatient sorrow to the Queen’s speech, in consonance with the sense which it bore as an exclamation of impatience in Shakespeare’s time.

27.play: QUEEN MARGARET: When holy Harry died, and my sweet son.

28.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Blind sight, dead life, poor mortal living ghost,

29.play: Woe's scene, world's shame, grave's due by life usurp'd,

30.play: Brief abstract and record of tedious days,
notes: record] 30. For change of accent in this word, see WALKER, Vers., p. 133.

31.play: Rest thy unrest on England's lawful earth,
notes: Rest thy vnrest] 31. DELIUS: Compare: ‘But let her rest in her unrest awhile.’ —Twi. And., IV, ii 31.

32.play: [Sitting down]

33.play: Unlawfully made drunk with innocents' blood!

34.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O, that thou wouldst as well afford a grave

35.play: As thou canst yield a melancholy seat!

36.play: Then would I hide my bones, not rest them here.

37.play: O, who hath any cause to mourn but I?

38.play: [Sitting down by her]

39.play: QUEEN MARGARET: If ancient sorrow be most reverend,

40.play: Give mine the benefit of seniory,
notes: signeurie] 40. That is, seniority.

41.play: And let my woes frown on the upper hand.
notes: let my greefes frowne on the vpper hand] 41. MALONE: Compare: 'By this starts Collatine as from a dream, And bids Lucretius give his sorrow place.'—Rape of Luc., 177.

42.play: If sorrow can admit society,

43.play: [Sitting down with them]

44.play: Tell o'er your woes again by viewing mine:

45.play: I had an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him;
notes: I had a Husband] 45. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 26): Here the Quarto is undoubtedly wrong. For it is Margaret that speaks. The obvious error might have suggested the Folio correction to anybody.... I suspect, however, that it was Shakespeare's own mistake, and I doubt whether it was his own correction. The line was evidently meant to correspond in form with the next but one: 'Thou hadst a Richard,' etc., and no word but 'Richard' will give the proper effect. Here, therefore, I admit that we have reason to suspect the intrusion of a non-Shakespearean hand.—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 94): As Spedding justly observes, the force of the whole passage is spoiled by changing Richard into any other word.... But has he fully considered the consequences, upon this admission, on his own theory? There can be little doubt that Shakespeare wrote Richard; it is, so to speak, one of those ten-barred gates which he takes with such splendid audacity and imperturbable sang-froid. But if, like Spedding, I supposed that

46.play: I had a Harry, till a Richard kill'd him:

47.play: Thou hadst an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him;

48.play: Thou hadst a Richard, till a Richard killed him;

49.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I had a Richard too, and thou didst kill him;

50.play: I had a Rutland too, thou holp'st to kill him.

51.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thou hadst a Clarence too, and Richard kill'd him.

52.play: From forth the kennel of thy womb hath crept
notes: From forth the kennell] 52. ROLFE: Apparently an allusion to the myth of Scylla. [It is impossible to say that there is not here this allusion, but if it be, is it not also combined with an allusion to the fact set forth in the lines in the old play, The Tragedie of Richard Duke of Yorke, upon which 3 Henry VI. was founded?—‘the midwife cride O Jesus blesse vs, he is borne with teeth. And so I was indeed, which plainelie signified That I should snarle and bite, and plaie the dogge.’—V, vi, 63 ed. Cam. These lines with but slight change appear in 3 Hen. VI.: V, vi, 72; and Margaret, in the present scene, l. 52, refers to this fact in regard to Richard’s birth.—Ed.]

53.play: A hell-hound that doth hunt us all to death:

54.play: That dog, that had his teeth before his eyes,
notes: Dogge . . . his teeth before his eyes] 54. The foregoing note on l. 50 seems an adequate explanation of this allusion; DELIUS, however, considers that Margaret refers to the fact that the child does not see until after birth and normally gets its teeth much later.

55.play: To worry lambs and lap their gentle blood,

56.play: That foul defacer of God's handiwork,
notes: handy worke] 56. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): 1. Work of the hands; a thing or collection of things made by the hands of any one. From Old English hand-gewæorc. As gewæorc, iwork did not survive in Middle English, hand-iwere was naturally analysed as a compound of the simple were, with handi, often written separately and treated as an adjective.

57.play: That excellent grand tyrant of the earth,
notes: excellent] 57. BRADLEY (N. E. D. s. v. t b) gives several examples of ‘excellent’ used in a ‘bad or neutral sense’; among them the two following from Shakespeare: ‘A very excellent piece of villainy.’—Tit. And., II, iii, 7; ‘This is the excellent foppery of the world.’—Lear, I, ii, 128.

58.play: That reigns in galled eyes of weeping souls,
notes: reignes] 58. Is there, perchance, here a play on the words rains and ‘weeping’?—Ed.
galled] 58. Compare: ‘Ere yet the salt of most unrighteous tears Had left the flushing in her galled eyes.’

59.play: Thy womb let loose, to chase us to our graves.

60.play: O upright, just, and true-disposing God,

61.play: How do I thank thee, that this carnal cur
notes: carnall] 61. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. 6) quotes the present line as the only example of ‘carnal’ used in this figurative sense of bloody, murderous.

62.play: Preys on the issue of his mother's body,

63.play: And makes her pew-fellow with others' moan!
notes: Pue-fellow] 63. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. pew-fellow): One who has a seat in the same pew; a fellow worshipper; one of the same persuasion or sect; a companion; an associate.—BIRCH (p. 207): The mention of God is accompanied by the usual satire upon it in the mouth of Queen Elizabeth, and by renewed invective in that of Queen Margaret. Margaret, however, thanks God for having revenged her on her enemies—as if resort was made to a pew in a church, to utter cries of anguish, to ask for vengeance on each other, and record their thanks for its receipt. [BIRCH assures us, in his preface, that: ‘—he wishes to be considered merely as an enquirer, not as a censor. He desires not to judge Shakespeare for his sentiments, but only to exhibit them. This, he trusts, he has done truly and impartially, without levity on the one side or bigotry on the other.’ Are neither bigotry, nor a certain levity, entirely absent in the above note?—Ed.]

64.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O Harry's wife, triumph not in my woes!
notes: triumph] 64. Here accented, for the sake of the metre and for this alone, ‘triúmph’; compare III, iv, 100; or see WALKER, Crit., iii, 174.

65.play: God witness with me, I have wept for thine.

66.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Bear with me; I am hungry for revenge,
notes: Beare with me] 66. WARNER (Eng. Hist. in Sh., p. 239): Thus cries Margaret, not only to Elizabeth, but down the centuries it is her frank appeal to the judgement of history. There are few instances of a passion more detestable in the heart of one more excusable for nursing it.

67.play: And now I cloy me with beholding it.

68.play: Thy Edward he is dead, that stabb'd my Edward:

69.play: Thy other Edward dead, to quit my Edward;
notes: The other Edward, dead, to quit my Edward] 69. VAUGHAN (iii, 110): It is clear that Margaret needed revenge both for her son and her husband, both for Edward (Rutland) and Henry, his father. As the passage stands, although she expressly says that she is ‘cloyed with beholding her revenge,’ yet her revenge ought not moderately to be even satisfied, for it discloses no revenge for her Henry, while it reveals triple revenge for Edward. The same omission, therefore, of Henry which in l. 43 spoiled and falsified her account of her loss, is here, in truth, continued to mar and falsify her story of her satisfied revenge, and needs precisely the same remedy, that is, the substitution of his name. I would read: ‘Thy other Edward dead to quit my Henry.’ [Before considering the necessity of the change from ‘Edward’ to Henry, suggested by VAUGHAN, it may be worthy of notice that one or two slight historic details seem to have been forgotten. Rutland, whose name, by the way, was Edmund, not Edward, was not the son of Henry VI., but of Richard Plantagenet, Duke of York; he was elder brother to Gloucester and Clarence; he was not killed by Edward IV., but by Clifford. Vaughan has apparently confused him with Edward, Prince of Wales, the son of Henry VI. As regards Vaughan’s proposed change, Margaret says, in effect: thy Edward, i.e., Edward IV., who killed my son Edward, is dead; the other Edward, your son, is dead to balance the death of my Edward; young York’s death barely counts, because both Edward IV. and Edward V. will not even make up for my one son. If now we change ‘Edward’ at the end of l. 67 to Henry, wherein lies the force of Margaret’s remark that young York ‘is but boot’?—Ed.]

70.play: Young York he is but boot, because both they
notes: boot] 70. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. 1): 2. That which is ‘thrown in,’ or given in addition, to make up a deficiency of value; a premium, compensation, odds.

71.play: Match not the high perfection of my loss:

72.play: Thy Clarence he is dead that kill'd my Edward;

73.play: And the beholders of this tragic play,
notes: beholders of this . . . play] 73. That is, the murder of Edward, Prince of Wales; but here, as in I, iii, 220, Margaret accuses Rivers of having been a witness, and Hall does not give his name among the bystanders. In the former scene Margaret also mentions Dorset instead, as here, of his brother Grey, but at the time of the present scene Dorset has escaped to France. Shakespeare, possibly, did not turn back to see what he had formerly written; it is equally possible that he relied on the fact that an audience’s memory for the names of characters no longer on the scene is not very tenacious.—Ed.

74.play: The adulterate Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey,

75.play: Untimely smother'd in their dusky graves.
notes: Untimely smother’d] 75. TAWNEY: The idea seems to be that the fiery

76.play: Richard yet lives, hell's black intelligencer,
notes: Intelligencer] 76. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): One who conveys intelligence or information. One employed to obtain secret information, an informer, a spy, a secret agent.

77.play: Only reserved their factor, to buy souls
notes: their] 77. Many other examples of the plural pronoun, used in reference to both heaven and hell, will, doubtless, suggest themselves to every reader of the older dramatists.

78.play: And send them thither: but at hand, at hand,

79.play: Ensues his piteous and unpitied end:

80.play: Earth gapes, hell burns, fiends roar, saints pray.

81.play: To have him suddenly convey'd away.
notes: from hence] 81. The Folio change here seems an improvement on the away of the Qq. Triplets are not common in Shakespeare’s verse; besides the caesura in l. 81 falling at ‘say’ gives almost the impression of a fourth rhyme.—Ed.

82.play: Cancel his bond of life, dear God, I prey,
notes: Cancell his bond of life] 82. The lines: ‘Cancel and tear to pieces that great bond which keeps me pale.’—Macbeth, III, ii, 49, 50, will, of course, occur to every reader. ROLFE compares also: ‘If you will take this audit take this life, And cancel these cold bonds.’—Cymb., V, iv, 27.—Ed.

83.play: That I may live to say, The dog is dead!
notes: The Dogge is dead] 83. MRS JAMESON (ii, 259): She should have stopped

84.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O, thou didst prophesy the time would come
notes: thou did’st prophesy] 84. See I, iii, 256.

85.play: That I should wish for thee to help me curse

86.play: That bottled spider, that foul bunch-back'd toad!
notes: bottl’d Spider] 86. See I, iii, 254.

87.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I call'd thee then vain flourish of my fortune;

88.play: I call'd thee then poor shadow, painted queen;

89.play: The presentation of but what I was;
notes: presentation] 89. Wright: That is, the mere semblance without the reality.

of but what I was] 89. Marshall conjectures that we should here read: ‘but of what I was’; but does not Margaret mean that Elizabeth is only the semblance of what she, Margaret, is alone—the rightful queen? Marshall’s suggested inversion makes Margaret speak of Elizabeth as the semblance of what Margaret formerly was—a queen surrounded by friends. In I, iii, 214, 215, Margaret expresses the hope that Elizabeth may live to see another ‘decked in thy rights as thou art stalled in mine.’ It is to this, I think, that she here refers.—Ed.

90.play: The flattering index of a direful pageant;
notes: Index of a direfull Pageant] 90. Steevens: ‘Pageants’ are dumb-shows, and the poet meant to allude to one of these, the index of which promised a happier conclusion. The pageants then displayed on public occasions were generally preceded by a brief account of the order in which the characters were to walk. These indexes were distributed among the spectators, that they might understand the meaning of such allegorical stuff as was usually exhibited.—Wright: This explanation given by Steevens receives some support from Ford’s Lover’s Melancholy, III, iii, in which, before the performance of The Masque of Melancholy, Corax hands Palador a paper which explains the dumb-show: ‘Pray, my lord, Hold, and observe the plot: ’tis there express’d In kind, what shall be now expressed in action.’ For ‘Index’ used in the sense of prologue or introduction, see II, ii, 156. [For a history of the word ‘pageant’ see Murray, N. E. D. s. v. 6 b. Note.]

91.play: One heaved a-high, to be hurl'd down below;

92.play: A mother only mock'd with two sweet babes;
notes: mockt with two faire Babes] 92. That is, a mother only deceived in thinking that she has two fair babes. Compare: 'O my most gracious lord I hope you will not mock me with a husband. Duke. It is your husband mocked you with a husband.'—Meas. for Meas., V, i, 421.

93.play: A dream of what thou wert, a breath, a bubble,
notes: A dreame of what thou wast, a garish Flagge] 93. Though it does not become me to dissent from many of my betters, I cannot persuade myself that the Folio arrangement of this line and the next is not to be preferred to that of the Qq. The 'garish Flag' seems to be in apposition to the 'dream.' In what way can a garish flag refer to her present deplorable state? In times past she was, indeed, so brilliant an apparition that she might well be the aim of all the dangerous shots of envy—but is she so now? Assuredly her present state could not be compared to that of the prominent position of a standard bearer.—Ed.

94.play: A sign of dignity, a garish flag,
notes: garish] 94. BRADLEY (N. E. D. s. v. 1): Of dress, ornament, ceremonial, etc.: Obtrusively or vulgarly bright in colour, showy, gaudy. (The early spellings gaurish, gawrish, suggest derivation from gaze, to stare.)

95.play: To be the aim of every dangerous shot,

96.play: A queen in jest, only to fill the scene.

97.play: Where is thy husband now? where be thy brothers?

98.play: Where are thy children? wherein dost thou, joy?

99.play: Who sues to thee and cries 'God save the queen'?

100.play: Where be the bending peers that flatter'd thee?

101.play: Where be the thronging troops that follow'd thee?

102.play: Decline all this, and see what now thou art:
notes: Decline] 102. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. verb, 20. b): To say or recite formally or in definite order. [The present line quoted.]

103.play: For happy wife, a most distressed widow;

104.play: For joyful mother, one that wails the name;

105.play: For queen, a very caitiff crown'd with care;
notes: Caytiffe] 105. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. A. 2): A wretched miserable person, a poor wretch, one in a piteous case. From Old Norman French caitif, caitive, captive, weak, miserable. The central Old French form chaitif, gave the English variant chaitif, frequent in 14th and 15th centuries, but did not displace the earlier Norman form. The transition of meaning has taken place, more or less, in most of the Romanic languages. [In spite of the manifestly French origin of this word, DUPORT thus renders the line: ‘Qui n’a sur les autres hommes d’autre avantage que d’etre plus accablé de maux,’ which is hardly even a paraphrase of the idea that, instead of the golden crown of a queen, Elizabeth has upon her brow the deep wrinkles and whitened locks of care. SCHLEGEL is more literal: ‘Statt Königin, mit Noth gekrönte Sclavin,’ reproducing, to a certain extent, the force of the original alliteration, and the idea of captive in the use of the word Sclavin.—Ed.]

106.play: For one being sued to, one that humbly sues;

107.play: For one that scorn'd at me, now scorn'd of me;

108.play: For one being fear'd of all, now fearing one;

109.play: For one commanding all, obey'd of none.

110.play: Thus hath the course of justice wheel'd about,
notes: Justice whirl’d about] 110. WALKER (Crit., iii, 175): This might seem to be

111.play: And left thee but a very prey to time;

112.play: Having no more but thought of what thou wert,

113.play: To torture thee the more, being what thou art.

114.play: Thou didst usurp my place, and dost thou not
notes: Usurpe] 114. The use of ‘usurp’ applied to ‘sorrow’ seems somewhat incongruous; no one would forcibly take for his own use grief or pain. But as the word had been used in regard to benefits in the foregoing line, its employment in regard to evils naturally suggested itself.—Ed.

115.play: Usurp the just proportion of my sorrow?

116.play: Now thy proud neck bears half my burthen'd yoke;
notes: my burthen’d yoke] 116. Wright: That is, the yoke which is a burden to me. ‘Burthened’ in the sense of burdensome is formed from the substantive burthen, and not from the verb. Compare: ‘Or any creeping venom’d thing,’ etc., I, ii, 23.

117.play: From which even here I slip my weary neck,

118.play: And leave the burthen of it all on thee.

119.play: Farewell, York's wife, and queen of sad mischance:

120.play: These English woes will make me smile in France.

121.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O thou well skill'd in curses, stay awhile,

122.play: And teach me how to curse mine enemies!

123.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Forbear to sleep the nights, and fast the days;
notes: Forbear to sleep... and fast] 123. MALONE: ‘Fast’ has no connection with the preceding word ‘forbear’; the meaning being, sleep not at night, and fast during the day.

124.play: Compare dead happiness with living woe;
notes: Compare dead happinesse, etc.] 124. See l. 110 above.

125.play: Think that thy babes were fairer than they were,
notes: sweeter] 125. R. G. WHITE: It is noteworthy that [in l. 90] in Margaret’s preceding speech, ‘sweet babes’ of the Qq was changed to ‘faire babes’ in the Folio. This double change in counterpart could not have been accidental; and, indeed, it is far more natural and touching to use fair in the mere descriptive allusion to the babes, and ‘sweet’ in describing a mother’s memory of them. And yet fairer has been hitherto retained ‘as antithetical to “fouler”’ in the next line!

126.play: And he that slew them fouler than he is:

127.play: Bettering thy loss makes the bad causer worse:
notes: Bett’ring thy losse, makes, etc.] 127. M. MASON: We must either read this line: ‘Bett’ring thy loss, make the bad causer,’ etc., which I believe to be the true reading, or include it in a parenthesis.—MALONE: ‘Bettering’ is amplifying, magnifying, thy loss. Shakespeare employed this word for the sake of an antithesis, in which he delighted, between better and loss. [Mason’s emendation make is anticipated by the editor or compositor of Q1,—ED.]

the bad causer worse] 127. VAUGHAN (iii, 112): Steevens’s change, bad-causer, is, I conceive, of doubtful advantage to the sense. ‘Causer’ is sufficiently explained by the word ‘loss’ in the same line, to which it refers, as meaning the causer of loss, and the conversion of ‘bad’ from a simple epithet of ‘causer’ to a substantive dependent upon ‘causer’ mars both the form and essence of the aphorism, that is, making good destroyed better than it is, makes the bad man who destroyed it worse than he is.

128.play: Revolving this will teach thee how to curse.

129.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My words are dull; O, quicken them with thine!

130.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thy woes will make them sharp, and pierce like mine.

132.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Why should calamity be full of words?

133.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Windy attorneys to their client woes,
notes: Atturnies] 133. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. Attorney): From Old French atourner, in sense of ‘one appointed or constituted,’ whence all the specific uses.

their Clients Woes] 133. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 27): [The Folio is here] probably right as to ‘their,’ but is certainly wrong as to ‘clients.’ [See Text. Notes.] ‘Intestine’ in the next line must be a blunder, but need not be imputed to the reviser, being an ordinary case of a less familiar word misread by the printer for a more familiar one. [‘Client’ as an adjective is unknown to MURRAY.—ED.]

succeeders of intestine joyes] 134. THEOBALD: As I cannot understand this reading, I have adopted that of the Quarto, intestate, that is, ‘words tun’d to complaints, succeed joys that are dead; and, unbequeathed to them, to whom they should properly descend.—CAPELL (ii, 187): Intestate joys may be expressive of joys so utterly exhausted that, having no part of themselves to bequeath to others, they dy’d without a bequest; the line’s first part tells us who were their successors, namely, words of complaint.—MALONE: The metaphor is extremely harsh. The joys already possessed being all consumed and passed away, are supposed to have died intestate, having nothing to bequeath; and mere verbal complaints are their successors, but inherit nothing but misery.—VAUGHAN (iii, 115): Theobald’s interpretation is incongruous; for the person who dies is never bequeathed, and the possession which is bequeathed is not what dies. Malone seems strangely to interpret ‘poor breathing orators of miseries’ as ‘inheriting nothing but misery.’ But ‘orators’ are not heritors and ‘orator’ seems here to have a purely forensic and technical signification, and ‘breathing’ has the sense common in Shakespeare of speaking. In ‘airy’ I half suspect an equivocation between the words heir and air, to crown and emphasize the contrast between title by inheritance and title by testament.—DAVIS (p. 205): This conceit could never have occurred to a writer whose knowledge of law was only of that uncertain kind which every layman has, or thinks he has, yet who would be very cautious how he used it for such purposes of illustration or personation.—R. L. ASHHURST (MS note): If ‘intestate’ be the right word, of which I feel some doubt, I think ‘intestate joys’ must mean, pleasures in the midst of which a man is cut off without having time to make a will—wills being seldom made except almost in ‘extremis.’ Thus succeeders would mean those who received the succession to the intestate. Rather, perhaps, the civil law idea of the next of kin who accepted the succession. The joys I think belong to the original decedant. After the death, we have only words, windy attorneys, airy succeeders, breathing orators of misery. Orator is either one who prays or one who speaks. In Shakespeare’s time it was universally used in bills in equity and all chancery proceedings, and is still used in many of our courts. The old bills begin ‘Humbly complaining prayeth your orator.’ But I doubt if it mean in the present passage more than general complainants of miseries.
Poore breathing] 135. WRIGHT: ‘Poor’ is here used as an adverb.

136.play: Let them have scope: though what they do impart

137.play: Help not all, yet do they ease the heart.
notes: Helpe nothing els] 137. WRIGHT: That is, are of no other use; this change from the Quarto reading: ‘Help not at all,’ appears to have been due to a misunderstanding of the word ‘help,’ which here means, have no power to cure, but simply to relieve the heart. ‘Help’ is found frequently in this sense. Compare: ‘If all the wise men in my bottle will recover him, I will help his ague.’—Temp., II, ii, 97.—VAUGHAN (iii, 115): I am not sure that ‘help’ in the sense of cure is applicable to this place, where ‘help’ seems legal or diplomatic.—TAWNEY: It seems better to take ‘help’ here in the sense of be of use, avail, as Schmidt does. This interpretation suits equally well either [Folio or Quarto] reading.

138.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: If so, then be not tongue-tied: go with me.

139.play: And in the breath of bitter words let's smother

140.play: My damned son, which thy two sweet sons smother'd.

141.play: I hear his drum: be copious in exclaims.
notes: exclaimest] 141. See I, ii, 58.

142.play: [Enter KING RICHARD III, marching, with drums and trumpets]

143.play: KING RICHARD III: Who intercepts my expedition?
notes: Who intercepts me] 143. It may, perhaps, seem strange that Richard does not at once recognize his mother and sister-in-law. But a moment’s reflection will show that if we perchance have forgotten, Shakespeare has not, that the three women are seated upon the ground; Margaret has withdrawn, but the other two are still bowed in grief; at Richard’s first speech the Duchess rises and confronts him—a much more dramatic arrangement than if while standing she intercept him.—ED.

144.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O, she that might have intercepted thee,
notes: O she] 144. VAUGHAN, the only advocate for the Quarto reading A she, vindicates it on the ground that ‘Shakespeare often thus designates a woman.’

145.play: By strangling thee in her accursed womb

146.play: From all the slaughters, wretch, that thou hast done!

147.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Hidest thou that forehead with a golden crown,

148.play: Where should be graven, if that right were right,
notes: that] 148. For other examples of ‘that’ as a ‘conjunctional affix,’ see, if needful, ABBOTT, § 287. Compare l. 233.

149.play: The slaughter of the prince that owed that crown,

150.play: And the dire death of my two sons and brothers?
notes: Brother] 150. See I, iii, 42, and notes.

151.play: Tell me, thou villain slave, where are my children?

152.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Thou toad, thou toad, where is thy brother Clarence?

153.play: And little Ned Plantagenet, his son?

154.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Where is kind Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey?
notes: Dut. Where is kinde Hastings] 154. WRIGHT: The corrector of the Folio here changes the Quarto reading. [See Text. Notes.] The Queen is not likely to have spoken so of Hastings, who was always opposed to her family. As it appears to me highly improbable that Shakespeare wrote either the one or the other, I have followed the reading of the earlier copies.—VAUGHAN (iii, 116): I would restore the single Quarto line, but make the earlier half of it a part of the speech spoken by the Duchess, thus: ‘And little Ned Plantagenet his son? Where is kind Hastings? Q. Elis. Rivers? Vaughan? Grey?’ There is every reason for considering the remembrance of Hastings with kindness as appropriate to the Duchess of York; and not less natural is the Queen’s immediate recurrence to the loss of Rivers and his two companions.

155.play: KING RICHARD III: A flourish, trumpets! strike alarum, drums!
notes: A flourish Trumpets] 155. DOWDEN (p. 184): Richard’s cynicism and insolence have in them a kind of grim mirth; such a bonhomie as might be met with among the humourists of Pandemonium. His brutality is a manner of joking with a purpose. When his mother, with Queen Elizabeth, comes by ‘copious in exclaims,’ the mirthful Richard calls for a flourish of trumpets to drown these shrill female voices.

156.play: Let not the heavens hear these tell-tale women

157.play: Rail on the Lord's enointed: strike, I say!

159.play: Either be patient, and entreat me fair,
notes: intreat me fayre] 159. WRIGHT explains this as, ‘use me well,’ quoting in corroboration: ‘I’ll write unto them and entreat them fair.’—3 Hen. VI: I, i, 271.

160.play: Or with the clamorous report of war

161.play: Thus will I drown your exclamations.

162.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Art thou my son?
notes: Art thou my Sonne?] 162. On which word in this question is the emphasis to fall—On ‘thou,’ or on ‘Son’? Rhythm decides in favour of the former, and, in fact, would make both emphatic.—Ed.

163.play: KING RICHARD III: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.

164.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Then patiently hear my impatience.

165.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, I have a touch of your condition,
notes: touch] 165. Schmidt (Lex.) here interprets ‘touch’ as, a dash, a spice, a smack.

166.play: Which cannot brook the accent of reproof.
notes: accent of reproofe] 166. What studied insolence is contained in this euphemism for all the bitter names which had been showered upon him.—Ed.

167.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: O, let me speak!

168.play: KING RICHARD III: Do then: but I'll not hear.

169.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I will be mild and gentle in my speech.

170.play: KING RICHARD III: And brief, good mother; for I am in haste.

171.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Art thou so hasty? I have stay'd for thee,

172.play: God knows, in anguish, pain and agony.

173.play: KING RICHARD III: And came I not at last to comfort you?

174.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: No, by the holy rood, thou know'st it well,
notes: the holy Rood] 174. See III, ii, 84, and note.

175.play: Thou camest on earth to make the earth my hell.

176.play: A grievous burthen was thy birth to me;
notes: A greeuous burthen] 176. There is, possibly, an allusion here to the following fact in regard to Richard’s birth, mentioned by Rous, p. 215: ‘—rex Ricardus, qui natus est apud Fodringhay in comitatu Northamptoniz, biennio matris utero tentus.’—Ed.

177.play: Tetchy and wayward was thy infancy;
notes: Tetchy . . . thy Infancie] 177. Bucknill (Sh.’s Med. Knowl., p. 186): The Duchess of York’s description of her terrible son’s nativity, infancy, childhood, and

Tetchy] 177. Skeat (Dict.): The sense of ‘tetchy’ (better, techy) is full of tetches or teches, that is, bad habits, freaks, whims, vices. The adjective is formed from Middle English teche or tache, a habit, especially a bad habit.

178.play: Thy school-days frightful, desperate, wild, and furious,

179.play: Thy prime of manhood daring, bold, and venturous,
notes: Thy prime of Manhood] 179. That is, the springtime of thy manhood. See I, ii, 274.

180.play: Thy age confirm'd, proud, subdued, bloody,
notes: Thy Age confirm’d] 180. R. G. White (ed. ii): This is quite preposterous. Richard had hardly reached even the ‘prime of manhood.’ He was only thirty-three years old, and was slain at Bosworth field in his thirty-fifth year.

182.play: More mild, but yet more harmful, kind in hatred:
notes: more harmfull; Kinde in hatred] 182. Walker (Crit., ii, 175) suggests that we here read ‘harmful-kind in hatred’; but are not the words, ‘kind in hatred,’ intended here as a repetition of the idea contained in ‘more mild but yet more harmful’?—Ed.

183.play: What comfortable hour canst thou name,
notes: comfortable] 183. Here used causitively; as in the Book of Common Prayer: ‘The most comfortable sacrament,’ etc.

184.play: That ever graced me in thy company?
notes: grac’d] 184. Compare: ‘Please’t your highness To grace us with your royal company.’—Macbeth, III, iv, 45.

185.play: KING RICHARD III: Faith, none, but Humphrey Hour, that call'd
notes: Humfrey Hower] 185. Stevens: This may probably be an allusion to some affair of gallantry of which the Duchess had been suspected. I cannot find the name in Holinshed. Surely the poet’s fondness for a quibble has not induced him at once to personify and christen that ‘hour’ of the day which summoned his mother to break fast. Compare: ‘Gentlemen, time makes us brief: our old

Humfrey Hower] 185. Shakespeare might indeed, by this strange phrase, have designed to mark the hour at which the good Duchess was as hungry as the followers of Duke Humphrey. The common cant phrase of ‘dining with Duke Humphrey,’ I have never yet heard satisfactorily explained. It appears, however, from The Guls Horn-booke, Dekker, 1609, that in the ancient church of St Paul, one of the aisles was called ‘Duke Humphrey’s Walk;’ in which those who had no means of procuring a dinner affected to loiter. For example: ‘— sundry fellowes in their silkes shall be appointed to keepe Duke Humfrey company in Poules, because they know not where to get their dinners abroad.’—Nash, A Wonderful Prognostication for this Year, 1591; Again: ‘—to seeke his dinner in Poules with duke Humphrey: to licke dishes, to be a beggar.’—G. Harvey, Foure Letters, etc., 1592. [STEEVENS quotes three other passages wherein this phrase occurs; all of them, however, bearing a date subsequent to that of Qu1 of this present play.—Ed.]—MALONE: I believe nothing more than a quibble was meant. In Sonnet xx we find a similar conceit: a quibble between hwes (colours), and Hughes, formerly spelt (Hues), the person addressed. [This note appears in Malone’s edition, 1790, but is not repeated in the Variorum of 1821, where its place is taken by the following: ‘“Humphrey Hour” is merely used in ludicrous language for hour, like Tom Truth, for truth, and twenty more such terms.]—WRIGHT: If this phrase ever had any meaning it is now completely lost. If Steevens’s explanation be correct, it is not clear how ‘Humphrey Hour’ can mean the hour at which the Duchess was summoned to breakfast. The spelling in the Qq Howre and in the Ff ‘Hower’ does not throw much light upon the question.—VAUGHAN (iii, 171): ‘Humphrey Hour,’ on account of that name, was an ‘hour’; by virtue of the invitation to the comfort of an important meal, was a ‘comfortable hour’; by reason of inviting ‘her grace the Duchess’ with proper address of ‘your grace,’ ‘graced her’; and as delivering an invitation which was to take her ‘forth of Richard’s company,’ necessarily ‘graced her’ in his company. This is but one amongst several strokes of equivocation in the conduct of dialogue by which Shakespeare industriously touches in a perfect delineation of Richard’s social character. They have, however, here, as I opine, and certainly in other places, eluded the observation even of the most acute critics, and provoked either misinterpretation or corruption of the text.—WALSH (p. 500) gives, as the origin of the phrase, ‘to dine with Duke Humphrey,’ the following: ‘The Duke Humphrey, with whom the dinnerless are facetiously said to dine, was Humphrey, Duke of Glouceter (Henry V.’s brother), who was protector during the minority of Henry VI. He was. a great patron of literature and the arts and famous for his hospitality. Fuller, in his Worthies, says the proverb “hath altered the original meaning thereof; for first it signified scienta vivere quadra, to eat by the bounty or feed by the favour of another man; for Humphrey, Duke of Gloucester (commonly called the good duke) was so hospitable that every man of fashion, otherwise unprovided, was welcome to dine with him. But after the death of the good Duke Humphrey (when many of his former almsmen were at a loss for a meal’s meat), this proverb did alter its copy; to dine with Duke Humphrey importing to be dinnerless” [ii, 345]. A more circumstantial explanation of the saying is that on the Duke’s death the

187.play: To breakfast once forth of my company.

188.play: If I be so disgracious in your sight,

189.play: Let me march on, and not offend your grace.

notes: Strike vp the Drumme] 190. Wright: That is, strike aloud. So 'blow up' signifies blow aloud in the expression: 'Blow up the trumpet in the new moon,' Psalms lxxxi, 3.

191.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I prithee, hear me speak.

192.play: KING RICHARD III: You speak too bitterly.

193.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Hear me a word;

194.play: For I shall never speak to thee again.

196.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Either thou wilt die, by God's just ordinance,
notes: Either] 196. For other examples, where, for metrical reasons, 'either' is pronounced as a monosyllable, see Walker, Vers., p. 103; compare I, ii, 70.
thou wilt dye] 196. Marshall: Pope's change, 'thou 'lt die,' is inadmissible, since the emphasis is on 'thou,' which is opposed to 'I' in l. 197.

197.play: Ere from this war thou turn a conqueror,

198.play: Or I with grief and extreme age shall perish

199.play: And never look upon thy face again.

200.play: Therefore take with thee my most heavy curse;
notes: take . . . my most greeuous Curse] 200. Collins (p. 81) remarks that these following lines recall 'with close exactness the scene in which Oedipus curses Polynices.' [The Oxford translator thus renders the passage: 'But do you go

201.play: Which, in the day of battle, tire thee more

202.play: Than all the complete armour that thou wear'st!
notes: compleat] 202. Compare: ‘That thou dead corse, again in complete steel.’—Hamlet, I, iv, 52.

203.play: My prayers on the adverse party fight;

204.play: And there the little souls of Edward's children

205.play: Whisper the spirits of thine enemies

206.play: And promise them success and victory.

207.play: Bloody thou art, bloody will be thy end;

208.play: Shame serves thy life and doth thy death attend.

210.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Though far more cause, yet much less spirit to curse

211.play: Abides in me; I say amen to all.

212.play: KING RICHARD III: Stay, madam; I must speak a word with you.
notes: Stay Madam, etc.] 212. JOHNSON: On this dialogue ’tis not necessary to bestow much criticism, part of it is ridiculous, and the whole improbable.—M. MASON: I cannot agree with Johnson’s opinion. I see nothing ridiculous in any part of this dialogue; and with respect to probability it was not unnatural that Richard, who by his art and wheedling tongue, had prevailed on Lady Anne to marry him in her heart’s extremest grief, should hope to persuade an ambitious and, as he thought her, a wicked woman, to consent to his marriage with her daughter, which would make her a queen, and aggrandise her family.—WRIGHT: My own sympathies, I confess, are with Johnson’s opinion.—BROOKE (p. 118): This scene . . . is of that cunning which overreaches its aim. Richard thinks he has persuaded Elizabeth to give him her daughter; but it is he who has been deceived, he whom the woman has played with. She pretends to consent, but is already in communication with Richmond, to whom she does give her daughter. From this point of view, which I think Shakespeare meant, the unnaturalness of the scene (the far too great length of which is only excused by the impossible effort Richard makes) is modified; and the weakness which has come on Richard’s intellect is more than suggested. All is breaking down in him: his self-control, his temper, intelligence, his clear sight of things, his foresight, his power to keep men and subdue them to his will. The art is excellent with which this is shown in Richard’s talk with Catesby, Ratcliffe, Stanley, and the messengers.

213.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: I have no more sons of the royal blood

214.play: For thee to murder: for my daughters, Richard,

215.play: They shall be praying nuns, not weeping queens;

216.play: And therefore level not to hit their lives.
notes: levell] 216. That is, to aim; from the technical use of the word in archery.

217.play: KING RICHARD III: You have a daughter call'd Elizabeth,

218.play: Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.

219.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And must she die for this? O, let her live,

220.play: And I'll corrupt her manners, stain her beauty;

221.play: Slander myself as false to Edward's bed;

222.play: Throw over her the veil of infamy:

223.play: So she may live unscarr'd of bleeding slaughter,
notes: So she may liue] 223. That is, provided she may live; for other examples, see ABBOTT, § 133.

224.play: I will confess she was not Edward's daughter.

225.play: KING RICHARD III: Wrong not her birth, she is of royal blood.

226.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: To save her life, I'll say she is not so.

227.play: KING RICHARD III: Her life is only safest in her birth.
notes: safest onely] 227. WRIGHT: The Quarto arrangement [only safest] is in accordance with the usage of the time. Compare, Much Ado, III, i, 23: ‘That only wounds by hearsay’; that is, wounds by hearsay only.

228.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And only in that safety died her brothers.

229.play: KING RICHARD III: Lo, at their births good stars were opposite.
notes: good starres were opposite] 229. That is, an astrological sense, beneficent stars were in opposition to each other and thus their good effects were neutralised. Lilly (Astrology, p. 24) classes opposition among the ‘evil aspects.’—ED.

230.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: No, to their lives bad friends were contrary.

231.play: KING RICHARD III: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.
notes: vnavoyded] 231. MALONE—misled, possibly, by similarity in the form of the word—quotes as a parallel use: ‘Whose unavoided eye is murderous.’—IV, i, 66. SCHMIDT (Dict.) makes, I think, the proper distinction, that ‘unavoided’ here means inevitable, as in: ‘And unavoided is the danger now.’—Rich. II: II, i, 268; and that, in the line quoted by Malone, it means not avoided, not shunned. Schmidt does not give any other example of its use in this last sense. WRIGHT compares

232.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: True, when avoided grace makes destiny:

233.play: My babes were destined to a fairer death,

234.play: If grace had bless'd thee with a fairer life.

235.play: KING RICHARD III: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.

236.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Cousins, indeed; and by their uncle cozen'd
notes: Cosins . . . cousend] 236. Murray (N. E. D. s. v. cozen): This word has usually been associated with the substantive cousin and compared with French cousinier, explained by Cotgrave as 'to clayme kindred or advantage for particular ends; as he who to saue charges in trauelling goes from house to house as cosin to the owner of euerie one.' From this it is not far to a transitive sense, 'to beguile, cheat, under pretext of cousinship.' It is noteworthy that while in the noun cousin the ending in predominates, this verb has sometimes on, most commonly en. In view of this difficulty Mr Smythe Palmer has suggested derivation from Italian cozzonare, explained by Florio as 'to play the horse-breaker, or courser . . . Also to play the crafty knaue.' But this also presents difficulties which the extant evidence is not sufficient to remove.

237.play: Of comfort, kingdom, kindred, freedom, life.

238.play: Whose hand soever lanced their tender hearts,
notes: lanch'd] 238. Bradley (N. E. D. s. v. launch): To pierce, transfix, wound; cut, slit; to make a wound by piercing.

239.play: Thy head, all indirectly, gave direction:
notes: indirectly] gaue direction] 239. Steevens: Compare 'By indirections find directions out.'—Hamlet, II, i, 66; also: 'though indirect, Yet indirection thereby grows direct.'—King John, III, i, 275.

240.play: No doubt the murderous knife was dull and blunt

241.play: Till it was whetted on thy stone-hard heart,

242.play: To revel in the entrails of my lambs.

243.play: But that still use of grief makes wild grief tame,
notes: still vse] 243. That is, constant, continued use.

244.play: My tongue should to thy ears not name my boys

245.play: Till that my nails were anchor'd in thine eyes;
notes: Nayles . . . anchor’d in thine eyes] 245. ROLFE: Compare ‘I am not yet so low But that my nails can reach into thine eyes.’—Mid. N. Dream, III, ii, 298; and ‘Could I come near your beauty with my nails I’d set my ten commandments in your face.’—2 Hen. VI: I, iii, 144. In the latter case it is a duchess, as here a queen, that speaks. ‘Tempora mutantur,’ etc. [Compare, also, in Cleopatra’s violent speech to Seleucus: ‘Go back, I warrant thee; but I’ll catch thine eyes, though they had wings.’—V, ii, 156.]

246.play: And I, in such a desperate bay of death,

247.play: Like a poor bark, of sails and tackling reft,
notes: reft] 247. CRAIGIE (N. E. D. s. v. reave, v1): 3. To despoil, rob, or forcibly deprive (usually a person) of something. (In modern use chiefly in past participle, reft.)

248.play: Rush all to pieces on thy rocky bosom.

249.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise

250.play: And dangerous success of bloody wars,
notes: dangerous successe] 250. Is it possible that ‘success’ may here mean succession? as in: ‘—our parents’ noble names In whose success we are gentle.’—Wint. Tale, I, ii, 393; also: ‘And so success of mischief shall be born.’—2 Hen. IV: IV, ii, 47; these are the only examples of ‘success’ in this sense given by SCHMIDT (Lex.). He quotes the present line as an example of the meaning issue, result.

251.play: As I intend more good to you and yours,

252.play: Than ever you or yours were by me wrong'd!

253.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What good is cover'd with the face of heaven,

254.play: To be discover'd, that can do me good?
notes: discouered] 254. ‘Discover’ is, possibly, here used in the sense both of to uncover and also to find what before was hidden.

255.play: KING RICHARD III: The advancement of your children, gentle lady.

256.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Up to some scaffold, there to lose their heads?

257.play: KING RICHARD III: No, to the dignity and height of honour

258.play: The high imperial type of this earth's glory.
notes: The high Imperiall Type] 258. M. MASON: By this Richard means, a crown.—MALONE: Compare ‘Thy father bears the type of king of Naples.’—3 Hen. VI: I, iv, 121.—WRIGHT: Compare Richard’s speech to his soldiers, as reported in Hall’s Chronicle (p. 414): ‘By whose wisdome and polecie, I haue obteyned the crowne & type of this famous realme & noble region.’

259.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Flatter my sorrows with report of it;

260.play: Tell me what state, what dignity, what honour,

261.play: Canst thou demise to any child of mine?
notes: demise] 261. DAVIS (p. 205): ‘Demise’ is a conveyance, either in fee, for life, or for years. According to Chief Justice Gibson, the word strictly denotes a posthumous grant, and no more (5 Whart. 278).—ALLEN (p. 123): In Shakespeare’s time the words bequeath, inherit, and perhaps ‘demise,’ were not exclusively legal terms, and no strong argument is to be derived from their use in a sense which would be technically inaccurate. The word ‘demise,’ at least, seems to be here used not only in an untechnical, but in an unusual, sense. [MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. a) quotes the present line under: ‘To convey, transmit; to lease.’ The word does not occur elsewhere in Shakespeare.—Ed.]

262.play: KING RICHARD III: Even all I have; yea, and myself and all,

263.play: Will I withal endow a child of thine;
notes: withall] 263. ABBOTT (§ 196): ‘Withal’ is here not placed at the end of the sentence on account of the ‘all’ at the end of the previous verse.

264.play: So in the Lethe of thy angry soul

265.play: Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs

266.play: Which thou supposest I have done to thee.

267.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Be brief, lest that be process of thy kindness
notes: processe] 267. See IV, ii, 173.

268.play: Last longer telling than thy kindness' date.
notes: date] 268. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. substant. 2): 4. The time during which something lasts; period, season; duration; term of life or existence.

269.play: KING RICHARD III: Then know, that from my soul I love thy daughter.
notes: I loue thy Daughter] 269. OECHSLEHÄUSER (Essay, p. 114): Richard’s language throughout this whole dialogue manifests a self-control in strong contrast to that of his most hated enemy—Elizabeth; he firmly and at once represses any anger, which may often strive to blaze out, at her stubborn refusals to understand, and her deep-drawn lamentations. The hypocrisy of his love for Elizabeth’s daughter has an earnest and an honourable appearance—the king stands ever behind the lover. But in this repetition of the scene with Anne, Shakespeare should at least have omitted the threadbare motive that Richard murdered his nephews and wife through love for Elizabeth’s daughter, as once he had killed Edward and Henry through love for Anne. This same reason repeated to any character other than Elizabeth might, to the audience, be of value; it would, moreover, have had a strong effect on Anne, but certainly not on Elizabeth.

270.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My daughter's mother thinks it with her soul.

271.play: KING RICHARD III: What do you think?

272.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: That thou dost love my daughter from thy soul:
notes: loue... from thy soule] 272. The COWDEN-CLARKES: That the Queen here uses ‘from’ with bitter sarcasm, in the sense of apart from, is made evident by Richard’s hastily changing ‘from’ to ‘with,’ in line 275, which word she has previously used in the phrase ‘thinkis it with her soul.’—WRIGHT: A poor quibble but not unworthy of the dialogue. [ABBOTT (§ 158) gives numerous examples of ‘from’ used in the sense of apart from.]

273.play: So from thy soul's love didst thou love her brothers;

274.play: And from my heart's love I do thank thee for it.

275.play: KING RICHARD III: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning:

276.play: I mean, that with my soul I love thy daughter,

277.play: And mean to make her queen of England.
notes: England] 277. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 47): I do not know whether Shakespeare ever pronounced ‘England’ as a trisyllable. The corrector of the Folio read it as a disyllable and changed the metre [of the Quarto line] accordingly. [For examples wherein ‘England’ is apparently pronounced as a tri-syllable, see WALKER, Vers., p. 7.]

278.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Say then, who dost thou mean shall be her king?

279.play: KING RICHARD III: Even he that makes her queen who should be else?

281.play: KING RICHARD III: I, even I: what think you of it, madam?

282.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: How canst thou woo her?

283.play: KING RICHARD III: That would I learn of you,

284.play: As one that are best acquainted with her humour.

285.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: And wilt thou learn of me?
notes: And wilt thou learne] 285. ABBOTT (§ 99): That is: do you indeed wish to learn of me? Hence Ben Jonson, who quotes Chaucer: ‘What? quod she, and be ye wood? [Hous of Fame, Bk. iii, l. 623.—Ed.] adds that: ‘And, in the beginning of a sentence, serveth for admiration.’—[English Grammar], p. 789.

286.play: KING RICHARD III: Madam, with all my heart.
notes: Madam] 286. STEEVENS: I suppose this word may be safely omitted, as it violates the metre.—MALONE: No doubt that, or any other word, may be omitted, but not safely, unless it can be shown that we are at liberty to rewrite our author’s plays, and convert into verse what he has left us as prose.

287.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Send to her, by the man that slew her brothers,

288.play: A pair of bleeding-hearts; thereon engrave

289.play: Edward and York; then haply she will weep:

290.play: Therefore present to her--as sometime Margaret

291.play: Did to thy father, steep'd in Rutland's blood,--
notes: steep’t in Rutland’s blood] 291. See 3 Hen. VI: I, iv, 79–83; compare I, iii, 186 above.

292.play: A handkerchief; which, say to her, did drain

293.play: The purple sap from her sweet brother's body
notes: Brothers body] 293. CAPELL (ii, 188): Smother’d bodies, as were the princes’, emit no ‘purple sap’; therefore body is meant of another ‘brother,’ Lord Grey, who had a death to wet many ‘handkerchiefs.’

294.play: And bid her dry her weeping eyes therewith.

295.play: If this inducement force her not to love,

296.play: Send her a story of thy noble acts;

297.play: Tell her thou madest away her uncle Clarence,

298.play: Her uncle Rivers; yea, and, for her sake,

299.play: Madest quick conveyance with her good aunt Anne.
notes: conveyance] 299. DYCE (Gloss.): That is, juggling artifice, secret management.

300.play: KING RICHARD III: Come, come, you mock me; this is not the way

302.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: There is no other way

303.play: Unless thou couldst put on some other shape,

304.play: And not be Richard that hath done all this.

305.play: KING RICHARD III: Say that I did all this for love of her.

306.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Nay, then indeed she cannot choose but hate thee,
notes: she cannot choose but] 306. WRIGHT: That is, she must of necessity. Compare: ‘Yond same cloud cannot choose but fall by paifuls.’—Temp., II, ii, 24; and: ‘That cannot choose but amaze him.’—Merry Wives, V, iii, 18. [See, also, if needful, ABBOTT, § 353.]

hate thee] 306. TYRWHITT: The sense seems to require that we should read ‘love thee,’ ironically.—M. MASON: I should rather amend it by reading ‘have thee’; as the word have is more likely to have been mistaken for ‘hate,’ the traces of the letters being nearly the same.—STEEVENS: This conjecture is, in my opinion, at once fortunate and judicious. A somewhat corresponding error appears in Coriol., IV, iv, 23: ‘My birth place hate I,’ where have was apparently given instead of hate.—BOSWELL: It is by no means evident that this is spoken ironically; and, if not, the old reading affords a perfectly clear meaning. A virtuous woman would hate the man who thought to purchase her love by the commission of crimes.

307.play: Having bought love with such a bloody spoil.

308.play: KING RICHARD III: Look, what is done cannot be now amended:

notes: Men shall deale] 309. ABBOTT (§ 315): That is, men cannot help making mistakes.

310.play: Which after hours give leisure to repent.
notes: giues] 310. For this interpolation of s at the end of certain words in the Folio see note by WALKER, IV, i, 116.

311.play: If I did take the kingdom from your sons,

312.play: To make amends, Ill give it to your daughter.

313.play: If I have kill'd the issue of your womb,

314.play: To quicken your increase, I will beget

315.play: Mine issue of your blood upon your daughter

316.play: A grandam's name is little less in love

317.play: Than is the doting title of a mother;

318.play: They are as children but one step below,

319.play: Even of your mettle, of your very blood;
notes: mettal] 319. WRIGHT: No distinction is consistently made in spelling [in the Folio] between the literal and metaphorical meanings of this word. Like the Latin metallum, from which it is derived, it primarily denotes that which is dug out of a mine, and so, stuff or substance generally. Hence it is applied to that which forms the basis of character, and so disposition or temper.

320.play: Of an one pain, save for a night of groans
notes: Of all one paine] 320. CAPELL (ii, 188): In what sense are a daughter's children ‘of all one pain’ to the grandmother; that is, of the same pain to her that they are to the daughter? The exception [‘save for a night,’ etc.] directs us to it; the pain (that is, the solicitude) of both is one and the same because their love is the same—the mother’s is only heightened by consideration of that pain which she underwent in their births.
