321.play: Endured of her, for whom you bid like sorrow.
notes: bid] 321. HEATH (p. 300): We should read, ’bid, that is, abode, the preter perfect tense of the verb abide. The sense is, all proceeding from one and the same pang of child-birth which gave your daughter to the world, except those groans only which she in her turn must endure, as you have already endured them on her account.—JOHNSON: ‘Bid’ is in the past tense from bide.—MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. Abide. 16. To endure, suffer, bear, undergo, sustain.) gives two examples of the past tense abid.

322.play: Your children were vexation to your youth,

323.play: But mine shall be a comfort to your age.

324.play: The loss you have is but a son being king,

325.play: And by that loss your daughter is made queen.

326.play: I cannot make you what amends I would,

327.play: Therefore accept such kindness as I can.

328.play: Dorset your son, that with a fearful soul

329.play: Leads discontented steps in foreign soil,

330.play: This fair alliance quickly shall call home

331.play: To high promotions and great dignity:

332.play: The king, that calls your beauteous daughter wife.

333.play: Familiarly shall call thy Dorset brother;

334.play: Again shall you be mother to a king,

335.play: And all the ruins of distressful times

336.play: Repair'd with double riches of content.

337.play: What! we have many goodly days to see:

338.play: The liquid drops of tears that you have shed

339.play: Shall come again, transform'd to orient pearl,
notes: Orient Pearle] 339. Shakespeare uses ‘orient’ as an adjective applied to pearls in two other passages: Mid. N. Dream, IV, i, 59; and Ant. & Cleo., I, v, 41. On the foregoing passage from Mid. N. Dream Wright has the following note: ‘Compare: “Bright orient pearl, alack, too timely shaded.”—Pass. Pilgrim, 133 The epithet appears to be originally applied to the pearl and other gems as coming from the east, and to have acquired the general sense of bright and shining.

Love] 340. THEOBALD: My easy emendation [loan] will convince every reader that ‘love’ and lone are made out of one another only by a letter turned upside down. Thus: The tears that you have lent to your affections shall be turned into gems; and requite you by way of interest.

341.play: Of ten times double gain of happiness.

342.play: Go, then my mother, to thy daughter go

343.play: Make bold her bashful years with your experience;

344.play: Prepare her ears to hear a wooer's tale

345.play: Put in her tender heart the aspiring flame

346.play: Of golden sovereignty; acquaint the princess

347.play: With the sweet silent hours of marriage joys
notes: sweet silent hours] 347. The turbulent reign of Edward furnished but few opportunities for Elizabeth to enjoy any such hours; her first marriage with Sir John Grey might, perhaps, have afforded her some experience of them.—Ed.

348.play: And when this arm of mine hath chastised
notes: chastised] 348. WRIGHT: The accent is here on the first syllable, as in all other passages of Shakespeare, except Tro. & Cress., V, v, 4: ‘Tell her I have chastised the amorous Trojan.’

350.play: Bound with triumphant garlands will I come

351.play: And lead thy daughter to a conqueror's bed;

352.play: To whom I will retail my conquest won,
notes: retaile] 352. See III, i, 89.

353.play: And she shall be sole victress, Caesar's Caesar.

354.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What were I best to say? her father's brother

355.play: Would be her lord? or shall I say, her uncle?

356.play: Or, he that slew her brothers and her uncles?

357.play: Under what title shall I woo for thee,

358.play: That God, the law, my honour and her love,

359.play: Can make seem pleasing to her tender years?

360.play: KING RICHARD III: Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.
notes: [Inferred]] 360. See III, v, 81.

361.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Which she shall purchase with still lasting war.

362.play: KING RICHARD III: Say that the king, which may command, entreats.

363.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: That at her hands which the king's King forbids.
notes: the kings King forbid] 363. Grey (i, 64): Alluding to the prohibition in the Levitical law. See Leviticus xviii, 14.—Boswell: She rather means that her [Qu. his?] crimes would render such a marriage offensive to Heaven. This is consistent with the rest of the dialogue. [The chapter and verse cited by Grey refer to the illegality of marriage with a father's brother, or with a father's brother's wife. In the Book of Common Prayer, 1549, there is, following the Articles of Belief; a Table of Kindred and Affinity, wherein whosoever are related are forbidden in Scripture and our Laws to marry together, and therein 'Brothers' daughter' is enumerated among the prohibited relationships. Grey's reference to Leviticus has been uniformly followed, I believe, in all modern editions, but is it not more likely that it is to the foregoing Table that Elizabeth refers?—Ed.

364.play: KING RICHARD III: Say, she shall be a high and mighty queen.

365.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: To wail the tide, as her mother doth.
notes: vaile] 365. The Quarto reading, waile, is possibly right; but might not a meaning be obtained from the Folio—taking 'vail' in the sense of lower, as in, 'Vailing her high-top'?—Mer. of Ven., I, i, 28; and, 'thy vailed lids.'—Hamlet, I, ii, 70. By this interpretation the antithesis to 'high' in Richard's speech is preserved.—Ed.

366.play: KING RICHARD III: Say, I will love her everlastingly.

367.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But how long shall that title 'ever' last?

368.play: KING RICHARD III: Sweetly in force unto her fair life's end.

369.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But how long fairly shall her sweet lie last?

370.play: KING RICHARD III: So long as heaven and nature lengthens it.

371.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: So long as hell and Richard likes of it.
notes: likes of it] 371. Abbott (§ 177): 'Of' after 'to like' is perhaps a result of the old impersonal use of the verb me liketh, which might seem to disqualify the verb from taking a direct object. Similarly: it repents me of, becomes I repent of.

372.play: KING RICHARD III: Say, I, her sovereign, am her subject love.
notes: low] 372. WALKER (Crit., iii, 175): ‘Low’ cannot be from Shakespeare’s hand.—SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 28): Which [reading, that of Quarto or Folio] is right may be questioned; but right or wrong, the change requires no curious explanation.—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–76; p. 95): I am surprised to find that in Spedding’s opinion, ‘which reading is right may be questioned.’ Most readers, I think, will rather agree with Walker. Does not the alteration bear upon the face of it the mark of an injudicious corrector, in whose eyes love in the sense of lover was an offence?—WRIGHT: The Folio reading gives no opportunity for the play on words contained in ‘loathes’ in the next line.

373.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But she, your subject, loathes such sovereignty.

374.play: KING RICHARD III: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.

375.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: An honest tale speeds best being plainly told.

376.play: KING RICHARD III: Then in plain terms tell her my loving tale.
notes: Then plainly to her, etc.] 376. The COWDEN-CLARKES: It is worthy of observation that the dialogue in this scene, like that in I, ii, is in the same style of snip-snap rejoinder which pervades the dialogue in almost every scene of Love’s Lab. Lost. It is full of quibbling and word-catching, ingenious twisting of phrase, and wilful perversion of meanings: just the verbal fencing and adroit sentential play of fancy in which a student-pen, first essaying its skill, delights to exercise itself. [Might not this be summed up in the pedantic but convenient word stichomythia? Compare 360–378, above.—Ed.]

377.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Plain and not honest is too harsh a style.

378.play: KING RICHARD III: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.

379.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O no, my reasons are too deep and dead;

380.play: Too deep and dead, poor infants, in their grave.

381.play: KING RICHARD III: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.
notes: Harpe not on that string] 381. LOWELL (p. 96): Though this had long been a common expression, it is reasonable to suppose that Shakespeare borrowed it from More or from Holinshed . . . More says: ‘The Cardinall made a countinance to the other Lord, that he should harp no more upon that string’ [p. 54]. These words are spoken in the presence of Elizabeth, but are addressed to Lord Howard.

382.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Harp on it still shall I till heart-strings break.

383.play: KING RICHARD III: Now, by my George, my garter, and my crown,--
notes: my George] 383. COURTENAY (Comment., ii, p. 107, foot-note): There is here an inaccuracy, when Richard swears by his George, a badge not used in his time.— ASHMOLE (p. 173, § 6), after describing the robes and hoods of the Order of the Garter, says: ‘There remains now the Collar and George, brought in by King Henry VIII.’ Again, speaking of the collar, he says, p. 174: ‘—nor ought the collar to be adorned with precious stones (as the George may be) such being prohibited by the Law of the Order. At the middle of the Collar before pendant at the Table of one of the Garters in the Collar is to be fix’d the image of St George arm’d sitting on Horseback, who having thrown the Dragon on his Back encounters him with a Tilting-Spear.’

384.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Profaned, dishonour'd, and the third usurp'd.

386.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: By nothing; for this is no oath:

387.play: The George, profaned, hath lost his holy honour;
notes: Lordly] 387. PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 112): The Folio, probably to get rid of the alliteration, changes the Quarto reading holy. But surely this involves a sacrifice of the proper epithet, which Shakespeare would not have made. To say nothing of the sacred character of the George, the word ‘profaned’ shows that holy is the true reading.

388.play: The garter, blemish'd, pawn'd his knightly virtue;

389.play: The crown, usurp'd, disgraced his kingly glory.

390.play: if something thou wilt swear to be believed,

391.play: Swear then by something that thou hast not wrong'd.

392.play: KING RICHARD III: Now, by the world--

393.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: 'Tis full of thy foul wrongs.

394.play: KING RICHARD III: My father's death--

395.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Thy life hath that dishonour'd.

396.play: KING RICHARD III: Then, by myself--

398.play: KING RICHARD III: Why then, by God--

399.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: God's wrong is most of all.

400.play: If thou hadst fear'd to break an oath by Him,

401.play: The unity the king thy brother made

402.play: Had not been broken, nor my brother slain:
notes: Brothers] 402. Earl Rivers is the only brother of Elizabeth introduced in the present play; for an attempt to explain this use of the plural, see note by DANIEL, I, iii, 42.

403.play: If thou hadst fear'd to break an oath by Him,

404.play: The imperial metal, circling now thy brow,

405.play: Had graced the tender temples of my child,

406.play: And both the princes had been breathing here,

407.play: Which now, two tender playfellows to dust,
notes: Bed-fellowes] 407. R. G. WHITE: ‘Bed-fellows’ for play-fellows, the Quarto reading, is an improvement of the poetic beauty of the line too obvious to need comment. Variations such as these are evidently the result of the author’s revision of the play. And the addition of ‘What canst thou swear by now,’ making with the reply (which is found in the Qq) a perfect verse, shows at least authoritative resta

408.play: Thy broken faith hath made a prey for worms.

409.play: What canst thou swear by now?

410.play: KING RICHARD III: The time to come.

411.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: That thou hast wronged in the time o'erpast;

412.play: For I myself have many tears to wash

413.play: Hereafter time, for time past wrong'd by thee.

414.play: The children live, whose parents thou hast
notes: Fathers] 414. BARNARD thinks that Elizabeth here refers to the children of Clarence, orphaned by Richard; but Elizabeth had no valid reason to accuse Richard of this crime: Edward, in her presence, had assumed all responsibility.—ED.

416.play: Ungovern'd youth, to wail it in their age;

417.play: The parents live, whose children thou hast butcher'd,

418.play: Old wither'd plants, to wail it with their age.
notes: waile it with their Age] 418. DANIEL, who seeks to establish a direct connection between Qq and F1, says (Introd., p. x), in regard to this passage: ‘Here Qq puts in its solitary claim to exclusive connection with F1 [see Text. Notes]; but all things considered, I am disposed to think that, so far from this case telling against Qq, it affords proof in its favour. In their is clearly the right reading, and it is quite possible that the scribe who prepared the “copy” for F1, with the theatrical MS before him and working on a copy Qq, may, in correcting the obvious blunder her, have overlooked the other correction of “with” to in which he should also have made. This is one of the errors into which correctors for the press are, I know from experience, very liable to fall.’ [For a summary of DANIEL’s theory see Appendix: The Text.]

419.play: Swear not by time to come; for that thou hast

420.play: Misused ere used, by time misused o'erpast.

421.play: KING RICHARD III: As I intend to prosper and repent,

422.play: So thrive I in my dangerous attempt
notes: Affayres] 422. VERPLANCK: The Quarto reading attempts, as it might here imply not defensive war, but hostile aggression, I take to have been changed by the author.

423.play: Of hostile arms! myself myself confound!

424.play: Heaven and fortune bar me happy hours!

425.play: Day, yield me not thy light; nor, night, thy rest!

426.play: Be opposite all planets of good luck
notes: opposite] 426. ROLFE: That is, opposed, adverse. Shakespeare mentions ‘planets’ nearly a score of times, but always with an astrological reference.

427.play: To my proceedings, if, with pure heart's love,

429.play: I tender not thy beauteous princely daughter!
notes: tender] 429. That is, regard, hold in high estimation; compare I, i, 50; II, iv, 82.

430.play: In her consists my happiness and thine;

431.play: Without her, follows to this land and me,

432.play: To thee, herself, and many a Christian soul,

434.play: It cannot be avoided but by this;

435.play: It will not be avoided but by this.

436.play: Therefore, good mother,--I must can you so--

437.play: Be the attorney of my love to her:

438.play: Plead what I will be, not what I have been;

439.play: Not my deserts, but what I will deserve:

440.play: Urge the necessity and state of times,

441.play: And be not peevish-fond in great designs.
notes: peeuish found] 441. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 29): Here the Folio is certainly wrong. One cannot fancy Shakespeare making that alteration. But then it is easy to fancy a printer or transcriber not understanding the effect of the double epithet (peevish-fond) and supposing fond a mistake for ‘found,’ which makes very good sense.

442.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Shall I be tempted of the devil thus?

443.play: KING RICHARD III: Ay, if the devil tempt thee to do good.

444.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Shall I forget myself to be myself?

445.play: KING RICHARD III: Ay, if yourself's remembrance wrong yourself.

446.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But thou didst kill my children.

447.play: KING RICHARD III: But in your daughter's womb I bury them:

448.play: Where in that nest of spicery they shall breed

449.play: Selves of themselves, to your recomforture.
notes: recomforture] 449. That is, renewed comfort. CRAIGIE (N. E. D. s. v.) quotes the present instance as the only example of the use of this word.—Ed.

450.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Shall I go win my daughter to thy will?
notes: Shall I go win my daughter] 450. OECHSLEHAUSER (Essay, p. 116): Here we, who simply read the drama, feel for the second time in the same scene, and the second follows close upon the first, that the important psychologic moment in the action does not at once make itself apparent, but that, in order to comprehend it, recourse must be had to the aesthetic critic; I refer to Richard's behaviour under his mother's curse, and here Elizabeth's at the close of this wooing scene. In its arrangement for the stage this is a manifest error on Shakespeare's part. In so important a period of the action, the correct interpretation of the poet's idea should not be left to the actor's conception alone, either to distort or completely destroy. A word or two spoken aside—even a stage-direction, if need be—would have been sufficient to dispel all doubts as to the correct interpretation.

451.play: KING RICHARD III: And be a happy mother by the deed.
notes: And be a happy Mother] 451. See I. 285 ante.—HUDSON (Life, etc., p. 166): It appears somewhat uncertain whether Elizabeth is really beguiled by Richard's wizard rhetoric, or whether she only temporizes, and feigns a reluctant acquiescence, and so at last fairly outwits him. Most critics, I believe, have taken the former view; but I am far from seeing it so: for her daughter's hand is firmly pledged to Richmond already, and she is in the whole secret of the plot for seating him on the throne. So I take it as an instance of that profound yet innocent and almost unconscious guile which women are apt to use in defence of those they love, and which so often proves an overmatch for all the sources of deliberate craft.

452.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: I go. Write to me very shortly.
notes: shortly] 452. STEEVENS: This adverb, in the present instance, is employed as a trisyllable.—WALKER (Vers., 23) quotes this line as an example wherein 'shortly' seems to be so pronounced, and adds: 'But I suspect some error.' So also ABBOTT, § 477.

453.play: And you shall understand from me her mind.

454.play: KING RICHARD III: Bear her my true love's kiss; and so, farewell.

notes: Exit Q.] 455. WRIGHT: At the close of the narrative which suggested this scene, Hall has the following profound remark: ‘Surely the inconstancie of this woman were muche to be merueled at, yf all women had bene found constante, but let men speake, yet wemen of the verie bond of nature will followe their awne kynde.’—p. 406.

456.play: Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!
notes: Relenting Foole, etc.] 456. HORN (iii, 144): We might well think that in a scene, such as the foregoing, with its rumblings of thunder, its flashes of lightning, its sepulchral horror, and clarion-tongued justice, that even Richard would be somewhat daunted, but then we should forget that which Richard ever held steadfast in view: ‘I am determined to become a villain.’ In everything which

shallow-changing] 456. WALKER (Crit., i, 26), among many other examples of such compound adjectives, compares: ‘According to my shallow-simple skill.’—Two Gent., II, i, 8; ‘that loose grace Which shallow-laughing hearers give to fools.’—Love’s Labour’s, V, ii, 870. But in the present instance ‘shallow-changing’ is the reading of F1; and in the two examples quoted by Walker the hyphen is his own substitution for a comma, wherein he has been followed by only one or two modern editors.—Ed.

459.play: RATCLIFF: My gracious sovereign, on the western coast

460.play: Rideth a puissant navy; to the shore

462.play: Unarm'd, and unresolved to beat them back:

463.play: 'Tis thought that Richmond is their admiral;

464.play: And there they hull, expecting but the aid
notes: hull] 464. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. verb): Of a ship: To float or be driven by the force of the wind, or current on the hull alone; to drift to the wind with sails furled; to lie a-hull. [The present line quoted.]

465.play: Of Buckingham to welcome them ashore.

466.play: KING RICHARD III: Some light-foot friend post to the Duke of Norfolk:
notes: Some light-foot friend, etc.] 466. JOHNSON: Richard’s precipitation and confusion is, in this scene, very happily represented, by inconsistent orders, and sudden variations of opinion.—CHURCHILL (p. 517): The same purpose is apparent in this scene between Richard, Catesby, and Ratcliffe, as in that between Richard and Catesby in the True Tragedy, wherein it is shown how Richard is unnerved by Richmond’s conspiracy and by his conscience. He does not know whether he has called Catesby or not, misunderstands Catesby’s hope that his troubles will be overcome as a hope that he himself will be overcome, and flies into a passion when Catesby praises Richmond. [See Appendix: The True Tragedie, etc., p. 533.]

467.play: Ratcliff, thyself, or Catesby; where is he?

469.play: KING RICHARD III: Fly to the duke:

notes: Salisbury] 471. BARNARD: At Salisbury Richard would be in a position to intercept the junction of Buckingham and Richmond.

475.play: Why stand'st thou still, and go'st not to the duke?

476.play: CATESBY: First, mighty sovereign, let me know your mind,

477.play: What from your grace I shall deliver to him.

478.play: KING RICHARD III: O, true, good Catesby: bid him levy straight
notes: O true, good Catesby] 478. HUDSON (Life, etc., ii, 151): By his bland apology implied in, ‘O true, good Catesby,’ which drops so easily that it seems to spring fresh from his heart, Richard instantly charms out the sting of his former words, and we feel that the man is knit closer to him than ever. Yet his kingly dignity is not a whit impaired, nay, is even heightened, by the act, partly from his graciousness of manner, and partly from his quick art in putting the apology under a sort of transparent disguise.

479.play: The greatest strength and power he can make,

480.play: And meet me presently at Salisbury.

483.play: RATCLIFF: What is't your highness' pleasure I shall do at

485.play: KING RICHARD III: Why, what wouldst thou do there before I go?

486.play: RATCLIFF: Your highness told me I should post before.

487.play: KING RICHARD III: My mind is changed, sir, my mind is changed.
notes: My minde is chang’d] 487. Verplanck: By the repetition of these words, in the Qq, and also those in l. 496, the author’s first intention may have been to express the hurry and excitement of rapid action; which, in revision, may well have seemed to him inconsistent with the cool self-possession and lofty bearing of Richard.

489.play: How now, what news with you?

490.play: STANLEY: None good, my lord, to please you with the hearing;

491.play: Nor none so bad, but it may well be told.

492.play: KING RICHARD III: Hoyday, a riddle! neither good nor bad!

493.play: Why dost thou run so many mile about,

494.play: When thou mayst tell thy tale a nearer way?

496.play: STANLEY: Richmond is on the seas.

497.play: KING RICHARD III: There let him sink, and be the seas on him!
notes: There let him sinke] 497. Drake (p. 443): This reply is pointed with irony and invective. There are two causes in nature and character for this: First, Richard

498.play: White-liver'd runagate, what doth he there?
notes: White-liver'd] 498. Compare: ‘Go prick thy face, and over-red thy fear Thou lily-liver’d boy.’—Macb., V, iii, 14.

Runagate] 498. Skeat (Dict. s. v.): It so happens that gate in many English dialects signifies a way; whilst at the same time the Middle English verb remen passed into the form run, as at present. Hence the Middle English renegat, a renegade, was popularly supposed to stand for renne a gate, that is, to run on the way, and was turned into ‘runagate’ accordingly; especially as we also have the word runaway. But it is certain that the original sense of Middle English renegat was apostate or villain; ‘Renegat: a renegade, one that abjures his religion.’—Cotgrave. It is remarkable that when renegat had been corrupted into ‘runagate,’ we borrowed the word over again, in the form renegade, from Spanish renegado. It is a pity we could not do without it altogether.

499.play: STANLEY: I know not, mighty sovereign, but by guess.

500.play: KING RICHARD III: Well, sir, as you guess, as you guess?
notes: Well, as you guesse] 500. R. G. White (Sh.’s Scholar, p. 343): If there be two words for the use of which, more than any others, our English cousins twit us, they are ‘well,’ as an interrogative exclamation, and ‘guess.’ Milton uses both, as Shakespeare also frequently does, and exactly in the way in which they are used in America; and here we have them both in half a line. Like most of those words and phrases which it pleases John Bull to call Americanisms, they are English of the purest and best, which have lived here while they have died out in the mother country. [Is the phrase, ‘as you guess,’ as here used, an Americanism?—Ed.]

501.play: STANLEY: Stirr'd up by Dorset, Buckingham, and Ely,

502.play: He makes for England, there to claim the crown.

503.play: KING RICHARD III: Is the chair empty? is the sword unsway'd?

504.play: Is the king dead? the empire unpossess'd?

505.play: What heir of York is there alive but we?
notes: What Heire of Yorke? 505. MALONE: Richard asks this question in the plenitude of power, and no one dares to answer him. But they whom he addresses, had they not been intimidated, might have told him that there was a male heir of the house of York alive, who had a better claim to the throne than he; Edward, Earl of Warwick, the only son of the usurper’s elder brother, George, Duke of Clarence; and Elizabeth, the eldest daughter of Edward IV., and all her sisters, had a better title than either of them.—RITSON: The issue of King Edward had been pronounced illegitimate the Duke of Clarence attainted of high treason,—and the usurper declared 'the undoubted heir of Richard duke of York,'—by act of parliament: so that, as far as such a proceeding can alter the constitution and legalize usurpation and murder, he is perfectly correct and unanswerable.—R. G. WHITE, after giving the substance of the foregoing notes by Malone and Ritson, adds: ‘Although Edward’s issue had been pronounced illegitimate, and Clarence attainted of high treason, yet this was unjustly done by procurement of Richard himself.' [On the question of Richard’s influence in procuring these acts of parliament opinion is somewhat divided. More, who was followed closely by Vergil, Hall and Holinshed, says: ‘Somme wise menne also weene, that his drifte couterly conauyde, lacked not in helping furth his brother of Clarence to his death: which he resisted openly, howbeit somewhat (as menne demed) more faintly then he that wer hartely minded to his welth.’—p. 10. On the other hand, in the account of the proceedings against Clarence as given in Ingulph’s Chronicle of the Abbey of Croyland (commonly called the second continuation of the Croyland Chronicle), written about 1485, and thus nearer to the period of which it treats, it is stated: ‘—not a single person uttered a word against the Duke, except the King; not one individual made answer to the King except the Duke.’—p. 479. An extract so pertinent has naturally not escaped the vigilance of both Legge and Churchill. Buck says (p. 82): ‘it [Clarence’s death] was the King’s owne immoveable and inexpiorable doome who thought it justly and necessarily his due; for Clarence stood guilty of many treasons and great ones.' More directly accuses Richard of the slander on the Duchess of York, which made Edward’s issue illegitimate: ‘But the chief thing and the weighty of al that inuenion, rested in this that they should allege bastardy, either in King Edward himself, or in his children or both. So that he should seme dishabled to inheirte the crowne by the duke of Yorke, and the prince by him.’—p. 89. The Rotuli Parliamentorum contains, in full, the Act of Attainder against the Duke of Clarence, and that Richard was not responsible for the dastardly act of defaming his mother and brother is shown by the following: ‘And overe this, the said Duke beyng in full purpose to exalt hymself and his Heires to the Regallye and Corone of Englande, and erely in opinion to putte aside from the same for ever the said Corone from the Kyng and his Heirez, upon oon the falsest and moost unnatural coloured pretence that man might imagyne, falsely and untreuly noyed, published and saide, that the Kynge oure Sovereigne Lorde was a Bastard, and not begotten to reigne upon us.’—vol. vi, p. 194. Legge quotes the foregoing from the Rolls of Parliaments, but has not, I think, gone to the original source for his extract. This something what long note should, perhaps, be placed more fittingly among those referring to Richard’s complicity in the death of Clarence. Its answer to White’s charge, contained in his note, must be a justification for its present position.—ED.]

506.play: And who is England's king but great York's heir?

507.play: Then, tell me, what doth he upon the sea?

508.play: STANLEY: Unless for that, my liege, I cannot guess.
notes: Unless for that . . . I cannot guesse] 508. HUNTER (Illust., ii, 93): These words convey no idea. They are, in fact, a broken sentence—Richard interrupts him.—DYCE: Not so, surely. Stanley has previously told Richard his ‘guess’ in the matter; and Richard again having put to him the question, what Richmond ‘makes upon the sea,’ Stanley now replies: ‘Unless for that (i. e., the reason already given), I cannot guess.’ Richard immediately catches up the words: ‘Unless for that,’ using ‘for that’ in a different sense.

509.play: KING RICHARD III: Unless for that he comes to be your liege,

510.play: You cannot guess wherefore the Welshman comes.

511.play: Thou wilt revolt, and fly to him, I fear.

512.play: STANLEY: No, mighty liege; therefore mistrust me not.
notes: mistrust me not] 512. RAMSAY (ii, 573): Throughout the revolutions of the last six and twenty years the House of Stanley had been singularly successful in keeping out of hot water. Thomas, the present Lord, had throughout supported the Yorkist cause; but without committing himself to such an extent as to involve the active hostility of the Lancastrians, when their fortunes were in the ascendant. He had kept clear of Warwick and Clarence and their intrigues. . . . On the list of those who had profited by Richard’s bounty Stanley’s name was second only to those of Norfolk and Northumberland. Men wondered that Richard could trust the Stanleys; but if Richard were here to explain his conduct, he would doubtless tell us that he could not help himself; and that he had to trust them and hope for the best.

513.play: KING RICHARD III: Where is thy power, then, to beat him back?
notes: Power] 513. GUEST (p. 40): The disyllables containing y and w seem to have been once so numerous in our language that many words, both English and foreign, were adapted to their pronunciation, and thus gained a syllable; scūr, Anglo-Saxon, became shower, and fleur, French, became flower. Change of pronunciation has again reduced them to their original dimensions.

514.play: Where are thy tenants and thy followers?

515.play: Are they not now upon the western shore.

516.play: Safe-conducting the rebels from their ships!

517.play: STANLEY: No, my good lord, my friends are in the north.

518.play: KING RICHARD III: Cold friends to Richard: what do they in the north,
notes: Cold friends to me . . . in the North] 518. SPEDDING (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-76; p. 48): This [line, as in the Quarto], in any of Shakespeare's later plays, would be a common form of line: the redundant syllable, coming in the middle of the line immediately before a strong pause, drops, as it were, into the pause, and scarcely disturbs the measure more than the redundant syllable at the end of the line. But in the plays of the second period I think you will find very few such, and at the time when the inserted passages—the more considerable of them—were composed, I doubt whether Shakespeare would have admitted them at all. To get rid of it here, Richard is changed in the Folio to 'me.'—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875-76; p. 101): There is a stage-tradition, I believe, that the delivery of this line, as in the Quarto, was one of the finest 'points' in Edmund Kean's impersonation of Richard; perhaps, if Spedding had heard him, he might have been careful to omit this passage from the list of his examples [of alterations made to improve the metre].—WRIGHT: The Folio reading destroys what little force there was in the line.

519.play: When they should serve their sovereign in the west?
notes: When they . . . in the West] 519. T. R. GOULD (p. 46): This line was delivered by J. B. Booth in one continuous tone of commanding resonance, in which the words were dropped like stones in the current of his speech.

520.play: STANLEY: They have not been commanded, mighty sovereign:

521.play: Please it your majesty to give me leave,
notes: Pleaseth your Majestie] 521. Spedding, in his enumeration of alterations in the Folio not intended by Shakespeare (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 30), quotes this phrase and adds: ‘There is at least one other instance in Shakespeare of the use of “pleaseth” in this way, Com. of Err., IV, i, 12. But, for our present purpose, it is needless to discuss it; for it may as easily be a misprint as a change of construction.’ The construction in Com. of Err., IV, i, 12: ‘Pleaseth you walk with me,’ is pace Spedding, possibly open to question as to its parallelism with the present instance. But there can be no doubt, I think, that we have exactly parallel examples in the following: ‘Pleaseth your lordship to meet his grace.’—2 Hen. IV: IV, i, 225; ‘Pleaseth your grace to answer him directly.’—Ibid., IV, ii, 53; ‘Pleaseth your grace to appoint some of your council.’—Hen. V: V, ii, 78. It is, perhaps, noteworthy that in these three passages the Folio retains the Quarto reading ‘pleaseth.’—Ed.

522.play: I'll muster up my friends, and meet your grace

523.play: Where and what time your majesty shall please.

524.play: KING RICHARD III: Ay, ay. thou wouldst be gone to join with Richmond:
notes: I, thou would’st be gone] 524. Pickersgill (Sh. Soc., Trans., 1875–’76; p. 97): I should regard, the Folio reading here, as a misprint [see Text. Notes], were it not that the corrector has displayed in similar passages an inveterate dislike of this thoroughly dramatic repetition of a word (a different thing altogether, it will be observed, from the recurrence of the same word). With regard to the metre, the line in the Folio, although it will not scan, is yet decasyllabic; which might, in the judgement of the corrector, be sufficient.

525.play: I will not trust you, sir.

527.play: You have no cause to hold my friendship doubtful:

528.play: I never was nor never will be false.

530.play: Go muster men; but, hear you, leave behind

531.play: Your son, George Stanley: look your faith be firm.

532.play: Or else his head's assurance is but frail.

533.play: STANLEY: So deal with him as I prove true to you.

536.play: Messenger: My gracious sovereign, now in Devonshire,

537.play: As I by friends am well advertised,

538.play: Sir Edward Courtney, and the haughty prelate

539.play: Bishop of Exeter, his brother there,
notes: Bishop of Exeter] 539. FRENCH (p. 249): Peter Courtenay, who became Bishop of Exeter in 1478, was a zealous Lancastrian, and had to seek refuge in Brittany, whence he returned with the Earl of Richmond.

540.play: With many more confederates, are in arms.
notes: moe] 540. SKEAT (Did. s. v.): The modern English more does duty for two Middle English words which were generally well distinguished, viz., mo and more, the former relating to number, the latter to size. ‘Mo than thries ten,’ more than thirty in number; Chaucer: Cant. Tales, 578... The distinction between mo and more is not always observed in old authors, but very often it appears clearly enough.

542.play: Second Messenger: My liege, in Kent the Guildfords are in arms;
notes: the Guildfords] 542. COURTENAY (ii, 110): The Guildfords were a distinguished family seated at Hempstead in Kent. Sir Richard was K. G. under Henry VII., and Sir Edward, his son, was father-in-law to John Dudley, Duke of Northumberland.
Competitors] 543. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): 2. One associated with another in seeking the same common object; an associate, a partner. [The present line quoted.]

544.play: Flock to their aid, and still their power increaseth.

546.play: Third Messenger: My lord, the army of the Duke of Buckingham--

547.play: KING RICHARD III: Out on you, owls! nothing but songs of death?
notes: Owles . . . Songs of Death] 547. Pliny says (ch. xii, p. 276): ‘The Scritch-Owle alwaies betokeneth some heauie newes and is most execrable and accursed, and namely, in the presages of public affaires, he keepeth ever in desarts: and loueth not only such vnpcoped places, but also that are horrible and hard of accesse. In summer he is the very monster of the night, neither crying nor singing out cleare, but vttering a heauie groane of doleful mourning.’ Tawney refers, for a further account of the import of the cry of the Owl, to Brand iii, 206–210.—Ed.

549.play: Take that, until thou bring me better news.

550.play: Third Messenger: The news I have to tell your majesty

551.play: Is, that by sudden floods and fall of waters,
notes: sudden Floods, and fall of Waters] 551. Barnard: The king’s supporters had broken down the bridges over the Severn, and Buckingham found it impossible to unite his Welsh forces with the insurgents from Devon, for the whole country between Gloucester and Bristol was impassable owing to the floods. With the rains came the great gale which drove back Richmond’s ships. All this, together with an eclipse of the moon, was sufficient to persuade Buckingham’s Keltic followers that Heaven was against them, and they dispersed.

552.play: Buckingham's army is dispersed and scatter'd;

553.play: And he himself wander'd away alone,

555.play: KING RICHARD III: I cry thee mercy:
notes: I cry thee mercie] 555. Compare I, iii, 246; and V, iii, 261; see, if needful, Abbott, § 201.

556.play: There is my purse to cure that blow of thine.

558.play: Reward to him that brings the traitor in?

559.play: Third Messenger: Such proclamation hath been made, my liege.
notes: Proclamation hath been made] 559. BOSWELL-STONE (p. 404, foot-note): [Richard’s proclamation is printed in] Rymer’s Foedera, vol. xii, p. 204; it is dated 23 October, 1483.

561.play: Fourth Messenger: Sir Thomas Lovel and Lord Marquis Dorset,
notes: Sir Thomas Lovell] 561. WRIGHT: Sir Thomas Lovel was Chancellor of the Exchequer and Treasurer of the Household to Henry VII. and was one of Lady Margaret’s Executors. In the play of Hen. VIII. he appears as Constable of the Tower.

562.play: 'Tis said, my liege, in Yorkshire are in arms.

563.play: Yet this good comfort bring I to your grace,

564.play: The Breton navy is dispersed by tempest:
notes: The Brittaine Nauie] 564. See IV, ii, 182.

565.play: Richmond, in Yorkshire, sent out a boat

566.play: Unto the shore, to ask those on the banks

567.play: If they were his assistants, yea or no;
notes: yea, or no] 567. The following rule for the use of yea and nay, and yes and no, is given by Sir T. More in his Confutacion of Tyndales Aunswere: ‘I woulde not here note by the way, that Tyndal here translateth noe for nay, for it is but a trifle and mistaking of the englishe worde . . . . For the use of those two words in answering to a question is this. No aunswereth the question framed by the affirmative. As for example, if a manne should aske Tindall hymselfe: ys an heretike mete to translate holy scripture into englishe. So to thys question if he will aunswere trew englishe, he must aunswere nay and not no. But and if the question be asked hym thus lo: Is not an heretyque mete to translate holy scripture into english. To this question lo if he will aunswer true english, he must aunswere

568.play: Who answer'd him, they came from Buckingham.

569.play: Upon his party: he, mistrusting them,
notes: Vpon his parte] 569. Compare I, iii, 147; and III, ii, 54, for other examples of this phrase.

570.play: Hoisted sail and made away for Brittany.
notes: Hoys’d] 570. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. verb 1): To raise aloft by means of a rope or pulley and tackle, or by other mechanical appliances. (a) Originally nautical, and chiefly to hoise sail; often with up. It is to be noticed that the word appears early as an interjection, being the actual cry of sailors in hauling.

571.play: KING RICHARD III: March on, march on, since we are up in arms;

572.play: If not to fight with foreign enemies,

573.play: Yet to beat down these rebels here at home.

575.play: CATESBY: My liege, the Duke of Buckingham is taken;

576.play: That is the best news: that the Earl of Richmond

577.play: Is with a mighty power landed at Milford,
notes: Landed at Milford] 577. GARDINER (p. 269): On the First of August, Richmond embarked at Harfleur, with all his English followers.... He had a prosperous wind, and arrived at Milford Haven on the 7th or 8th.—WRIGHT: A gap of two years in the history is here bridged over by the dramatist. Richmond embarked on his first fruitless expedition to join Buckingham on 12 October, 1483, but his actual landing at Milford did not take place until August, 1485.

578.play: Is colder tidings, yet they must be told.

579.play: KING RICHARD III: Away towards Salisbury! while we reason here,

580.play: A royal battle might be won and lost

581.play: Some one take order Buckingham be brought

582.play: To Salisbury; the rest march on with me.

1.play: [Enter DERBY and SIR CHRISTOPHER URSWICK]
notes: Scena Quarta] 1. WRIGHT: The scene is probably at Knowsley in Lancashire, where the Lady Margaret was living when Morton first opened negotiations with her for the return of her son. But it is a little uncertain, because when the queen proposed the marriage of Richmond with the Princess Elizabeth, the Lady Margaret was at her husband's house in London, afterwards Derby House, the site of which is occupied by the Herald's College.—BOSWELL-STONE (p. 490): The historic date of this scene must be August, 1485, but Stanley gives Urswick a message relating to a matter which had been settled in 1483. When the marriage between Richmond and the Princess Elizabeth had been arranged, 'the countesse of Richmond tooke into hir service Christopher Urswike, an honest and wise priest . . . So the mother, studious for the prosperitie of hir son appointed this Christopher Urswike to saile into Britaine to the earle of Richmond, and to declare and reueale to him all pacts and agreements betweene hir & the queene agreed and concluded.'—Holinshed, iii, 742.—W. STRUNK, JR. (MS note): This scene has a close parallel (at exactly the same place in the play) in 1 Hen. IV: IV, iv, in which, on the eve of battle the Archbishop of York entrusts his chaplain, Sir Michael, with letters.

2.play: DERBY: Sir Christopher, tell Richmond this from me:
notes: Sir] 2. WRIGHT: The title 'Sir' which is here given to Urswick is out of place, for he was at this time LL. D. and master of King's Hall, Cambridge (1483-1488). [For this title 'Sir' given to priests, see III, ii, 123.]
Sir Christopher] 2. See Dram. Person.

3.play: That in the sty of this most bloody boar

4.play: My son George Stanley is frank'd up in hold:
notes: in hold] 4. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. hold subst.): 4. Custody, confinement, imprisonment. Chiefly in phrases, in hold (in holds, at, to hold).

5.play: If I revolt, off goes young George's head;

6.play: The fear of that withholds my present aid.

7.play: But, tell me, where is princely Richmond now?

8.play: CHRISTOPHER: At Pembroke, or at Harford-west, in Wales.

9.play: DERBY: What men of name resort to him?

10.play: CHRISTOPHER: Sir Walter Herbert, a renowned soldier;
notes: Sir Walter Herbert] 10. FRENCH (p. 238): Sir Walter was the second son of Sir William Herbert, a staunch Yorkist, created by Edward IV. Baron Herbert, and later Earl of Pembroke, and who is, no doubt, the nobleman of that title in 3 Hen. VI.

11.play: Sir Gilbert Talbot, Sir William Stanley;
notes: Sir Gilbert . . . Stanley] 11. ABBOTT (§ 469): It may be remarked that great license is taken with the metre wherever a list of names occurs. In the present line, and in some others, the pause between two names seems to license either the insertion or omission of a syllable.
Sir Gilbert Talbot] 11. WRIGHT: Sir Gilbert Talbot, called Sir George here by Hall, but afterwards Sir Gilbert, joined Henry at Newport. He was uncle to the young Earl of Shrewsbury.

12.play: Oxford, redoubted Pembroke, Sir James Blunt,
notes: redoubted Pembroke] 12. FRENCH (p. 250): This is Jaspar Tudor, uncle of the Earl of Richmond. After the victory at Bosworth, to which he so greatly contributed, he was created Duke of Bedford, and obtained from Henry VII. many high appointments and valuable grants.

13.play: And Rice ap Thomas with a valiant crew;
notes: Rice ap Thomas] 13. FULLER (iii, 522) says: ‘Sir Rice ap Thomas was never more than a knight, yet little less than a prince in this his native county [Carmarthenshire], if the author of Prælia Anglorum may not be believed—'Ricius Thomas floa Cambro-Brittanum.’ King Henry the Seventh will himself witness his worth. To him, lately landed at Milford Haven with contemptible forces, this Sir Rice repaired with a considerable accession of choice soldiers, marching with them to Bosworth field, where he right valiantly behaved himself. That thrifty King, according to his cheap course of remuneration (rewarding gownsmen in orders, by him most employed, with church livings, and swordsmen with honour) afterwards made Sir Rice knight of the order; and well might he give him a garter, by whose effectual help he recovered a crown.’ [FRENCH also quotes a portion of the above passage from Fuller.—ED.]

crew] 13. STEEVENS says this expression ‘sounds but meanly in modern ears.’ According to MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. I. 1) it was originally applied almost exclusively to ‘military reinforcements’; his earliest quotation of ‘crew’ used in this sense is from Rolls of Parliament, 1455. That the word was later used derogatively is shown by many examples (Ibid., s. v. 4), the earliest dated 1570. Shakespeare appears to have used ‘crew’ in both senses; but here it is, I think, used for military reinforcements.—ED.

14.play: And many more of noble fame and worth:

15.play: And towards London they do bend their course,

16.play: If by the way they be not fought withal.

17.play: DERBY: Return unto thy lord; commend me to him:

18.play: Tell him the queen hath heartily consented

19.play: He shall espouse Elizabeth her daughter.

20.play: These letters will resolve him of my mind. Farewell.

21.play: [Exeunt]

1.play: [Enter the Sheriff, and BUCKINGHAM, with halberds, led to execution]

2.play: BUCKINGHAM: Will not King Richard let me speak with him?
notes: Will not . . . speak with him] 4. Buckingham ‘made earnest sute to haue come into the presence of the same king Richard: which sute if he might haue obtiened, he hauing a knife secretlie about him, would have thrust it into the bodie of king Richard, as he had made semblance to kneele downe before him.’—Holinshed: Henry VIII. iii, 864. Boswell-Stone, p. 439. Steevens quotes (under the present line) the passage from Hen. VIII: I, ii, 194–199, which is based upon the foregoing account in Holinshed.—Ed.

3.play: Sheriff: No, my good lord; therefore be patient.

4.play: BUCKINGHAM: Hastings, and Edward's children, Rivers, Grey,

5.play: Holy King Henry, and thy fair son Edward,
notes: Holy King Henry] 7. DOUCE (ii, 38): This epithet is not applied without good reason, King Henry the Sixth, though never actually canonised, was regarded as a saint, and miracles were supposed to have been performed by him. In some of our church service books before the Reformation there are prayers which are said to have been of his composition, and one in particular, that is addressed to him is entitled: ‘A prayer to holy king Henry.’—WRIGHT, after quoting a portion of the foregoing note by Douce, adds: ‘Henry VII. found the expense of canonisation too great.’

6.play: Vaughan, and all that have miscarried
notes: miscarried] 8. SCHMIDT (Lex. s. v. e.) gives many examples of ‘miscarry’ used in the sense to die, to perish; compare I, iii, 20.

7.play: By underhand corrupted foul injustice,

8.play: If that your moody discontented souls

9.play: Do through the clouds behold this present hour,

10.play: Even for revenge mock my destruction!
notes: for revenge mock my destruction] 12. VAUGHAN (iii, 136): This is a strange prayer, both as regards those invoked and the speaker of it himself. The thought of ‘holy King Henry’ mocking a sufferer from the clouds is incongruous. Nor is such a request of such a sufferer to be mocked in his last agonies verisimilar. Neither had Buckingham any hand in King Henry’s or his fair son’s deaths. He had refused consent to those of Edward’s children. Why then ‘revenge’? Why even ‘for revenge’? For what else could it be, but for revenge, if they mocked at all? The poet wrote: ‘—for revenge mark my destruction.’ That the victim should in his solitude, invoke spirits to mark that they might testify to the cruel end inflicted on him, is most natural; and that he should add ‘even for revenge,’ particularly if he meant revenge on Richard, is natural. But that excludes ‘mock’ and admits mark.

11.play: This is All-Souls' day, fellows, is it not?
notes: fellows] 13. DYCE: In the Qq the word fellows is addressed by the Duke to the Guard generally. It seems rather odd that, in the Folio, Buckingham should

12.play: Sheriff: It is, my lord.

13.play: BUCKINGHAM: Why, then All-Souls' day is my body's doomsday.

14.play: This is the day that, in King Edward's time,
notes: This is the day] 16. See II, i, 38-46.

15.play: I wish't might fall on me, when I was found

16.play: False to his children or his wife's allies

17.play: This is the day wherein I wish'd to fall

18.play: By the false faith of him I trusted most;

19.play: This, this All-Souls' day to my fearful soul

20.play: Is the determined respite of my wrongs:
notes: the determined respite of my wrongs] 22. WRIGHT: That is, the fixed period to which the punishment of my wrong-doing is postponed.

respite] 22. SKBAT (Did. s. v.) quotes Cotgrave, ‘respit, a respit, a delay, a time or term of forbearance; a protection of one, three, or five years granted by the prince unto a debtor.’ He then adds: ‘The true original sense is regard, respect had to suit on the part of a prince or judge, and it is mere doublet of respect.’
wrongs] 22. JOHNSON: ‘Wrongs’ here means, wrongs done, or injurious practices.

21.play: That high All-Seer that I dallied with

22.play: Hath turn'd my feigned prayer on my head

23.play: And given in earnest what I begg'd in jest.

24.play: Thus doth he force the swords of wicked men

25.play: To turn their own points on their masters' bosoms:

26.play: Now Margaret's curse is fallen upon my head;
notes: Margarets curse] 28. See I, iii, 313-315.

27.play: 'When he,' quoth she, 'shall split thy heart with sorrow,

28.play: Remember Margaret was a prophetess.'

29.play: Come, sirs, convey me to the block of shame;

30.play: Wrong hath but wrong, and blame the due of blame.

31.play: [Exeunt]

1.play: [Enter RICHMOND, OXFORD, BLUNT, HERBERT, and others, with drum and colours]

2.play: RICHMOND: Fellows in arms, and my most loving friends,

3.play: Bruised underneath the yoke of tyranny,

4.play: Thus far into the bowels of the land

5.play: Have we march'd on without impediment;

6.play: And here receive we from our father Stanley

7.play: Lines of fair comfort and encouragement.

8.play: The wretched, bloody, and usurping boar,
notes: wretched] 10. WALKER (Crit., iii, 175) marks this word as ‘palpably wrong’; but offers no emendation.—DYCE (Gloss., s.v. wretched). That is, vile, hateful, utterly bad. ‘A wretched fellow, Deplorate malus.’—Coles’s Lat. and Eng. Dict.—COLLIER (Notes and Emend., p. 335): ‘Wretched’ is an epithet that has little comparative appropriateness, while the word recommended in manuscript [reckless] is especially adapted to the character of Richard, and we may readily believe it to have been that of the poet. Reckless was of old frequently spelt wrecless, and hence, perhaps, the misprint.—SINGER (Sh. Vind., 176): That ‘wretched’ is the word of the poet, and therefore the most ‘appropriate’ and undoubted, will appear from the use of it by Roderigo in Othello, v, i, 41, when he receives his death-wound and exclaims: ‘Oh wretched villain.’—KINNEAR (p. 271): This [defence by Singer] is a misapprehension. Roderigo exclaims: ‘O villain that I am,’ V, i, 29, and later ‘O wretched villain’—the dying confessions of his own guilt, and that his miserable end is deserved: this was before Iago stabbed him; he was only wounded by Cassio,

9.play: That spoil'd your summer fields and fruitful vines,

10.play: Swills your warm blood like wash, and makes his trough

11.play: In your embowell'd bosoms, this foul swine
notes: embowell'd] 13. JOHNSON: That is, exenterated, ripped up, alluding, perhaps, to the Promethean vulture: or, more probably, to the sentence pronounced in the English courts against traitors by which they are condemned to be hanged, drawn, that is, ‘embowelled,’ and quartered. [Both TOLLET and BLACKSTONE find fault with Johnson’s use of this word drawn: Tollet, on the ground that: ‘Drawn in the sentence pronounced upon traitors only signifies to be drawn by the heels or on a hurdle from the prison to the place of execution.’ Blackstone objects, on the ground that ‘Drawn in the sense of ‘embowelled’ is never used but in speaking of a fowl.’ MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. Draw III, 50): ‘To draw out the viscera or intestines of; to disembowel (a fowl, etc., before cooking, a traitor or other criminal after hanging.’ A note is added: ‘In many cases of executions it is uncertain whether this or sense 4 [namely: to drag (a criminal) at a horse’s tail to the place of execution] is meant. The presumption is that where drawn is mentioned after hanged, the sense is as here,’ i. e., embowelled.—Ed.]

12.play: Lies now even in the centre of this isle,
notes: Lies] 14. WRIGHT: The Quarto reading lies, suits better with the figure of the boar.

centre] 14. This is, possibly, not a mere misprint for centre; MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. Centry, 1.) quotes, beside the present line, one other example of this word: ‘This country is scituate as it were in the centrie or midst of others.—Stubbs, 1583, Anatomy of Abuses, II, 5.’ It is, perhaps, noteworthy that in both passages it is used to designate a geographical position.—Ed.

13.play: Near to the town of Leicester, as we learn

14.play: From Tamworth thither is but one day's march.

15.play: In God's name, cheerly on, courageous friends,

16.play: To reap the harvest of perpetual peace

17.play: By this one bloody trial of sharp war.

18.play: OXFORD: Every man's conscience is a thousand swords,

19.play: To fight against that bloody homicide.

20.play: HERBERT: I doubt not but his friends will fly to us.

21.play: BLUNT: He hath no friends but who are friends for fear.

22.play: Which in his greatest need will shrink from him.
notes: greatest] 24. For examples of 'dear' used in the sense of extreme, urgent, see Shakespeare passim.

23.play: RICHMOND: All for our vantage. Then, in God's name, march:

24.play: True hope is swift, and flies with swallow's wings:

25.play: Kings it makes gods, and meaner creatures kings.

26.play: [Exeunt]

1.play: [Enter KING RICHARD III in arms, with NORFOLK, SURREY, and others]
notes: Earle of Surrey] 1. See Daniel on Text, in Appendix.

2.play: KING RICHARD III: Here pitch our tents, even here in Bosworth field.

3.play: My Lord of Surrey, why look you so sad?
notes: My Lord of Surrey] 3. Capell (ii, 190): For a good entrance upon their critical office in it, by two of the moderns [Pope and Hanmer], the second line of the scene has a change in their copies, fetch’d from Quarto’s, which debases Richard’s majesty something, in making him address a dependant too familiarly: the motive seems to have been, to purge the play of a character that has but one single line in it, a motive most insufficient: nor have they effected their purpose if that were it, for the character stands in their Dramatis Personae.

4.play: SURREY: My heart is ten times lighter than my looks.

5.play: KING RICHARD III: My Lord of Norfolk,--

6.play: NORFOLK: Here, most gracious liege.

7.play: KING RICHARD III: Norfolk, we must have knocks; ha! must we not?

8.play: NORFOLK: We must both give and take, my gracious lord.

9.play: KING RICHARD III: Up with my tent there! here will I lie tonight;
notes: Tent] 9. Dyce: The Qq here have tents, and rightly, it would seem; Richard speaking of the tents for himself and his officers. Presently he speaks of his own particular tent; ‘Up with my tent,’ l. ii, and ‘Up with the tent,’ l. 18.

10.play: But where to-morrow?  Well, all's one for that.

11.play: Who hath descried the number of the foe?
notes: descried] 11. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. iii): 6. To catch sight of, especially from a distance, as the scout or watchman who is ready to announce the enemy’s approach; to espy.

12.play: NORFOLK: Six or seven thousand is their utmost power.

13.play: KING RICHARD III: Why, our battalion trebles that account:
notes: Battalia] 13. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.): 2. A large body of men in battle array, a marshalled force or host, whether constituting the whole of an army, or one of its great divisions or battalions. [The present line quoted as the earliest use of this word.]—PICKERSGILL (Sh. Soc. Trans., 1875–’76; p. 113): If this word is the plural, can any one suppose that Shakespeare would have used this pedantic form [see Text. Notes]? If any one can, let him refer to Hamlet, IV, v, 78, where he will find that, as a matter of fact, Shakespeare preferred the plural in s: ‘When sorrows come, they come not single spies, But in battalions.’—WRIGHT: In Hamlet, IV, v, 79, F1 has: ‘But in Battailes.’ [From the fact that ‘battalia’ is here used with the verb in the singular, and Wright’s reference to Hamlet, may we not hazard the conjecture that Shakespeare thought ‘battalia’ was the singular form of the word?—Ed.]

trebles that account] 13. MALONE: Richmond’s forces are said to have been only five thousand; and Richard’s army consisted of about twelve thousand men. But Lord Stanley lay at a small distance with three thousand men, and Richard may be supposed to have reckoned on them as his friends, though the event proved otherwise.—CHURCHILL (p. 514): In The True Tragedy, Richmond asks Stanley: ‘What number do you thinke the kings power to be?’ to which Stanley replies: ‘Mary some twentie thousand . . . Richmond. And we hardly fiue thousand’ etc . . . . The passages may well be entirely independent of

14.play: Besides, the king's name is a tower of strength,
notes: the Kings name is a Tower of strength] 14. DOUCE (ii, 39): Borrowed from Proverbs xviii, 10: ‘The name of the Lord is a strong tower.’

15.play: Which they upon the adverse party want.

16.play: Up with my tent there! Valiant gentlemen,

17.play: Let us survey the vantage of the field

18.play: Call for some men of sound direction
notes: sound direction] 18. JOHNSON: That is, true judgment, sound military skill.

19.play: Let's want no discipline, make no delay,

20.play: For, lords, to-morrow is a busy day.

21.play: [Exeunt]

22.play: [Enter, on the other side of the field, RICHMOND,  Sir William Brandon, OXFORD, and others. Some of the Soldiers pitch RICHMOND's tent]
notes: Enter Richmond] 22. R. G. WHITE: It should be remembered that the field was represented by a platform about as large as the floor of a drawing-room in a modern full-sized house. The representatives of Richard and Richmond were actually within easy conversational distance of each other, and could almost have shaken hands; and the tents, of course, occupied the same relative positions. Such were the arrangements of our primitive stage. We now, by the aid of scene-painters and carpenters, and at the sound of the prompter’s whistle, separate the representatives of York and Lancaster by certain yards of coloured canvas, and our stage ghosts address themselves to Richard only; and there are those who, forgetting that the stage does not, never can, and should not if it could, represent the facts of real life, think that we have gained greatly by the change.—MARSHALL: Grant White’s note seems to us a very foolish one. Certainly the effect of the modern stage arrangement is that the ghosts ‘address themselves to Richard only’; but we believe Shakespeare would have been the first to recognise the fact that the dramatic force of the situation is thereby increased, and that his poetry only suffered by being spoken amid surroundings which distracted, by their ridiculous incongruity, the minds of the audience from the language of the characters, and the incidents represented. It would be just as sensible to regret the time when perspective was unknown, and when painters necessarily represented objects, whether near or distant, in the same plane, as to affect to sigh over the times when the want of any stage machinery prevented the dramatist from having appropriate scenes for the action of his play.

Dorset] 22. MALONE: This is one of the numerous proofs that many of the alterations in F1 were made by the players and not by Shakespeare [see Text. Notes]; for Shakespeare had been informed by Holinshed that Dorset was not at the battle of Bosworth: Richmond before his leaving Paris having borrowed a sum of money from the French King, Charles VIII., and having left the Marquis of Dorset and Sir John Bourchier as hostages for the payment.—DANIEL (Introd., p. xix) after giving the substance of the foregoing note by MALONE, adds: ‘I should suppose Dorset’s name to be struck out when the Qto was prepared. All these variations, it seems to me, can only be regarded as alterations of the Folio version, and therefore, till evidence to the contrary is forthcoming, all the other textual variations in the two versions must be set down to the same cause, due allowance being, of course, made for error and corruption.’ [See note by Daniel, III, i, 209.]

23.play: RICHMOND: The weary sun hath made a golden set,

24.play: And by the bright track of his fiery car,
notes: Tract] 24. SKEAT (Dict. s. v. Track): Confused with ‘tract’ in old authors; also with trace, both in old and modern authors. Minsheu has: ‘A trace or tracke; Cotgrave explains French trac by ‘a track, tract, or trace.’ In Rich. II: III, iii, 66 [and also in the present line] the F1 have ‘tract’ for track; and in Timon, I, i, 50, the word ‘tract’ is used in the sense of trace. These words require peculiar care, because trace and ‘tract’ are really connected, but track is not of Latin origin at all, and therefore quite distinct from the other two words. From French trac, ‘a track, tract, or trace, a beaten way or path, a trade or course.’ The sense of ‘beaten track’ is the right one: we still use that very phrase.

25.play: Gives signal, of a goodly day to-morrow.

26.play: Sir William Brandon, you shall bear my standard.

27.play: Give me some ink and paper in my tent

28.play: I'll draw the form and model of our battle,

29.play: Limit each leader to his several charge,
notes: Limit] 29. That is, assign, appoint.

30.play: And part in just proportion our small strength.

31.play: My Lord of Oxford, you, Sir William Brandon,

32.play: And you, Sir Walter Herbert, stay with me.

33.play: The Earl of Pembroke keeps his regiment:
notes: Earle of Pembroke] 33. See IV, iv, 16.
keepes] 33. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. II. 33): To stay or remain in, on, or at (a place); not to leave; especially in, to keep one's bed; to keep the house.

Regiment] 33. R. G. WHITE: 'Regiment' was used in Shakespeare's time to mean any considerable body of men, under the regiment or command of one leader, and without reference to the number or organization of the troops that composed it.—CRAIGIE (N. E. D. s. v.): A considerable body of troops, more or less permanently organized under the command of a superior officer, and forming a definite unit of an army or military force; since the seventeenth century the specific name of the largest permanent unit of the cavalry, infantry, and foot-guards of the British Army.

34.play: Good Captain Blunt, bear my good night to him

35.play: And by the second hour in the morning

36.play: Desire the earl to see me in my tent:

37.play: Yet one thing more, good Blunt, before thou go'st,

38.play: Where is Lord Stanley quarter'd, dost thou know?

39.play: BLUNT: Unless I have mista'en his colours much,

40.play: Which well I am assured I have not done,

41.play: His regiment lies half a mile at least

42.play: South from the mighty power of the king.

43.play: RICHMOND: If without peril it be possible,

44.play: Good Captain Blunt, bear my good-night to him,
notes: Sweet Blunt] 44. R. G. WHITE: This affords a marked instance of the warm

45.play: And give him from me this most needful scroll.

46.play: BLUNT: Upon my life, my lord, I'll under-take it;

47.play: And so, God give you quiet rest to-night!

48.play: RICHMOND: Good night, good Captain Blunt. Come gentlemen,

49.play: Let us consult upon to-morrow's business

50.play: In to our tent; the air is raw and cold.
notes: the Dew is rawe and cold] 50. R. G. WHITE: To well-informed readers the reading of the Qq [aire] may seem preferable on account of its conformity to the facts of nature; but such conformity is not to be sought against authority, and in disregard of the belief of Shakespeare's time. Even now-a-days there are many people that would be offended if they were called ignorant, and who consider that the danger of exposure to the night-air is caused by the falling dews.

52.play: [Enter, to his tent, KING RICHARD III, NORFOLK, RATCLIFF, CATESBY, and others]

53.play: KING RICHARD III: What is't o'clock?

notes: Supper time . . . it's nine a clocke] 54. STEEVENS: I think we ought to read—six instead of ‘nine.’ A supper at so late an hour as nine o’clock, in the year 1485, would have been a prodigy.—DOUCE (ii, 39): It certainly would, and even at the time when this play was written, the period to which the criticism more justly belongs. In either instance there was a reason for preferring the text of the Qq, and yet the unnecessary alteration is retained.—VERPLANCK: Harrison tells us

56.play: KING RICHARD III: I will not sup to-night.

57.play: Give me some ink and paper.
notes: Give me some Ink and Paper] 57. Capell (ii, 190): Words proceeding from printers in all likelihood, from having their eye caught by line opposite: useless they are, undoubtedly, to say no more of them; for the matters spoke of in them are enquir’d for afterwards, in fitter place, and with fitter expressions.

58.play: What, is my beaver easier than it was?
notes: Beaver] 58. Murray (N. E. D. s. v. 2): The lower portion of the face-guard of a helmet, when worn with a visor; but occasionally serving the purpose of both [derived from Old French baviere, a child’s bib, from bave, saliva]. ‘In the fourteenth century applied to the moveable face-guard of the basinet, otherwise called visiere, ventaille, or aventaile. In the early part of the fifteenth century the beaver appears formed of overlapping plates, which can be raised or depressed to any degree desired by the wearer. In the sixteenth century it again became confounded with the visor, and could be pushed up entirely over the top of the helmet, and drawn down at pleasure.’—Planché.

59.play: And all my armour laid into my tent?

60.play: CATESBY: If is, my liege; and all things are in readiness.

61.play: KING RICHARD III: Good Norfolk, hie thee to thy charge;

62.play: Use careful watch, choose trusty sentinels.

64.play: KING RICHARD III: Stir with the lark to-morrow, gentle Norfolk.

65.play: NORFOLK: I warrant you, my lord.

69.play: KING RICHARD III: Send out a pursuivant at arms

70.play: To Stanley's regiment; bid him bring his power

71.play: Before sunrising, lest his son George fall

72.play: Into the blind cave of eternal night.
notes: blinde] 72. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. III): 6. Enveloped in darkness; dark, obscure.

74.play: Fill me a bowl of wine. Give me a watch.
notes: Giue me a Watch] 74. JOHNSON: A ‘watch’ has many significations, but I

75.play: Saddle white Surrey for the field to-morrow.
notes: white Surrey] 75. ‘Richard, (uninored with his gard) with a frowning countenance and cruell visage, mounted on a great white courser, . . . entered the towne of Leicester after the sunne set.’—Holinshed.—W. Strunk, Jr. (MS note): White steeds have sacred associations (Tacitus, Germania, ch. 10), are reserved for royalty, and are the best of three colors in times of need: see Lady Maisry (Child, iii, p. 116), and Fair Mary of Livingston (Ibid., 20-22). Tam Lin, a favourite of the queen, too, rides, not the black nor the brown, but, a milk-white steed: 27-28. ‘Dem Pabst is gesetzt’ ran an old regulation, ‘dass er reyte auf einem blanckem Pferde.’ For a deeper glimpse, see Hehn: Kulturpflanzen und Haustiere, pp. 44 f., 478.—Gummere, Old English Ballads, p. 351, note.
Surrey] 75. Brewer (Dict. of Phrase, &c., s. v.): Surrey is the Syrian horse, as Roan Barbary in Richard II. is the Barbary horse or barb.

76.play: Look that my staves be sound, and not too heavy.
notes: Staves] 76. Johnson: That is, the wood of the lances.—Steevens: As it was usual to carry more lances than one into the field, the lightness of them was an object of some consequence. Hall says that, at the marriage of Mary Tudor with the King of France, ‘a gentleman called Anthony Bowmarne which came into the feld all armed, and on his body brought in sight x. speres, that is to wyt. iii. speres set in every styroppe forward, and vnder euery thygh. ii. speres vpwarde, and vnder his lefte arme was on spere backward, and the. x. in his hand.’ [—p. 573. ed. 1809.]

79.play: KING RICHARD III: Saw'st thou the melancholy Lord Northumberland?
notes: melancholy Lord [Northumberland] 79. Malone: Richard thus calls him

80.play: RATCLIFF: Thomas the Earl of Surrey, and himself,

81.play: Much about cock-shut time, from troop to troop
notes: Cockshut time] 81. MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v.). 1. Twilight. Perhaps the time when poultry go to roost, and are shut up; though some think it is equivalent to cockshood, and refers to the time when wood-cocks ‘shoot’ or fly.—Ibid. (s. v. cockshoot. A broad way, or glade in a wood, through which woodcocks, etc., might dart or ‘shoot,’ so as to be caught by nets stretched across the opening. The statements that the net itself was the cockshod, and that the proper spelling is cockshut, appear to be dictionary blunders, founded on a misunderstanding of the word as something to ‘shut’ in or enclose the birds. [Whatever be the derivation, it is evident that ‘cockshut time’ here means twilight.—ED.]

82.play: Went through the army, cheering up the soldiers.

83.play: KING RICHARD III: So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine:
notes: So, I am . . . Bowle of Wine] 83. MALONE: In speeches of this description, where minute orders are given about trifles, the poet appears to have paid little attention to metre, and to have interposed sentences of mere prose, of which I have given several examples in the course of this tragedy. [That Shakespeare again and again has disjointed lines we all know; but that they are ‘mere prose’ is not so evident. They are not invariably, but very frequently, disjointed rhythmic prose, as here: ‘So I | am sat | isfied,’ Then there is a pause, a mora vacua, and there follows: ‘Give me | a bowl | of wine.’—ED.]

84.play: I have not that alacrity of spirit,
notes: I haue not that Alacrity of Spirit] 84. ‘—he pronosticated before the doubtfull chaunce of the battayle to come, not vsing the cheerfulness and mirth of minde, and of countenance as he was accustomed to doe before he came towarde the battayle.’—Grafton, ap. More, p. 219. This depression on the part of Richard is, however, mentioned as following, not preceding, his ‘dreadfull’ and ‘terrible dreame.’ BOOTH (p. 94) gives, after this line, the following stage-direction: ‘The drum-roll and trumpets cease gradually: tremulo music pianissimo begins and is kept up through the dream. After a few vivid flashes of light the scene becomes illuminated and shows the ghosts and the distant tents of Richmond.’—ED.

85.play: Nor cheer of mind, that I was wont to have.

86.play: Set it down. Is ink and paper ready?

88.play: KING RICHARD III: Bid my guard watch; leave me.
notes: Bid my Guard watch] 88. ROLFE: If this is not the order for the guard, it is a message to the guard that would be set at the royal tent, as a matter of course, admonishing them to be vigilant.

89.play: Ratcliff, about the mid of night come to my tent

90.play: And help to arm me. Leave me, I say.
notes: Leave me I say] 90. HAZLITT (Sh. Char., p. 150): Kean's manner of bidding his friends ‘Good night,’ after pausing with the point of his sword, drawn slowly backward and forward on the ground, as if considering the plan of the battle next day, is a particularly happy and natural thought.

92.play: [Enter DERBY to RICHMOND in his tent, Lords and others attending]

93.play: DERBY: Fortune and victory sit on thy helm!

94.play: RICHMOND: All comfort that the dark night can afford

95.play: Be to thy person, noble father-in-law!
notes: Father in Law] 95. FRENCH (p. 230): Shakespeare, who like other writers was not particular in applying correct terms of relationship, makes Richmond speak of Stanley as his ‘father-in-law,’ whereas he was step-son to him, and became son-in-law to Edward IV. at his marriage with Elizabeth of York. [Shakespeare is, however, in this respect no more careless than many writers both of his time and later. According to MURRAY (N. E. D. s. v. father-in-law), this term was used equally to designate the father of a husband or wife, and the relationship resulting from marriage of a mother, known as step-father. Compare, for example, in the present play, where Clarence speaks of Warwick as his ‘great father-in-law’ (I, iv, 50). ‘The step- in stepson and the like is the adjective steop, “destitute,” “bereaved,” so that stepson or stepchild is the same as orphan, which comes from the Greek for “bereaved.” Stepfather and stepmother are therefore terms which could only have arisen after the step- had lost its proper sense.’—GREENOUGH & KITTRIDGE (p. 211).—ED.]

96.play: Tell me, how fares our loving mother?
notes: Tell me . . . Noble Mother] 96. CHURCHILL (p. 514): In The True Tragedy, ‘Enters a Messenger’ from the ‘mother queene’ with a letter from Richmond’s mother, Margaret. Richmond’s first words are: ‘Welcome my friend, how fares our mother & the rest?’ Is it mere chance that makes Richmond's greeting in Richard III: ‘Tell me,’ etc.? There is nothing in the chronicle story to hint at this solicitude on Richmond’s part for his mother.

97.play: DERBY: I, by attorney, bless thee from thy mother

98.play: Who prays continually for Richmond's good:

99.play: So much for that. The silent hours steal on,
notes: The silent houres steale on] 99. The COWDEN-CLARKES: This is one of Shakespeare’s expedients for marking dramatic time. The present allusion to approaching dawn, just between Richard’s late words: ‘about the mid of night,’ and Richmond’s subsequent words: ‘lest leaden slumber peize me down tomorrow’ serves to prepare the spectator’s imagination for the supposed passing of the period, during this scene, from sunset to sunrise.

100.play: And flaky darkness breaks within the east.
notes: flakie darkenesse breakes within the East] 100. MARSHALL: The accuracy of this description will be recognised by any one who has ever watched the break of dawn, in the country, long before the sun rises above the horizon after a starless night. The mass of darkness begins to break into irregular pieces shaped like snow-flakes.—[It is, perhaps, interesting to compare other descriptions of sunrise, by Shakespeare: ‘look, the gentle day Before the wheels of Phoebus round about Dapples the drowsy east with spots of grey.’—Much Ado, V, iii, 25; ‘The grey-eyed morn smiles on the frowning night, Chequering the eastern clouds with streaks of light, And flecked [Qq.] darkness like a drunkard reels From forth day’s path and Titan’s fiery wheels.’—Rom. & Jul., II, iii, 7; ‘look, love, what envious streaks Do lace the severing clouds in yonder east: Night’s candles are burnt out, and jocund day Stands tiptoe on the misty mountain tops.’—Ibid., III, v, 7; ‘yon gray lines That fret the clouds are messengers of day.’—Jul. Cas., II, i, 103; ‘look, the morn, in russet mantle clad, Walks o’er the dew of yon high eastward hill.’—Hamlet, I, i, 166; ‘Full many a glorious morning have I seen Flatter the mountain tops with sovereign eye, Kissing with golden face the meadows green, Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy; Anon permit the basest clouds to ride With ugly rack on his celestial face, And from the forlorn world his visage hide, Stealing unseen to west with this disgrace.’—Sonnet, xxxiii. ‘The sun ariseth in his majesty; Who doth the world so gloriously behold That cedar-tops and hills seem burnish’d gold.’—Ven. & Ad., l 856.—ED.]

101.play: In brief,--for so the season bids us be,--

102.play: Prepare thy battle early in the morning,

103.play: And put thy fortune to the arbitrement

104.play: Of bloody strokes and mortal-staring war.
notes: mortall staring] 104. STEEVENS: I suppose by this is meant: war that looks big or stares fatally on its victims.—The COWDEN-CLARKES: ‘Mortal-staring war’ includes the effect of War staring or glaring fatally upon its victims, and of their deadly stare when killed.

105.play: I, as I may--that which I would I cannot,--

106.play: With best advantage will deceive the time,
notes: With best advantage will deceiue the time] 106. JOHNSON: I will take the best opportunity to elude the dangers of this conjuncture.—VAUGHAN (iii, 146): Lady Macbeth says to her husband: ‘To beguile the time Look like the time.’—I, v, 64; that is: to deceive everybody about you, look as everybody about you looks. The expression here ‘deceive the time,’ then is, possibly: I will, on the first opportunity, trick those who are about me, and give you my help.

107.play: And aid thee in this doubtful shock of arms:

108.play: But on thy side I may not be too forward

109.play: Lest, being seen, thy brother, tender George,
notes: tender George] 109. WRIGHT: ‘Tender George’ was at this time a married man, and had been summoned to Parliament since 1482. But the dramatist followed the chroniclers; for Hall, and Holinshed after him, both speak of Lord Strange as a child.

110.play: Be executed in his father's sight.

111.play: Farewell: the leisure and the fearful time

112.play: Cuts off the ceremonious vows of love

113.play: And ample interchange of sweet discourse,

114.play: Which so long sunder'd friends should dwell upon:

115.play: God give us leisure for these rites of love!

116.play: Once more, adieu: be valiant, and speed well!

117.play: RICHMOND: Good lords, conduct him to his regiment:

118.play: I'll strive, with troubled thoughts, to take a nap,
notes: He strike with troubled noise] 118. R. G. WHITE: The reading of the Qq has

119.play: Lest leaden slumber peise me down to-morrow,
notes: leaden slumber] 119. MALONE compares: ‘Now leaden slumber with life’s strength doth fight.’—Rape of Luc., l. 124; and WRIGHT adds: ‘O murderous slumber Lay’st thou thy leaden mace upon my boy?’—Jul. Caes., IV, iii, 268.
